name: Home
title: Home
description: Home page that loads dynamic portfolio content, highlights featured artworks, shop categories, and exhibitions, and routes visitors to key sections such as Gallery, Shop, Commissions, Projects, About, and Newsletter.

core_features: Load all home page content via the getHomePageContent interface on initial page render.
Display a hero area using dynamic headline, subheadline, and background image, with navigation actions to Gallery, Shop categories, Commissions, Projects, About, and Newsletter.
Display a featured artworks section using Artwork entities provided in featured_artworks, including size and price labels and read-only indicators for favorites and wishlist membership.
Provide a clear navigation control from the featured artworks section to the Gallery page.
Display shop highlights based on shop_highlights, and route to the Shop page with the appropriate categoryId URL parameter when a highlight is activated.
Display featured exhibitions using Exhibition entities from featured_exhibitions, including date range and location labels and read-only visit-plan status.
Allow users to open an exhibition detail page by clicking a featured exhibition, passing exhibitionId as a URL parameter.
Display an inline newsletter call-to-action using newsletter_cta content, and route to the newsletter flow with source=inline_home as a URL parameter.
Expose global navigation to the main sections: Home, Gallery, Shop (Prints and Originals), Commissions, Projects, About, and Newsletter, using URL-based navigation only.
user_workflows:
Browse featured artworks and open the gallery:
1) User navigates to index.html.
2) Page calls getHomePageContent and enters a loading state.
3) After successful response, featured_artworks are rendered in the featured artworks component.
4) User scrolls to the featured artworks section and reviews artwork cards (image, title, category label, size label, price_display, read-only favorite/wishlist indicators).
5) User activates a gallery navigation control in this section (e.g., a button or link associated with the section).
6) Browser navigates to gallery.html (no additional URL parameters are required by this page).

Navigate to shop by category from home:
1) User navigates to index.html; getHomePageContent loads shop_highlights.
2) Shop highlights are rendered as cards for each shop_highlights item, bound to its shop_category, label, and description.
3) User selects a highlight representing a specific shop_category (for example, a highlight where shop_category is "prints").
4) Component constructs a URL to shop.html with the query parameter categoryId set to the selected shop_category value (e.g., shop.html?categoryId=prints).
5) Browser navigates to the generated Shop URL, where filtering is handled by the Shop page.

Discover exhibitions and view event details:
1) User navigates to index.html; getHomePageContent returns featured_exhibitions.
2) Featured exhibitions are rendered as a list or grid showing exhibition title, date_range_label, location_label, and a read-only indicator based on is_in_visit_plan.
3) User selects a particular exhibition card.
4) Component reads the exhibition.id of the selected item and constructs a URL exhibition_detail.html?exhibitionId=<exhibition.id>.
5) Browser navigates to the exhibition detail page, which will use the exhibitionId parameter to load full details.

Join the newsletter and access the lookbook via the newsletter flow:
1) User navigates to index.html; getHomePageContent returns newsletter_cta (title, body, button_label).
2) Newsletter CTA is rendered in a dedicated section using this data.
3) User activates the newsletter CTA button.
4) Component constructs a URL newsletter.html?source=inline_home.
5) Browser navigates to newsletter.html, where subscription and lookbook download handling occur using other page-specific interfaces.

Access commissions, projects, and about information from home:
1) User navigates to index.html and sees global navigation and/or hero actions.
2) User activates one of the navigation items:
   - Commissions: navigate to commissions.html.
   - Client projects / murals: navigate to projects.html.
   - About the artist: navigate to about.html.
3) The respective target page reads its own URL and loads its content; the Home page state is discarded on navigation.
interactions: On page load, the home page component invokes getHomePageContent (no parameters) and manages loading and error states until data is returned.
User clicks the site logo or the Home navigation item in the header to navigate to index.html (no special parameters needed).
User interacts with hero-section navigation actions to navigate to gallery.html, shop.html with categoryId, commissions.html, projects.html, about.html, or newsletter.html with source=inline_home.
User hovers or focuses on a featured artwork card to see basic artwork information (title, category_label, size_label, price_display) and read-only indicators for is_in_favorites and is_in_wishlist.
User clicks within the featured artworks section on a dedicated gallery navigation control to navigate to gallery.html.
User clicks a shop highlight card; the component reads the associated shop_category and navigates to shop.html with categoryId set to that value.
User clicks a featured exhibition card; the component reads exhibition.id from the bound data and navigates to exhibition_detail.html with exhibitionId set from that id.
User clicks the inline newsletter CTA button; the component navigates to newsletter.html with the source query parameter set to inline_home.
If getHomePageContent fails, the home page shows a functional error state (e.g., an error message and a retry control) without any hardcoded content values, and allows the user to retry loading.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Home page does not require or interpret any incoming URL parameters for its own rendering; it always calls getHomePageContent with no parameters on load. The page state is therefore determined entirely by the response of getHomePageContent and the user's interactions. URL parameters are used only when navigating away from Home to control the state of target pages:
- Navigating to the Shop page from a shop highlight or hero action uses a URL parameter categoryId. The component sets categoryId to the value of the selected shop_highlights[i].shop_category (e.g., 'prints' or 'originals'), resulting in a URL like shop.html?categoryId=<shop_category>. The Home page does not read this parameter; it only writes it into the outgoing URL.
- Navigating to an exhibition detail page from a featured exhibition card uses a URL parameter exhibitionId. The component extracts featured_exhibitions[i].exhibition.id and constructs exhibition_detail.html?exhibitionId=<id>. The Home page does not read exhibitionId; it only passes it along.
- Navigating to the newsletter page from the inline newsletter CTA sets the URL parameter source to 'inline_home', resulting in newsletter.html?source=inline_home. This parameter is fixed for this CTA to allow the newsletter page to track origin. The Home page does not conditionally behave based on this parameter.
- Navigating to the Shop page with categoryId from global navigation behaves the same as from shop highlights: the navigation handler constructs shop.html?categoryId=<chosenCategory> using only predefined category values understood by the Shop page.
The Home page does not store state in localStorage or similar mechanisms; all persistent or cross-page state (such as favorites, wishlists, visit plans, newsletter subscriptions) is reflected only through fields included in the getHomePageContent response (e.g., is_in_favorites, is_in_wishlist, is_in_visit_plan) and is treated as read-only on this page.

components.functionality: Invokes getHomePageContent on initial page load, manages loading and error states, and distributes the resulting data (hero, featured_artworks, shop_highlights, featured_exhibitions, newsletter_cta) to child components.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: call getHomePageContent() and store the resolved data in in-memory page state.
onLoadSuccess: render child components with the fetched data.
onLoadError: set an error flag and expose an error message to the UI without hardcoded content values.
onRetryClick: re-invoke getHomePageContent() when the user chooses to retry.

components.functionality: Provides persistent navigation controls (e.g., in a header) to access Home, Gallery, Shop categories, Commissions, Projects, About, and Newsletter pages using URL-based navigation only.
event_handlers: onClickNewsletter: navigate to newsletter.html (may optionally include source=inline_home or another configured source value).

components.functionality: Displays the main hero area using dynamic text and background image from getHomePageContent.hero, and exposes primary navigation actions to key sections (Gallery, Shop categories, Commissions, Projects, About, Newsletter).

Renders a section showcasing featured artworks from getHomePageContent.featured_artworks, showing each artwork's image, basic metadata, price display, and read-only indicators for favorites and wishlist membership. Provides a section-level control to navigate to the full Gallery.
event_handlers: onArtworkCardHover: show additional metadata for the hovered artwork (purely client-side, using already loaded data).
onArtworkCardFocus: for keyboard users, highlight the focused artwork card and expose metadata via accessible text.
onArtworkCardClick: navigate to gallery.html as a gallery-entry action (no additional parameters are required by this spec).
onViewGalleryClick: navigate to gallery.html to explore the full portfolio.

components.functionality: Displays a list of shop category highlights using getHomePageContent.shop_highlights and allows navigation to the Shop page filtered by the selected category using the categoryId URL parameter.
event_handlers: onShopHighlightClick: read the bound shop_category value for the clicked highlight and navigate to shop.html?categoryId=<shop_category> when the category is compatible with the Shop page (for example, 'prints' or 'originals').
onShopHighlightKeyActivate: support keyboard activation (e.g., Enter/Space) to trigger the same navigation logic as onShopHighlightClick.

components.functionality: Renders a list or grid of featured exhibitions from getHomePageContent.featured_exhibitions, showing title, date range, location label, and a read-only indicator of whether each exhibition is in the visitor's visit plan.
event_handlers: onExhibitionCardClick: read exhibition.id from the bound exhibition object and navigate to exhibition_detail.html?exhibitionId=<exhibition.id>.
onExhibitionCardKeyActivate: support keyboard activation (e.g., Enter/Space) to trigger the same navigation as onExhibitionCardClick.

components.functionality: Displays the inline newsletter call-to-action using newsletter_cta content from getHomePageContent and routes the user to the newsletter subscription page while tagging the origin using a URL parameter.
event_handlers: onNewsletterButtonKeyActivate: support keyboard activation to trigger the same navigation as onNewsletterButtonClick.

components.functionality: Provides secondary navigation (mirroring header actions where needed) and may include a minimal prompt linking to the newsletter page, without handling newsletter submission directly on this page.

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered fixed-width layout, likely based on a 12-column grid within a max-width container (~960–1140px), with a two-column split in the main about section (image column ~35–40%, text column ~60–65%).
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a strictly vertical, top-to-bottom structure within a centered, fixed-width container. At the very top, the hero section introduces the artist’s name and a short descriptor in a single-column, centered composition. Immediately beneath this, the primary navigation appears as a centered horizontal menu. After a generous vertical gap, the main content stack begins: featured artworks first, then shop highlights, followed by featured exhibitions. A newsletter inline CTA is placed near the bottom, just above the footer, acting as a soft final prompt. The home-content-loader sits above everything else as a temporary full-viewport overlay when data is loading.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a strictly vertical, top-to-bottom structure within a centered, fixed-width container. At the very top, the hero section introduces the artist’s name and a short descriptor in a single-column, centered composition. Immediately beneath this, the primary navigation appears as a centered horizontal menu. After a generous vertical gap, the main content stack begins: featured artworks first, then shop highlights, followed by featured exhibitions. A newsletter inline CTA is placed near the bottom, just above the footer, acting as a soft final prompt. The home-content-loader sits above everything else as a temporary full-viewport overlay when data is loading.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: All content sits inside a max-width container of roughly 960–1140px, centered on the page with wide left and right margins to preserve a gallery-like feel. The hero (name + descriptor) plus navigation collectively occupy about the top 25–30% of the initial viewport, surrounded by generous whitespace. Each subsequent section (featured artworks, shop, exhibitions) uses clear vertical separation of about 48–64px between sections. Within sections that use two columns, the left column generally occupies about 35–40% of the content width (often image- or list-focused) and the right column about 60–65% (text and detail-focused), echoing the portrait/bio layout from the mockup.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density remains low to moderate. The header and hero area are very spacious, with ample whitespace above, below, and between lines. Each major section starts with a lightly styled heading and minimal supporting text, followed by content grids or lists that have comfortable gaps (16–24px) between items. Text blocks use generous line spacing and are never pushed edge-to-edge; images and cards have internal padding around 16–24px. No section fills the viewport aggressively; instead, each reads like a curated spread in an editorial layout.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye is drawn first to the top center where the artist’s name in the hero section is prominent. From there, the eye moves straight down to the horizontal navigation menu, then continues downward into the main content stack. Within sections that adopt a two-column structure (featured artworks lead, exhibitions, newsletter CTA), the visual flow follows a subtle left-to-right pattern: attention first goes to the more visually distinct left column (image, date column, or text highlight), then moves right to read details or interact with controls. Across the page, the flow is predominantly vertical, with horizontal scans inside sections, maintaining an F-pattern reading path similar to the original mockup.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a calm, centered editorial layout within a wide, white canvas. A fixed-width content column, roughly 960–1140px wide, floats in the middle of the viewport with generous margins on both sides, giving everything a gallery-like airiness. At the very top, a spacious hero band introduces the artist’s name in thin, understated type, centered with plenty of space above and below, accompanied by a short descriptor line. Immediately underneath, a minimal primary navigation row stretches horizontally across the same centered column, its links evenly spaced and lightly styled. A noticeable vertical gap then transitions into the main content area, where the first section highlights featured artworks with a lead two-column band: a prominent artwork image on the left, with descriptive copy and controls on the right, followed by a light, evenly spaced grid of additional works. Further down, shop highlights are presented in a simple, airy band of product or category cards. Below that, featured exhibitions echo the about-page feel with another two-column composition: dates or locations on the left, exhibition details on the right. Near the bottom, a newsletter call-to-action appears as a softly framed card, centered within the column and slightly narrower than the main content, again using a left-right split between text and form. The page ends with a light, compact footer band that visually recedes. Throughout, large vertical intervals, restrained typography, and a consistent center-aligned framework keep the focus on the artwork and words, while a temporary full-viewport loader gently overlays the entire layout during initial data fetching.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active, the home-content-loader appears as a subtle, full-viewport overlay that sits on top of all other components. It covers the entire browser window width and height, from the very top edge to the bottom edge, with a slightly opaque white or very light gray background that still hints at the structure beneath. At the center of the viewport, both horizontally and vertically, a small loading indicator (such as a thin-line spinner and a single-line status message) is displayed. The loader aligns to the same central axis as the main content column, reinforcing the centered layout. Once data is loaded, the overlay fades out, revealing the hero section and navigation beneath without shifting their positions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed to the viewport, positioned above all content layers (high z-index), and only visible during loading states; does not scroll with the page because it covers the full viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The primary-navigation is positioned near the top of the page, directly below the hero-section’s main title area, inside the same centered max-width container. It sits on its own horizontal row, with its baseline roughly 96–128px from the top of the viewport once the hero heading is accounted for. The navigation bar itself does not stretch edge-to-edge; instead, it is constrained to the central column and spans approximately 60–70% of that width, centered. Navigation links are arranged in a single line, horizontally centered, with even spacing between items (about 24–32px). There is modest vertical padding above and below the links (around 16–24px), creating a light, airy strip with no heavy background color or borders, relying instead on whitespace for separation. A small vertical gap (about 16px) separates the navigation from the bottom of the hero content above and a larger gap (about 48–64px) separates it from the first main content section below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the document flow, scrolling away with the content to preserve a minimal, non-intrusive feel; no sticky or fixed behavior by default.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The hero-section occupies the very top of the content column and defines the header area of the page. It is laid out as a single, vertically stacked column inside the centered max-width container. From the top of the viewport, there is a generous top margin (approximately 64–96px) before the artist’s name appears in large, thin typography, centered horizontally within the column. The name spans roughly 50–60% of the container width, leaving ample whitespace to the left and right. Directly below the name, separated by about 16–24px, a single-line or short multi-line descriptor (such as a subtitle or tagline) appears, also centered, with more modest text size but generous line spacing. The entire hero block (name plus descriptor) together occupies around the top quarter of the viewport height on a typical desktop screen, dominated by whitespace above and below the text. The primary-navigation component is placed immediately beneath this hero block, creating the combined effect of a unified header area. A clear vertical gap of about 48–64px is left between the navigation bar and the start of the first main content section, marking a clean transition from the header/hero zone into the body of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the normal page flow, anchored to the top of the main content; no parallax or fixed behavior to maintain simplicity.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured-artworks-section is the first main content band beneath the header area (hero + navigation). It begins after a generous vertical gap of about 48–64px from the bottom of the navigation. Within the centered max-width container, the section opens with a two-column lead layout that mirrors the portrait/bio structure of the mockup. The left column takes up roughly 35–40% of the container width and is aligned to the top, containing a tall featured artwork image or a large artwork card. This image/card is framed with a subtle, thin border or soft shadow and has internal padding of about 16–24px, reinforcing the gallery aesthetic. The right column occupies the remaining 60–65% of the width and starts at the same vertical height as the top of the left image, ensuring a clear horizontal alignment. It contains the section heading, a short descriptive paragraph, and possibly simple filtering or sorting controls. Text in this column is arranged in a vertical stack with 16–24px gaps between elements, maintaining a light density. Below this lead two-column band, the section transitions into a clean artwork grid that spans the full container width: typically 3 columns on desktop, each card occupying about one-third of the width with 16–24px gaps both horizontally and vertically. Cards are vertically elongated with plenty of whitespace around each artwork image and caption. The section’s bottom edge leaves a vertical margin of about 48–64px before the next section begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; the two-column layout collapses into a single column on smaller screens, with the featured artwork image stacking above the text to preserve the original visual hierarchy.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shop-highlights-section is placed directly beneath the featured-artworks-section, starting after a vertical interval of about 48–64px. It remains within the same centered max-width container, but uses a slightly simpler structure to keep the focus primarily on artworks. At the top of this section, a centered heading and a brief descriptive line are stacked vertically in a single column, occupying about 50–60% of the container width and aligned to the central axis. Below the heading area, a row or light grid of shop highlight cards is laid out with a low-to-moderate density: typically 3 cards per row on desktop, each card about one-third of the container width with consistent 16–24px spacing between them. Each card has internal padding around 16px and a thin outline or subtle shadow to echo the framed-portfolio feel, but the overall visual weight stays light. Whitespace above the heading and below the last row of cards ensures the section reads as its own airy band. The final row of cards ends with about 48–64px of vertical spacing before the featured-exhibitions-section begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static grid behavior within the container; cards wrap to additional rows as needed and collapse into 1–2 columns on narrower screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured-exhibitions-section sits below the shop-highlights-section, separated by roughly 48–64px of vertical whitespace. Its layout intentionally echoes the original about-section two-column pattern to give exhibitions a more editorial presence. Inside the centered max-width container, the section is divided into two vertical columns. The left column occupies about 35–40% of the width and starts near the top of the section. It may display a vertical list of exhibition dates, locations, or a concise timeline, with each entry separated by 16px, creating a stacked, column-like rhythm. The right column occupies the remaining 60–65% and aligns its top edge horizontally with the first item in the left column. This right side hosts the section title at the top, followed by short descriptions of current or highlighted exhibitions, laid out in paragraphs or lightly divided cards. The text here uses generous line spacing and 16–24px vertical gaps between entries, giving the impression of an editorial column. The asymmetry—more text on the right, more compact list or visual markers on the left—guides the eye left-to-right similarly to how the portrait and bio interact in the mockup. The section ends with about 48–64px of vertical space before the newsletter inline CTA is introduced.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static two-column layout that gracefully stacks into a single column on smaller screens, with the left list or date column appearing above the detailed descriptions to preserve the reading order.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter-inline-cta is positioned near the bottom of the page, immediately below the featured-exhibitions-section and above the footer, with about 48–64px of whitespace on both sides to separate it from adjacent sections. It appears as a distinct but still understated card centered within the main container. The card itself is slightly narrower than the content width, spanning roughly 60–70% of the container and centered horizontally, with generous internal padding of 24–32px and a very subtle border or lightly tinted background to differentiate it from the white page. Inside the card, content is arranged in a gentle two-column layout: the left column (about 60% of the card width) contains the heading and one or two lines of explanatory text, aligned to the left but visually anchored to the overall page center; the right column (about 40%) holds the email input field and subscribe button, aligned vertically to the middle or top of the text. The columns sit side by side with ample breathing room, and elements within each column are separated by 8–16px gaps. The card itself leaves a clear bottom margin before the footer, allowing it to act as a soft, standalone focal point near the end of the scroll.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline block centered within the layout; on smaller screens, the two columns collapse into a single vertical stack, with text appearing above the input field and button.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The home-footer forms the final band at the bottom of the page. It spans the full browser width, but all content is constrained to the same centered max-width container as the rest of the page. A generous top margin of about 48–64px separates it from the newsletter CTA, maintaining the page’s airy vertical rhythm. The footer occupies a relatively shallow vertical height and keeps a very light visual footprint: a simple horizontal row or compact two-column arrangement inside the container. On the left side, small, understated text shows copyright or name information; on the right, secondary links (such as social icons or legal links) are aligned right but kept minimal and thin. Vertical padding within the footer band is around 24–32px, and there are no heavy backgrounds—either remaining white or using a very slight tint only if needed to subtly distinguish it. The overall effect is that the footer gently closes the page without competing for attention with the artwork-focused sections above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the bottom of the document; no sticky or fixed behavior, allowing the page to end quietly after the main content.

name: Gallery
title: Gallery
description: Browse all artworks in the portfolio with dynamic filters, sorting, and tools to save items to Favorites or Wishlist. The gallery state is fully driven by URL parameters so specific views can be shared and revisited.

core_features: Initialize gallery filters and sort options using getGalleryFilterOptions, including categories, year, price, size, color palette, and sorting criteria.

Read URL parameters (category, year_min, year_max, price_min, price_max, width_min_cm, width_max_cm, height_min_cm, height_max_cm, color_palette, sort, page) and construct the filter object for getGalleryArtworks.
Retrieve paginated artworks for the current filter/sort state using getGalleryArtworks and display them in a grid or list layout.
Display key artwork metadata in each artwork card (title, category label, year, size label, price display, and sale status), using the data returned from getGalleryArtworks.
Allow users to change filters (category, year range, price range, size range, color palette) and sorting, updating the URL parameters and reloading artworks from getGalleryArtworks.
Allow users to paginate through gallery results; pagination updates the page parameter and re-queries getGalleryArtworks.

Provide interactive controls on each artwork card to add an artwork to Favorites via addArtworkToFavorites and to Wishlist via addArtworkToWishlist, with immediate UI feedback using the returned counts and success flags.
Open a quick-view overlay for a selected artwork using getArtworkDetails, showing detailed information, sale options, and current favorites/wishlist status.
Show a small carousel or strip of related artworks inside the quick-view overlay using getRelatedArtworks for cross-navigation within the gallery context.
Navigate to product.html with a product id if the clicked artwork card represents a purchasable item that has a linked primary_product_id.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Landing with pre-filtered gallery from another page
1. User clicks a link to gallery.html from index.html or about.html, where the link includes one or more URL parameters (for example, category, year_min, price_max, color_palette, sort).
2. Gallery page controller reads the incoming URL parameters and merges them into an internal filter state.
3. Page calls getGalleryFilterOptions to obtain available filter ranges and option lists.
4. Page maps URL parameters onto the filter controls (select boxes, sliders, inputs) so the UI reflects the preselected filters.
5. Page calls getGalleryArtworks with the constructed filters, sort, and page (defaulting page to 1 if not present or invalid).
6. User sees only artworks matching the incoming criteria and can further adjust filters, sort, or pagination.
7. When the user modifies any filter or sort, the corresponding URL parameters are updated, page resets to 1, and getGalleryArtworks is called again with the new filters.

Workflow 2: Browsing and refining results within the gallery
1. User arrives on gallery.html without specific URL parameters or with minimal ones (e.g., only sort).
2. Page controller calls getGalleryFilterOptions to configure filters and then getGalleryArtworks with an unfiltered or minimally filtered state.
3. User opens the filter panel and changes one or more filters (e.g., selects a category, adjusts year and price ranges, changes color palette).
4. Each filter change updates the URL parameters (e.g., category, year_min, year_max, price_min, price_max, width_min_cm, width_max_cm, height_min_cm, height_max_cm, color_palette) and resets page to 1.
5. The controller re-calls getGalleryArtworks using the updated filter object and current sort, then refreshes the gallery listing.
6. User changes sorting via a sort control; the sort parameter in the URL changes, page resets to 1, and getGalleryArtworks is called again.
7. User navigates through pagination controls; each pagination click updates the page parameter and triggers a new getGalleryArtworks call with the same filters and sort but a different page.

Workflow 3: Quick view and related artworks
1. From the gallery listing, user clicks a dedicated quick-view action on an artwork card.
2. The page opens a modal overlay component that calls getArtworkDetails with the artwork id.
3. The overlay displays detailed information about the artwork, using the returned labels (category_label, medium_label, size_label, price_display) and flags (is_in_favorites, is_in_wishlist).
4. In parallel or after details load, the overlay calls getRelatedArtworks with the same artwork id.
5. The overlay shows a small list of related artworks that the user can click to update the overlay (making another getArtworkDetails and getRelatedArtworks call) or navigate back to the main grid by closing the overlay.
6. Actions in the overlay to add to Favorites or Wishlist call addArtworkToFavorites or addArtworkToWishlist, respectively; on success, the overlay updates its own state and signals the gallery listing to update that artwork card’s status.

Workflow 4: Saving artworks to Favorites and Wishlist from the gallery
1. User browses the gallery and sees artwork cards with favorites and wishlist buttons whose states reflect is_in_favorites and is_in_wishlist values returned by getGalleryArtworks.
2. User clicks the favorites button on an artwork card.
3. The page calls addArtworkToFavorites with the artwork id.
4. On success (success = true), the component updates the favorites button state for that artwork (e.g., active), updates any displayed favorites count using favorites_count, and may surface a transient confirmation message using message.
5. User clicks the wishlist button on the same or another artwork card.
6. The page calls addArtworkToWishlist with the artwork id and optionally an empty or user-provided notes value.
7. On success, the wishlist button reflects the saved state, and any wishlist count in the header or page is updated using wishlist_count.
8. If the user then clicks the Favorites or Wishlist header links, the browser navigates to favorites.html or wishlist.html without altering gallery state.

Workflow 5: Transitioning to product detail for purchasable items
1. User sees within an artwork card whether an artwork is for sale based on is_for_sale and the presence of a primary_product_id returned from getGalleryArtworks.
2. User clicks the main clickable area of the card (such as the image or title) of an artwork that is for sale and has a primary_product_id.
3. The page executes navigation to product.html with URL parameter id set to that primary_product_id (product.html?id=<primary_product_id>).
4. For artworks not for sale or without a linked product id, the card click opens the quick-view overlay instead of navigating away, using getArtworkDetails.
5. The user can use the browser back button from product.html to return to gallery.html with the original URL parameters, restoring the same gallery view.

Workflow 6: Sharing and revisiting a specific gallery view
1. User applies various filters and sorting and navigates to a particular page of results.
2. The current URL includes all relevant parameters (category, year_min, year_max, price_min, price_max, width_min_cm, width_max_cm, height_min_cm, height_max_cm, color_palette, sort, page).
3. User copies the URL from the browser and shares it with someone else or saves it for later.
4. When the gallery is opened via this URL, the page controller reads the parameters, sets the filter controls accordingly, and calls getGalleryFilterOptions followed by getGalleryArtworks.
5. The displayed results match the original view, including filters, sort order, and pagination, without relying on local storage or session data.
interactions: Changing any filter control (category selector, year range, price range, width/height range, color palette picker) updates the corresponding URL parameters, resets page to 1, and triggers a getGalleryArtworks request with the new filter object.
Changing the sort control updates the sort URL parameter, resets page to 1, and triggers a new getGalleryArtworks request with the same filters but different sort order.
Clicking pagination controls (next, previous, specific page number) updates the page parameter and triggers getGalleryArtworks with the current filters and sort for that page.

Clicking the favorites button on an artwork card calls addArtworkToFavorites; on success, the button switches to the active state, and any favorites counter or indicator is updated using favorites_count.
Clicking the wishlist button on an artwork card calls addArtworkToWishlist; on success, the button state and any wishlist counter are updated using wishlist_count.
Clicking the main clickable area of a card for an artwork with is_for_sale = true and a primary_product_id navigates the browser to product.html with id set to that primary_product_id.
Clicking the main clickable area of a card for an artwork without a linked primary_product_id opens the quick-view overlay, which then calls getArtworkDetails and displays more information.

Clicking a "quick view" or similar action on any artwork card opens the quick-view overlay regardless of sale status, using getArtworkDetails; within the overlay, clicking a related artwork triggers getArtworkDetails and getRelatedArtworks for that related artwork.
Clicking the site logo in the header navigates to index.html, leaving the current gallery URL and state behind.
Clicking the header Favorites or Wishlist link navigates to favorites.html or wishlist.html without changing the gallery URL.
Using the browser’s back and forward buttons moves through different gallery states, as each state is encoded in the URL parameters and re-resolved on each navigation.
page_functionality.state_logic: The gallery relies on URL query parameters as the single source of truth for filter, sort, and pagination state. On page load, a controller reads the current URL search parameters: category, year_min, year_max, price_min, price_max, width_min_cm, width_max_cm, height_min_cm, height_max_cm, color_palette, sort, and page. Each parameter is parsed and validated (for example, converting numeric strings to numbers; ignoring any invalid or out-of-range values). Missing parameters are treated as undefined, meaning the corresponding filter is not applied. The controller first calls getGalleryFilterOptions to retrieve metadata like valid year_range, price_range, size_filters, color_palettes, and sort_options. This metadata is used to bound and normalize the raw URL parameter values (for example, clamping year_min and year_max within the returned min_year and max_year if needed). The normalized parameters are then combined into a filters object and passed, along with sort and page, to getGalleryArtworks. The response from getGalleryArtworks (items, total_items, page, page_size) defines the gallery display and pagination. When a user modifies any filter or sort control, the UI updates only the corresponding URL parameters and removes parameters for filters that are cleared. After updating the URL (for example, via pushState or by reloading with new query parameters), the controller re-runs the same initialization logic: reading the URL, normalizing parameters against getGalleryFilterOptions, and calling getGalleryArtworks. Pagination interactions only change the page parameter, leaving filters and sort untouched. Because all state is encoded in the URL, no localStorage or other client-side persistence is required; reloading the page or sharing the URL reconstructs the same gallery, and browser navigation works naturally across different gallery states.

components.functionality: Orchestrates data loading and state synchronization for the Gallery page. On initialization and whenever the URL query string changes, it reads the URL parameters, calls getGalleryFilterOptions to obtain available filters, normalizes the URL values against that metadata, and then calls getGalleryArtworks with a constructed filters object, sort key, and page number. It distributes the resulting data (filter options, current filters, sort, pagination info, and artworks list) to child components.

Displays and manages filter controls for category, year range, price range, width/height range, and color palette. Initializes its controls using values from getGalleryFilterOptions combined with the current URL-derived filter state from the controller. When the user adjusts any filter, it emits an event with the updated filter values so the controller can update URL parameters and refresh getGalleryArtworks.

Controls sorting of artworks and optionally exposes a toggle between grid and list layout. It reads available sort options from getGalleryFilterOptions.sort_options and the currently selected sort key from URL-derived state. When the user selects a different sort option, it triggers an event so the controller can update the sort URL parameter and reset page to 1 before calling getGalleryArtworks. Any view-mode toggle managed here can be treated as transient UI state (not encoded in URL) unless the implementation chooses otherwise.

Displays summary information about the current gallery results, such as the total number of items and the current page, and may show a textual representation of active filters based on URL-derived state. It does not modify state but reacts to updated getGalleryArtworks results and the current filter/sort state.

Renders the list of artworks returned by getGalleryArtworks in either a grid or list configuration, using artwork cards for each item. It reacts to changes in the items array and view mode but does not directly manage filtering, sorting, or pagination.

Shows pagination controls based on total_items and page_size from getGalleryArtworks, and the current page from the URL-derived state. When a pagination control is activated, it emits an event so the controller can update the page URL parameter and call getGalleryArtworks with the same filters and sort.

Represents a single artwork within the gallery grid or list. Displays information based on getGalleryArtworks item data, including the artwork image, title, category_label, year, size_label, price_display, and status flags (is_in_favorites, is_in_wishlist, is_for_sale). It provides clickable areas for opening the quick-view overlay, navigating to product.html when a primary_product_id is present, and toggling Favorites and Wishlist states.

Handles adding artworks to Favorites from both the gallery cards and the quick-view overlay. When triggered with an artwork id, it calls addArtworkToFavorites and, based on the response, updates the local is_in_favorites state for that artwork and any global favorites count display. It also surfaces the response message in the UI where appropriate.

Handles adding artworks to the Wishlist from gallery cards and the quick-view overlay. When triggered with an artwork id (and optional notes), it calls addArtworkToWishlist. On success, it updates local is_in_wishlist state and any global wishlist count, and can expose the message from the response to the user.

Displays detailed information about a selected artwork without leaving the gallery page. When opened with a specific artwork id, it calls getArtworkDetails to fetch full artwork information and labels, and getRelatedArtworks to show related pieces. Within the modal, users can add the artwork to Favorites or Wishlist, or navigate between related artworks by triggering additional getArtworkDetails and getRelatedArtworks calls. Closing the modal does not change URL parameters.

Encapsulates logic for reading and writing URL query parameters that define gallery state. On initialization, it parses category, year_min, year_max, price_min, price_max, width_min_cm, width_max_cm, height_min_cm, height_max_cm, color_palette, sort, and page into a structured state object and passes it to the page controller. When child components emit filter, sort, or pagination events, this component updates the URL parameters accordingly (adding, updating, or removing parameters as needed) and notifies the controller to reload data. It also listens for browser navigation events to synchronize back/forward changes with the controller.

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered fixed-width layout, likely based on a 12-column grid within a max-width container (~960–1140px), with a two-column split in the main about section (image column ~35–40%, text column ~60–65%).
layout.layout_pattern: Within the existing site frame (artist name and centered navigation above), the Gallery page content is organized in a vertically stacked sequence inside a centered, fixed-width container. Immediately below the global header and a generous vertical gap, the gallery-page-controller introduces a two-column layout: a left filter column and a right content column. At the top of the right column, a slim horizontal control band holds the results summary and sort bar. Below this, the gallery grid fills the majority of the vertical space, with pagination controls at the bottom. Non-visual controllers (favorites, wishlist, URL sync) sit alongside these structures without affecting layout. A quick-view modal floats above everything when invoked, centered on the viewport.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Within the existing site frame (artist name and centered navigation above), the Gallery page content is organized in a vertically stacked sequence inside a centered, fixed-width container. Immediately below the global header and a generous vertical gap, the gallery-page-controller introduces a two-column layout: a left filter column and a right content column. At the top of the right column, a slim horizontal control band holds the results summary and sort bar. Below this, the gallery grid fills the majority of the vertical space, with pagination controls at the bottom. Non-visual controllers (favorites, wishlist, URL sync) sit alongside these structures without affecting layout. A quick-view modal floats above everything when invoked, centered on the viewport.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main gallery content sits within a max-width container of roughly 960–1140px, centered on the page with generous left and right margins to preserve the gallery-like feel. The two-column region allocates about 30–35% of the inner width to the left-side filter panel and 65–70% to the right-side content (summary, sort, grid, pagination), echoing the portrait/text split from the mockup. Vertically, there is a 48–64px gap between the global header/navigation and the start of the gallery content, another 24–32px between control bands and the grid, and 32–48px between the grid and pagination. Internal padding within bars and cards follows a 16–24px range, maintaining the established spacing scale.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page maintains a low-to-moderate density: controls are sparse, with substantial whitespace around each band and between components. The filter panel presents filters in well-separated vertical groups with clear breathing room. The right column uses a slim, editorial control row at the top and an airy grid with 2–4 artworks per row depending on screen width, with consistent gutters. Typography is thin and understated, and there are no heavy boxes or backgrounds—mostly white space with subtle lines and borders where necessary.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow continues the mockup’s vertical emphasis: from the site’s top-centered artist name and navigation, the eye drops down into the gallery section, first encountering the upper control area. Within that, the left filter column echoes the portrait image’s initial pull in the mockup, attracting attention through its solid vertical presence. The eye then moves horizontally to the right column: it first picks up the results summary and sort options at the top, then progresses downward into the grid of artworks. Within each card, attention moves from the artwork image (primary) to title/meta text, and then to lightweight favorite/wishlist icons. The quick-view modal, when open, interrupts this flow with a centered overlay that becomes the primary focal point.

layout.overall_description: The Gallery page sits within a calm, centered canvas that mirrors a white-walled gallery. Below the existing top-centered artist name and navigation, a generous band of whitespace introduces the main gallery area, which is contained in a fixed-width column floating in the middle of the browser window. This area splits into two understated columns: on the left, a tall, narrow filter panel reads like a clean sidebar of options, anchored near the top and scrolling with the page in a subtle, sticky way. On the right, a slightly wider content column begins with a thin horizontal control strip where the current results count and active filters are stated on the left, while sort and view controls align to the right. Just beneath this band, the artwork grid unfolds downward—rows of understated cards with prominent images, thin type, and plenty of air between them. At the very bottom of this right column, centered pagination dots or numbers quietly invite further browsing. Invisible behaviors—saving to Favorites or Wishlist and synchronizing state to the URL—sit behind the scenes, triggered by light-touch icons on the cards. When a user opens a quick view, the rest of this carefully spaced layout recedes behind a dim overlay, and a centered, spacious modal comes forward, still reflecting the same minimalist typography and restrained framing as the rest of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The gallery-page-controller defines the main content area for the Gallery page beneath the existing global header and navigation. It starts approximately 48–64px below the navigation, spanning a centered max-width container of about 960–1140px with equal left and right margins that create a strong white frame. Inside this container, it establishes a two-column layout: a left column for the filter panel and a right column for all gallery content (summary, sort bar, grid, pagination). The left column occupies roughly 30–35% of the container width, while the right column uses the remaining 65–70%, echoing the image/text proportion from the mockup’s about section. Vertically, the controller applies 32–48px top padding above its first visible child and 48–64px bottom padding beneath the pagination to keep the layout feeling airy and editorial.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, centered container that defines the local grid and column structure; it scrolls with the page but does not fix itself to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The gallery-filter-panel sits in the left column created by the gallery-page-controller. Its left edge aligns with the left edge of the main content container, and its top edge begins near the very top of the gallery content region, roughly aligned with the top of the results summary and sort bar on the right. It occupies about 30–35% of the container width and extends vertically based on the number of filters, forming a tall, slim column reminiscent of the portrait image in the mockup. Internally, filter sections are stacked vertically with 16–24px padding around each group and 16–24px vertical gaps between groups. The panel has a very light visual footprint: a white background, thin section dividers if needed, and subtle headings in thin typography. On large screens, the panel stays visually anchored near the top of the viewport via a subtle sticky behavior so it remains visible while the user scrolls through grid content on the right. On smaller screens, it can collapse into a top-of-page filter drawer or accordions above the grid, but always preserves generous internal spacing and avoids feeling dense.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Within the main content container it is a left-column element with sticky positioning (e.g., sticking a short distance below the global header) on larger screens, and a normal-flow, optionally collapsible block above the grid on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The gallery-sort-bar lives in the upper portion of the right column, horizontally aligned with the top of the filter panel on the left. Together with the results summary, it forms a thin control band across the top of the right column. This band spans the full width of the right column (about 65–70% of the overall container) and sits 0–8px below the top padding of the gallery-page-controller’s inner content. The sort bar’s controls are aligned to the right edge of the right column, arranged in a single horizontal row with lightweight UI elements—text labels and pill- or underline-style selectors rather than heavy buttons—maintaining a minimalist, editorial feel. There is 8–16px of vertical padding above and below the controls, and a subtle hairline divider may sit along the bottom edge, echoing the delicate separators in the mockup. On smaller screens, this bar shifts toward full-width above the grid, with controls centered or right-aligned while preserving plenty of surrounding whitespace.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column, scrolling away with the rest of the content; it may visually pair with the results summary as a single control band but remains a distinct, right-aligned segment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The gallery-results-summary is positioned at the top of the right column, forming the left side of the same horizontal band that contains the sort bar. It is aligned with the left edge of the right column and sits on the same vertical baseline as the sort controls on the far right. The summary itself is relatively compact—usually one to two lines—stating the number of artworks and key filter context. It uses thin, understated typography, but it acts as a focal point similar to the CV link in the mockup: positioned near the top of the content column and slightly separated from the dense grid below. The summary has 8–16px of vertical padding, mirroring the sort bar, and sits 0–16px above the first row of the gallery grid, with a generous gap (24–32px) before the cards to keep this band distinct. On mobile layouts, the summary may sit above the sort controls in a vertical stack, centered or left-aligned but still separated from the grid by a noticeable vertical gap.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inside the right column, functioning as a left-aligned element in a shared horizontal control row with the sort bar; scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The gallery-grid occupies the majority of the right column beneath the top control band (results summary and sort bar). It begins approximately 24–32px below that band, giving a clear visual separation, and spans the full width of the right column, with internal horizontal gutters of 16–24px between cards. On typical desktop widths, it displays 3–4 columns of artwork cards, each card maintaining a consistent width and an image-first vertical layout. The grid’s top edge aligns slightly lower than the lower edge of the filter panel’s heading area, mimicking the way the biography text starts near the top of the portrait image in the mockup. The bottom of the grid sits 32–48px above the pagination controls, forming a comfortable visual pause between browsing and navigation. There are no extra heavy frames or backgrounds around the grid itself; the white background of the page and the consistent spacing between cards create the structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Responsive multi-column grid that reflows based on viewport width—e.g., 1 column on very small screens, 2 on small tablets, 3–4 on larger desktops—while remaining anchored to the right column defined by the page controller.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The gallery-pagination sits beneath the gallery grid within the right column, centered horizontally. It begins approximately 32–48px below the bottom row of artwork cards, creating a clear end to each grid page. The pagination controls themselves are minimal and airy: small, evenly spaced page numbers or dots with thin typography and subtle hover/active states, perhaps including slim previous/next arrows. The control row does not extend beyond the width of the right column, and there is 24–32px of vertical padding above and below the pagination so it does not feel cramped against the bottom edge of the main content area. It acts as a quiet terminus for the scrolling experience on each page of results.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static control row at the bottom of the right column, scrolling away as the user navigates upwards; it may reappear at the bottom of each new page of results.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each artwork-card inhabits a single cell of the gallery grid. It is a vertically oriented card whose width is determined by the number of columns (e.g., approximately 1/3 or 1/4 of the right column’s width minus gutters) and whose height expands to fit its content. The top 60–70% of the card’s height is devoted to the artwork image, which is framed within a subtle border or fine hairline outline, echoing the portrait framing in the mockup. Below the image, a 16–24px vertical padding region contains the artwork title in thin, slightly larger type, followed by optional meta text (medium, dimensions, year) in a lighter weight and smaller size. Favorite and wishlist icons sit unobtrusively near the card’s top-right corner (over the image or just inside the padding area) with minimal iconography, and an optional quick-view trigger is placed as a small text link or icon near the bottom-right of the card content. The card’s background remains white, with a very subtle shadow or border only on hover/focus to avoid adding visual weight. Vertical spacing between the card content and the bottom edge of the card is at least 16px to maintain the open, editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Child element of the responsive grid; it maintains a consistent internal padding and image ratio while its width adjusts with the grid column layout. It is fully clickable or has clearly delineated interactive zones, but no special fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites-action-handler is non-visual and does not consume explicit layout space. Conceptually, it is associated with the favorite icons on each artwork-card. As the user interacts with these icons, the handler updates the underlying state and reflects changes on the cards (e.g., toggling an icon’s fill) without introducing new UI elements. Spatially, its influence is confined to the card area, but it remains invisible and does not alter the spacing, dimensions, or positioning of any visible component.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: No direct spatial presence; operates as a background controller tied to icon interactions on artwork cards, updating state without affecting layout flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Like the favorites controller, the wishlist-action-handler is an invisible behavioral layer associated with wishlist icons or controls within each artwork-card. It has no visual footprint and does not change the grid, card, or control positioning. It simply listens for interactions in those regions and updates the wishlist state, potentially triggering subtle visual changes on the card (such as an icon state change or a brief confirmation) within the card’s existing layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: No direct layout footprint; functions as a background action controller connected to specific elements inside artwork cards, with all updates occurring within existing space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The artwork-quick-view-modal appears as a full-viewport overlay that sits above the entire page, dimming the background gallery with a semi-transparent dark scrim. At the center of the viewport, a fixed, white modal panel is displayed, typically occupying around 60–70% of the viewport width (capped at the same 960–1140px max-width as the main container) and 60–80% of the viewport height, with ample margins on all sides. The panel itself continues the minimalist, editorial style: generous internal padding of 24–32px, thin typography, and a clear hierarchy where the artwork image is prominently displayed on the left or top, with details and actions on the right or below. The close control (an ‘X’ icon or text link) is positioned at the top-right corner of the modal content, set in from the edge by 16–24px. The modal is vertically and horizontally centered, and the rest of the page remains visible but visually recessed beneath the scrim, preserving the sense of a gallery space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position overlay anchored to the viewport, centered both vertically and horizontally; it covers interactive access to underlying components while open but does not alter their layout when closed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The url-state-sync component is non-visual and occupies no visible space within the layout. Conceptually, it is attached to the gallery-page-controller and listens to changes from the filter panel, sort bar, pagination, and grid. It keeps those states reflected in the URL and restores them when the page is loaded with specific parameters. This behavior does not introduce additional UI; instead, it quietly binds the spatially arranged controls and content into a shareable, bookmarkable state without affecting their positions or sizes.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: No direct spatial manifestation; functions as an invisible layer bound to the page controller and relevant interactive components, operating entirely outside the visual layout.

name: Shop
title: Shop
description: Page for browsing and purchasing artworks and prints with filtering, sorting, pagination, and quick cart/wishlist actions.

core_features: Load and display available filter and sort options for the active shop category using getShopFilterOptions, including subject categories, price range, size ranges, and sort choices.
Retrieve and display a paginated grid of products for the selected shop category using getShopProducts, respecting all active URL-based filters, sorting, and page number.
Allow users to refine the product list by subject category, price range, width, height, and other attributes, updating the URL parameters and re-fetching results via getShopProducts.
Allow users to change sorting (e.g., by newest, price, size) using values returned from getShopFilterOptions.sort_options, updating the URL and reloading products via getShopProducts.
Provide quick navigation from each product tile to the detailed product page (product.html?id=PRODUCT_ID) when clicking the image or title.
Enable quick add-to-cart actions from product tiles by calling addProductToCart with the selected product and quantity (and optional size/frame labels when applicable).
Enable quick add-to-wishlist actions from product tiles by calling addProductToWishlist, reflecting wishlist membership using the is_in_wishlist flag from getShopProducts.
Support pagination of the product grid using the page parameter and pagination metadata from getShopProducts, allowing users to move between pages while preserving current filters and sort settings.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Initial page load with optional pre-applied filters.
1. User arrives at shop.html, optionally with URL parameters such as categoryId, subject_category, price_min, price_max, width_min_cm, width_max_cm, height_min_cm, height_max_cm, sort, and page.
2. The page controller reads categoryId and maps it to the shop_category argument for both getShopFilterOptions and getShopProducts.
3. The page calls getShopFilterOptions(shop_category) to obtain the available subject_categories, price_range, size_filters, and sort_options, and populates the filter sidebar and sort control using these dynamic values.
4. The controller builds a filters object from the current URL parameters (subject_category, price_min, price_max, width_min_cm, width_max_cm, height_min_cm, height_max_cm).
5. The page calls getShopProducts(shop_category, filters, sort, page, page_size) and renders the returned items in the product grid.
6. The grid uses each item.product plus metadata (category_label, price_display, size_label, is_in_wishlist) to render product tiles with correct wishlist state and pricing.

Workflow: User changes filters (subject, price, size).
1. User adjusts a filter control in the sidebar (e.g., selects a subject category, adjusts a price range, or changes width/height inputs).
2. On filter change, the filter-sidebar component updates the corresponding URL parameters (subject_category, price_min, price_max, width_min_cm, width_max_cm, height_min_cm, height_max_cm). Filters that are cleared cause the related parameters to be removed from the URL.
3. The filter change also resets the page parameter to 1 in the URL to start from the first page of filtered results.
4. The shop-page-controller listens to URL changes, reconstructs the filters object from the updated URL, and calls getShopProducts with the new filters and existing sort value.
5. The product-grid re-renders with the new getShopProducts response, showing only products that match the selected filters.

Workflow: User changes sorting.
1. User selects a sort option from the sort control (e.g., one of the options returned by getShopFilterOptions.sort_options).
2. The sort-control component writes the selected sort value into the sort URL parameter, resetting the page parameter to 1.
3. The controller detects the change, rebuilds the filters object from URL parameters, and calls getShopProducts with the updated sort value.
4. The product-grid re-renders in the new order using the freshly returned items.

Workflow: User navigates to a different page of results.
1. User clicks a pagination control (e.g., Next, Previous, or a specific page number).
2. The pagination-controls component updates the page parameter in the URL while keeping categoryId, filters, and sort parameters unchanged.
3. The controller reads the new page value, constructs the filters object from the URL, and calls getShopProducts with the same shop_category, filters, and sort but with the new page value.
4. The product-grid re-renders, showing items for the requested page.
5. If the page number in the URL is outside the valid range, the implementation can clamp it to the nearest valid page and update the URL accordingly.

Workflow: User opens a product detail page.
1. User clicks on a product’s image or title within the product-grid.
2. The product-grid component reads the associated item.product.id.
3. The browser navigates to product.html?id=ITEM_PRODUCT_ID via a standard link, allowing the product detail page to load using that ID.
4. The user can later use the browser back button to return to the shop page, preserving the URL parameters and therefore the active filters, sort, and page state.

Workflow: Quick add to cart from product tile.
1. User clicks a quick add-to-cart action on a product tile (e.g., a button associated with the product-grid item).
2. The product-grid or a dedicated add-to-cart-form component determines the appropriate quantity (such as a default quantity of 1) and optional selected_size_label and selected_frame_label, based on either product defaults or inline selections on the tile.
3. The component calls addProductToCart with productId = item.product.id, quantity, selected_size_label (if applicable), and selected_frame_label (if applicable).
4. On success, the component updates any in-page cart indicator using the returned item_count and subtotal_display, and optionally exposes a “View cart” link that navigates to cart.html.
5. If addProductToCart returns success=false, the component displays the returned message and does not update the cart indicator.

Workflow: Add to wishlist from product tile.
1. User clicks a wishlist control (e.g., an icon or button) on a product tile.
2. The product-grid or a dedicated wishlist handler calls addProductToWishlist with productId = item.product.id and an optional notes value (which may be left empty for quick-add behavior).
3. On success, the product tile updates its wishlist visual state, using either the response or by setting a local state flag; if needed, subsequent data refreshes can also rely on is_in_wishlist from getShopProducts for consistency.
4. The page may update a header or in-page wishlist count using the wishlist_count from the API response.
5. If the user later clicks the global Wishlist navigation (e.g., a header icon), the browser navigates to wishlist.html, where the wishlist contents are managed.

Workflow: Deep link from another page with predefined criteria.
1. User clicks a link from another page (such as the home page) that points to shop.html with certain URL parameters set (e.g., categoryId, a preconfigured price_max, or a specific subject_category).
2. On arrival, the shop-page-controller reads these URL parameters, sets the shop_category accordingly, and initializes filters and sort based on the query string instead of any defaults.
3. The page calls getShopFilterOptions and getShopProducts using the derived shop_category and URL-derived filters and sort.
4. The product-grid then shows results consistent with the originating context (for example, a specific subject focus or price constraint), while still allowing the user to further refine or reset filters.
interactions: Changing any filter control (subject category, price range, width/height inputs) updates the corresponding URL parameters, resets page to 1, and triggers a new getShopProducts call through the controller.
Selecting a sort option updates the sort URL parameter, resets page to 1, and triggers a new getShopProducts call, reordering the product-grid.
Clicking product images or titles in the product-grid navigates the browser to product.html?id=PRODUCT_ID, using the clicked product’s ID.
Clicking a quick add-to-cart button on a product tile triggers addProductToCart with the product’s ID and configuration, and shows feedback to the user based on the API response.
Clicking a wishlist toggle on a product tile triggers addProductToWishlist with the product’s ID, then updates the visual state to reflect whether the product is wishlisted.
Using pagination controls updates the page URL parameter and causes a new getShopProducts call to fetch the corresponding page of products.

Reloading the page or using the browser’s back/forward buttons re-applies the state from current URL parameters, causing the controller to re-run getShopFilterOptions and getShopProducts with the same categoryId, filters, sort, and page values.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page’s state is fully derived from URL parameters and API responses, avoiding any reliance on localStorage. The categoryId URL parameter determines the active shop_category passed into getShopFilterOptions and getShopProducts. If categoryId is present, its value is used directly as the shop_category; if it is absent, a default shop_category can be applied by the implementation and, if desired, written to the URL for consistency. The subject_category, price_min, price_max, width_min_cm, width_max_cm, height_min_cm, and height_max_cm URL parameters are treated as the source of truth for user-selected filters. On every state change, the controller constructs a filters object using these parameters (including only those present in the URL) and passes that filters object into getShopProducts. The sort URL parameter stores the current sort choice. When the sort control changes, this parameter is updated and the page number is reset to 1. The page URL parameter stores the current pagination index. When pagination controls are used, only the page parameter is changed (unless a filter or sort change also occurs, in which case page is reset to 1). Any time the URL changes (via filter change, sort change, pagination, or navigation to the page), the shop-page-controller re-parses the URL, updates its internal representation of categoryId, filters, sort, and page, calls getShopFilterOptions with the current shop_category if necessary, and calls getShopProducts with shop_category, the constructed filters object, sort, page, and a configured page_size. The resulting product list and metadata are then bound to the product-grid. Because all interactive components (filter-sidebar, sort-control, pagination-controls) modify only the URL, the URL itself becomes the single source of truth for the page’s state, enabling deep linking, bookmarking, and consistent behavior across reloads.

components.functionality: Central logic component that initializes the shop page, reads URL parameters, calls getShopFilterOptions and getShopProducts, and distributes data to child components. It observes URL changes triggered by user interactions (filters, sort, pagination) and re-fetches data accordingly.

Displays dynamic filter controls for subject categories, price range, and size ranges using data from getShopFilterOptions. When the user changes any filter, it updates the corresponding URL parameters (subject_category, price_min, price_max, width_min_cm, width_max_cm, height_min_cm, height_max_cm), resets the page parameter to 1, and thereby triggers the controller to fetch a new filtered product list.

Renders a sort dropdown or equivalent control using sort_options from getShopFilterOptions. It initializes its selected value from the sort URL parameter (if present) or an implementation-defined default. When the user selects a different sort value, it writes that value to the sort URL parameter, resets page to 1, and allows the controller to re-run getShopProducts with the new sort order.

Displays the list of products returned from getShopProducts as a grid of tiles. Each tile uses item.product for core data, category_label for subject display, price_display for price, size_label for size, and is_in_wishlist to indicate if the product is in the wishlist. Clicking the product image or title navigates to product.html?id=product.id. The grid delegates quick add-to-cart actions to the add-to-cart component and quick add-to-wishlist actions to the wishlist component, passing the relevant product information.

Uses total_items, page, and page_size from the getShopProducts response to compute available pages. Renders controls for moving between pages. When a control is used, it updates the page URL parameter to the selected page number without altering other parameters, prompting the controller to call getShopProducts for that page.

Handles quick add-to-cart actions from product tiles. Receives a product reference from the product-grid, determines quantity and any selected_size_label and selected_frame_label based on tile-level configuration or defaults, and calls addProductToCart with these arguments. It processes the response, surfacing success or error messages and updating cart-related indicators (such as item counts) using item_count and subtotal_display.

Handles quick add-to-wishlist actions from product tiles. Receives a product reference from the product-grid and calls addProductToWishlist with productId and optional notes. On success, it updates the UI state for that product to indicate wishlist membership and may trigger an update to any wishlist count display using wishlist_count from the response. It can also reconcile with is_in_wishlist from subsequent getShopProducts responses to ensure consistency.

Optional in-page control for switching between shop categories (such as prints and originals). When the user selects a different category, it updates the categoryId URL parameter, may clear or retain existing filter parameters based on implementation rules, resets page to 1, and allows the controller to re-run getShopFilterOptions and getShopProducts for the new shop_category.

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered fixed-width layout, likely based on a 12-column grid within a max-width container (~960–1140px), with a two-column split in the main about section (image column ~35–40%, text column ~60–65%).
layout.layout_pattern: The shop page inherits the mockup’s linear, top‑to‑bottom structure with a centered, fixed‑width main container. At the top of the shop content sits a light, centered controller area containing the category switcher (as the local navigation) and the sort control. Below a generous vertical gap, the page transitions into a two‑column layout: the left column acts as the filter sidebar, and the right column contains the product grid. Within the right column, the sort control visually anchors near the top right, echoing the CV link’s placement in the mockup. Pagination appears centered below the grid, maintaining the single‑column rhythm at the bottom of the page.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The shop page inherits the mockup’s linear, top‑to‑bottom structure with a centered, fixed‑width main container. At the top of the shop content sits a light, centered controller area containing the category switcher (as the local navigation) and the sort control. Below a generous vertical gap, the page transitions into a two‑column layout: the left column acts as the filter sidebar, and the right column contains the product grid. Within the right column, the sort control visually anchors near the top right, echoing the CV link’s placement in the mockup. Pagination appears centered below the grid, maintaining the single‑column rhythm at the bottom of the page.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main container spans roughly 960–1140px and remains centered with wide side margins. The top controller area (category switcher + sort) takes up about the upper 20–25% of the initial viewport height with generous whitespace above and below. The two‑column content area beneath uses approximately a 35% width for the filter sidebar and 65% for the product grid. Vertical spacing between the controller area and the two‑column content follows the 48–64px section margin, and additional 32–48px gaps separate the product grid from the pagination. Component padding within cards and filter blocks follows the 16–24px range to keep everything airy.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density stays low to moderate. The controller area is visually sparse, with only a few controls centered and well separated. The filter sidebar organizes filters in clearly separated blocks with generous vertical breathing room. The product grid shows relatively large artwork thumbnails with minimal text and restrained actions, keeping each card uncluttered. Pagination controls are simple and spaced out. Overall, whitespace is used liberally to avoid any sense of crowding.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first lands on the centered category switcher within the top controller area, then naturally scans rightward to notice the sort control. From there, the flow drops into the two‑column region: the left filter column is seen peripherally, but the larger, image‑heavy product grid on the right becomes the main focal area, similar to how the bio text balanced the portrait in the mockup. Within the grid, users scan artworks in a loose F‑pattern across rows, with the subtle add‑to‑cart button and the slightly more colorful wishlist icon acting as localized focal points. Finally, the gaze moves down to the centered pagination controls at the bottom, reinforcing the vertical reading path.

layout.overall_description: The shop page sits inside a centered, fixed‑width container with generous left and right margins, evoking a gallery wall rather than a typical e‑commerce grid. At the top of the shop section, a spacious controller band introduces a minimalist, centered category switcher, flanked visually by a discreet sort control aligned toward the right edge of the content width. A clear 48–64px vertical gap then opens into a two‑column layout: on the left, a slim but tall filter sidebar runs down the page, and on the right, a broader product grid displays artworks in two to three columns with large, dominant images and minimal text beneath each piece.
