page_functionality.state_logic: The Favorites page uses URL parameters to manage view state, deep-linking, and navigation without relying on localStorage or other client-side persistence. The primary URL parameters are: 1) sort: controls client-side ordering of the favorites list. Examples of allowed values (interpreted purely on the client) include values representing different sort strategies (e.g., by date added, by artwork year, by price). When sort changes via the toolbar, the parameter is updated in the URL, and the current list of items from getFavoritesList is re-sorted in memory. No additional calls to getFavoritesList are required. 2) view: represents the layout mode of the list, such as grid or list. When this parameter changes, the Favorites list component re-renders items in the new layout but does not modify the dataset or call any API. This parameter is preserved in the URL so that refreshing or sharing the link keeps the preferred layout. 3) artworkId: when present, indicates that the artwork quick-view modal should be opened for the artwork with this id, assuming it exists in the favorites list loaded via getFavoritesList. On initial load, the page first calls getFavoritesList; once the data resolves, if artworkId matches an artwork.id in the favorites, the page opens the modal and calls getArtworkDetails(artworkId) and getRelatedArtworks(artworkId). While the modal is open, navigation between related artworks updates the artworkId parameter, enabling deep-linking between specific favorites. Closing the modal clears the artworkId parameter from the URL. Additional optional parameters specific to navigation back to gallery.html (such as gallery filters) are passed only when leaving this page and are not interpreted by the Favorites page itself. Throughout, the Favorites collection itself (which artworks belong to the list) is determined exclusively by the getFavoritesList, removeFavoriteItem, and clearFavoritesList interfaces; the URL state affects only ordering, layout, and which artwork is focused in the modal.

components.functionality: Initializes the Favorites page, orchestrates data loading via getFavoritesList, reads and applies URL parameters (sort, view, artworkId), and coordinates communication between toolbar, list, modal, and empty state components.
Displays favorites count, provides sort and view-mode controls, exposes a 'Clear all' action, and offers a 'Back to Gallery' navigation control.
event_handlers: updates the sort URL parameter and triggers client-side reordering of favorites
updates the view URL parameter for layout changes
calls clearFavoritesList and, on success, clears the items in the page shell

components.functionality: Renders the collection of favorite artworks returned by getFavoritesList in either grid or list view, based on the view URL parameter and current sort order.
event_handlers: opens the artwork quick-view modal by setting artworkId in the URL
calls removeFavoriteItem for the selected favorite_item_id and updates the list on success
initiates navigation to product.html after ensuring the artwork has linked products via getArtworkDetails

components.functionality: Represents a single favorite artwork entry, showing thumbnail, title, category label, size label, price display (if present), and per-item actions such as open details, remove, and view in shop.
event_handlers: bubbles up to favorites-list to set artworkId URL param and open quick-view modal
bubbles up to favorites-list to call removeFavoriteItem
bubbles up to favorites-list to resolve linked product via getArtworkDetails and navigate to product.html

components.functionality: Shows when getFavoritesList returns total_items equal to 0 or after clearFavoritesList succeeds, informing the user that no favorites are saved and offering navigation back to the gallery.

Displays full details for a selected favorite artwork and exposes actions to view it in the shop (if linked products exist) and to navigate among related artworks. Opens when artworkId is present in the URL and corresponds to an artwork in Favorites.
event_handlers: triggered when artworkId URL parameter is set; calls getArtworkDetails(artworkId) and getRelatedArtworks(artworkId)
clears artworkId from the URL and hides the modal
uses linked_products from getArtworkDetails to navigate to product.html?id={product_id}
updates artworkId in URL to the selected related artwork's id and re-calls getArtworkDetails and getRelatedArtworks

components.functionality: Within the quick-view modal, shows related artworks returned from getRelatedArtworks for the currently focused artwork, allowing navigation between them without leaving the Favorites page.
event_handlers: notifies artwork-quick-view-modal to update artworkId URL parameter and refresh details

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered fixed-width layout, likely based on a 12-column grid within a max-width container (~960–1140px), with a two-column split in the main about section (image column ~35–40%, text column ~60–65%).
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the same top-to-bottom, centered composition as the mockup: a calm header area with the artist’s name centered at the top, followed by a minimalist centered navigation bar. Below this, after a generous vertical gap, the Favorites content begins within a fixed-width central container. The Favorites section itself starts with a lightweight toolbar laid out in two columns (summary on the left, controls on the right), followed by the main favorites list. When the list is empty, a two-column empty state (image/illustration on the left, explanatory text and CTA on the right) visually echoes the original about-page layout. The related artworks strip anchors the bottom of the page as a secondary section. A modal overlays the entire page when quick view is triggered, again using a left image / right details two-column pattern.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the same top-to-bottom, centered composition as the mockup: a calm header area with the artist’s name centered at the top, followed by a minimalist centered navigation bar. Below this, after a generous vertical gap, the Favorites content begins within a fixed-width central container. The Favorites section itself starts with a lightweight toolbar laid out in two columns (summary on the left, controls on the right), followed by the main favorites list. When the list is empty, a two-column empty state (image/illustration on the left, explanatory text and CTA on the right) visually echoes the original about-page layout. The related artworks strip anchors the bottom of the page as a secondary section. A modal overlays the entire page when quick view is triggered, again using a left image / right details two-column pattern.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header (name + navigation) occupies roughly the top quarter of the first viewport, with large vertical spacing around the centered name. The main content container is fixed-width (~960–1140px) and centered, with wide left and right margins on large screens. Within this container, the Favorites toolbar occupies a relatively shallow band but has generous vertical padding so it feels airy. The favorites list consumes the bulk of the vertical space, but with clear breathing room between rows and around each card. The empty state, when shown, uses the same vertical territory as the list but with a more prominent central composition. The related artworks strip is visually lighter and occupies less vertical depth, separated from the list by a large vertical gap. The modal, when open, temporarily dominates the screen, with a two-column layout centered both vertically and horizontally, leaving soft margins to the viewport edges.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page maintains low to moderate content density: wide margins, generous vertical gaps (48–64px) between major sections, and comfortable line spacing in text blocks. The toolbar is intentionally sparse, containing a small number of succinct controls. The favorites list may contain many items, but each card has ample internal padding and clear spacing between items, so the grid feels like a curated gallery wall rather than a dense catalog. Text on cards and in the modal is kept short and well-spaced, mirroring the clean editorial reading experience from the mockup.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow begins at the top center with the artist’s name, then moves down to the horizontal navigation. From there, the eye drops further down to the Favorites toolbar, first picking up the left side (section title and count) and then scanning rightward to sorting and navigation actions. Below that, the eye flows down into the favorites grid, scanning artworks left to right across rows, following an F-shaped pattern. Within each card, the artwork image is the main attractor, with title and metadata encountered immediately below. The related artworks strip at the bottom becomes a gentle secondary focal area once the main list is consumed. When the quick view modal opens, attention is pulled to the large artwork image on the left, then to the right-hand text details and actions, mirroring the original left-image/right-text about layout.

layout.overall_description: The Favorites page feels like a quiet, curated gallery wall inside an editorial layout. A white background and a centered, fixed-width content column create a sense of structure and calm. At the very top, the artist’s name sits alone in the center with thin typography and substantial top and bottom padding, followed by a simple centered navigation row. After a pronounced vertical gap, the Favorites section begins inside the same central column: a slim, two-column toolbar lines up with the content grid below, with a brief text summary on the left and a few restrained actions on the right. Under this, the favorites themselves appear as a spacious grid of artwork cards, arranged within wide left and right margins so they feel like framed pieces on a gallery wall. Each card is mostly image with a subtle border and minimal details beneath, breathing in plenty of white space. If there are no favorites, this grid area is replaced with a balanced two-column empty state, with an image-like placeholder on the left and an inviting block of text and a primary link on the right, echoing the portrait-and-bio layout of the original mockup. At the bottom of the content column, separated by a generous gap, a low-key related artworks strip stretches horizontally, offering a few additional works without overwhelming the main favorites. When a user opens a quick view, the rest of the page softens behind a translucent overlay, and a centered, two-column modal lifts the selected artwork into focus, with the image on the left and concise details and actions on the right, preserving the same calm, editorial rhythm throughout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page shell establishes a full-viewport white canvas with a single centered content column. A max-width container (approximately 960–1140px) is centered horizontally, leaving wide, symmetric margins on both sides on desktop. At the very top of this container, the artist’s name is centered and given generous vertical padding (roughly 48–64px above and below), using thin typography that sets the quiet, editorial tone. Directly beneath the name, a horizontal navigation bar is also centered, spanning about 60–70% of the container width, with equal left and right padding and modest vertical spacing (16–24px) above and below. Below the navigation, a substantial vertical gap (around 56–64px) separates the header from the Favorites content, optionally anchored by a very subtle, short horizontal divider line centered in the column. All subsequent components (toolbar, list/empty state, related strip) are aligned to this same central container, ensuring consistent left and right edges for all content and preserving a gallery-like, fixed-width composition. The page scrolls vertically with no fixed chrome aside from the browser frame itself, retaining the linear, top-to-bottom reading flow of the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Defines the main fixed-width, centered content column and standard vertical scroll; no fixed or sticky elements at the shell level.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites toolbar sits at the top of the main Favorites section, directly below the header’s large vertical gap, inside the centered container. It spans the full width of the container but remains visually light, with vertical padding of roughly 16–24px above and below its contents. The toolbar is arranged in a two-column horizontal layout: the left block occupies about 35–40% of the content width and aligns to the container’s left edge, hosting elements like the section title (e.g., “Favorites”) and a small count of saved artworks. The right block takes the remaining 60–65% and aligns its contents to the right edge, containing compact controls such as a sort dropdown, a “Remove all” link, and a subtle blue-text link or button (e.g., “View main gallery”) that echoes the CV link’s role as a secondary focal point. The typography is thin and understated, with only slight weight differentiation between labels and actions. There is clear horizontal spacing (at least 24px) between individual controls so nothing feels crowded. The toolbar’s bottom edge aligns visually with the left and right edges of the favorites list below, creating a clean transition into the main content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sits in normal document flow at the top of the Favorites section; not sticky by default, though it maintains consistent alignment with the content grid beneath it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites list fills the main body of the Favorites section directly beneath the toolbar, separated by a comfortable vertical gap of about 32–40px. It remains within the same centered max-width container, so its left and right edges align perfectly with the toolbar and header. On larger screens, the list is presented as a spacious grid of artwork cards: each row typically contains three cards, each card occupying roughly 30–32% of the container width, with horizontal gutters of about 24px between cards. On slightly smaller widths, the layout gracefully collapses to two cards per row, and on mobile to a single centered column. Vertical spacing between rows of cards is similarly generous (24–32px), preserving an airy, gallery-like feel. The grid does not stretch to the edges of the viewport; the global container margins keep the cards visually framed with whitespace. The top row of cards visually aligns with the right-hand elements of the toolbar, supporting a subtle F-pattern scan: eyes move down from the right toolbar actions into the top row of artworks, then across each row left to right. The list extends vertically as long as necessary, with large section-level spacing separating it from the related artworks strip below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Expands vertically within the central container as more favorites are added; uses a responsive grid that adjusts the number of columns while maintaining consistent gutters and alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each favorite item card sits as a discrete, framed element within the favorites grid. On desktop, a card’s width is governed by the grid (approximately one-third of the container minus gutters), and its content is arranged in a vertical stack. At the top of the card, a portrait- or landscape-oriented artwork image occupies the full card width, maintaining a consistent aspect ratio (roughly 3:4 or 4:5) and framed by a very subtle, thin border that echoes the portrait treatment in the mockup. The image has no heavy shadows; the emphasis is on the artwork itself. Below the image, there is 16–24px of vertical padding before the text block begins. The text block is left-aligned and includes the artwork title, year, and possibly medium or size, laid out with ample line spacing and minimal font sizing, so it reads like small caption copy rather than UI labels. Above or adjacent to the title, a small, unobtrusive favorite toggle icon or “Remove from favorites” affordance sits in the top-right corner area of the card, aligned near the top edge so it doesn’t crowd the text. Total internal padding of the card (excluding the image which goes edge-to-edge within the card) is about 16–24px on all sides. The card itself may have a subtle border or faint hairline outline to differentiate it from the white page background without heavy chrome. Vertical space between the bottom of the text and the next card row is at least 24px, maintaining the low-density feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a fixed-size, responsive grid item: its width adjusts with the number of columns, while internal padding and vertical stacking of image and text remain consistent.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When there are no favorites, the empty state occupies the same horizontal territory as the favorites list, starting at the same vertical position relative to the toolbar. It is composed as a balanced, two-column layout inside the central container, strongly echoing the original about-page composition. The left column takes about 35–40% of the container width and contains a vertically oriented illustration or a stylized placeholder frame, sized similarly to the original portrait image. This illustration is centered within its column and framed by a delicate border, with generous internal padding (24px) so it feels like a standalone piece of artwork. The right column occupies the remaining 60–65% and is aligned to the container’s right edge. It contains the empty state text: a short, slightly larger heading, a short paragraph explaining that no favorites have been saved yet, and one or two CTAs (e.g., “Browse gallery” in blue text as the primary action, possibly with a secondary link like “Visit shop”). The heading starts near the upper third of the right column, aligning horizontally with the upper part of the illustration for visual balance. Lines of text are well spaced with generous line height, and there is at least 16–24px between the heading, body text, and CTAs. The entire empty state block has generous top and bottom padding (48–64px) relative to surrounding sections, making it feel like a deliberate, central message rather than a small inline note.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally replaces the favorites list area when there are zero items; occupies a fixed two-column structure within the central container and maintains alignment with the toolbar above and related strip below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quick view modal overlays the entire viewport when opened, dimming the underlying page with a semi-transparent black or dark gray layer at about 40–50% opacity. Centered within the viewport, both horizontally and vertically, is a clean, rectangular modal panel with a white background and subtle shadow. The modal’s width is approximately 70–80% of the viewport, capped at the same max-width as the main content (around 960–1040px), and it sits with comfortable margins from all sides of the screen. Inside this panel, content is arranged in a two-column layout that mirrors the original about-page structure: the left column, occupying about 60–65% of the modal width, contains a large, prominently displayed artwork image, aligned to the left and vertically centered with consistent padding (24–32px) around it. The right column, taking the remaining 35–40%, holds the artwork’s textual details (title, year, materials, dimensions, short description) and actions (e.g., Add/Remove favorite, View details page, Close). Text is left-aligned with generous line spacing and grouped into clear blocks separated by 12–16px. At the top-right of the modal, a minimal close icon is positioned with a small margin (16–24px) from the edges, not competing with the artwork. The bottom of the modal is left open and uncluttered, with only essential actions presented as slim text buttons or minimal controls, keeping the focus on the image and its details.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears as a centered, floating overlay above all page content; background is non-interactive while the modal is open. It is fixed to the viewport and scrolls internally only if content exceeds viewport height, maintaining the two-column proportion as space allows.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related artworks strip sits beneath the favorites list or empty state, separated by a clear vertical gap of about 56–64px to mark it as a secondary section. It stays inside the same centered max-width container, with its left and right edges aligned to all other content. At the top of the strip, a small, understated heading (e.g., “You may also like” or “Related works”) is left-aligned, with slightly increased letter spacing and thin weight, echoing the navigation style. Below this heading, a horizontal row of 3–4 smaller artwork thumbnails or mini-cards extends across the width of the container. Each thumbnail is framed with a subtle border similar to the main cards but scaled down, and the spacing between them (16–24px) keeps the row light and uncluttered. On smaller screens, this row can become a horizontally scrollable strip, but the default behavior is to fit the thumbnails in a single line where space permits. Vertical padding above and below the strip content (24–32px) ensures that it feels like a gentle, optional exploration zone rather than a dense, competing gallery.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Anchored near the bottom of the page’s main content within the central container; scrolls with the normal document flow and may allow horizontal scrolling for thumbnails on smaller screens while maintaining its left-aligned header.

name: Wishlist
title: Wishlist
description: Displays the user’s saved artworks and shop products, with options to review details, remove items, navigate to gallery or product pages, and add products to the cart.

core_features: Load and display all wishlist items using getWishlist, including title, thumbnail, price_display, and item_type-specific data.
Show a summary of the wishlist (total_items) and provide basic filtering (artworks/products) and sorting controls (e.g., by date added or price) driven by URL parameters.
For product-type wishlist items, provide a 'View details' link that navigates to product.html with the correct product id in the URL query string.

For artwork-type wishlist items, provide a 'View in gallery' link that navigates to gallery.html with the artwork.category as a URL parameter, and an optional quick-view modal that uses getArtworkDetails and getRelatedArtworks.
Allow users to remove individual items from the wishlist via removeWishlistItem, updating the list and total count without a full page reload.

Allow users to move items from Wishlist to Cart either by navigating to the product detail page from the wishlist item or by using an inline add-to-cart form that calls addProductToCart for eligible products.
Provide navigation controls to continue browsing the gallery (and to reach the cart via the global header Cart link after adding products).
Handle empty wishlist state by showing an appropriate message and clear navigation actions (e.g., link to gallery.html) without relying on any static or example data.
user_workflows: View wishlist items: 1) User opens wishlist.html from the header or a direct link. 2) On page initialization, the wishlist-page-loader component calls getWishlist with no parameters. 3) The response provides wishlist_id, items[], and total_items. 4) The wishlist-list component renders each item according to item_type (artwork or product), applying any filters/sorting derived from URL parameters. 5) The wishlist-summary-header displays total_items and current filter/sort state.

Remove an item from the wishlist: 1) User clicks 'Remove' on a specific wishlist item. 2) The wishlist-item-row component invokes removeWishlistItem with the clicked item’s wishlist_item_id. 3) On success, the component removes that item from the in-memory list and updates total_items using the returned wishlist_count. 4) If the list becomes empty, the wishlist-empty-state component is shown instead of the list. 5) Any global wishlist counter (if present in header) can be updated using wishlist_count.

Open product details from the wishlist: 1) User identifies a product-type wishlist item. 2) User clicks 'View details' on that item. 3) The wishlist-item-row component triggers navigation to product.html, appending ?id={product.id} to the URL. 4) Once on product.html, the user can configure size/frame options (handled on that page) and add the item to the cart. 5) After adding to cart, the user can click the global Cart link to navigate to cart.html.

Open artwork details and explore related pieces: 1) User identifies an artwork-type wishlist item. 2) User clicks 'Quick view' (or similar) on that item. 3) The artwork-detail-modal component opens and calls getArtworkDetails with artworkId = artwork.id from the wishlist item. 4) The modal displays full artwork details (category_label, medium_label, size_label, price_display, linked_products, is_in_favorites, is_in_wishlist). 5) In parallel or on demand, the modal can call getRelatedArtworks with the same artworkId, showing related artworks. 6) The user may click 'View in gallery' within the modal or the row, triggering navigation to gallery.html?category={artwork.category}. 7) Closing the modal returns the user to the wishlist context without reloading.

Move a wishlist product to cart via product detail page: 1) User clicks 'View details' on a product-type wishlist item. 2) Browser navigates to product.html?id={product.id}. 3) On product.html, user selects options and adds the product to cart (handled by that page’s logic). 4) After adding to cart, the user may click the global Cart link to go to cart.html. 5) The wishlist item remains until the user explicitly removes it from wishlist.html.

Quick-add a wishlist product directly to the cart: 1) User identifies a product-type wishlist item that is available for purchase (product.available is true). 2) User selects quantity and, if provided by the product object in getWishlist, selects size (from product.size_options) and frame (from product.frame_options), prefilled with product.default_size_label and product.default_frame_label when available. 3) User submits the inline add-to-cart form in the wishlist-item-row. 4) The wishlist-quick-add-to-cart-form component calls addProductToCart with productId = product.id, quantity, selected_size_label, and selected_frame_label. 5) On success, the component shows a confirmation message including subtotal_display and item_count returned from addProductToCart. 6) The user can then click the global Cart link (header) to navigate to cart.html if desired; the wishlist item is not auto-removed.

Continue browsing from the wishlist: 1) User sees the wishlist-summary-header or wishlist-empty-state components. 2) User clicks a 'Browse gallery' link that navigates to gallery.html (with optional category parameter when coming from an artwork item). 3) User may then add more items to the wishlist from the gallery or product pages. 4) When the user returns to wishlist.html (e.g., via header link), getWishlist is called again to show the updated list.
interactions: On page load (wishlist.html initial render), the wishlist-page-loader component triggers getWishlist; while loading, a loading state is shown and the list is hidden.

On filter change (e.g., switching between 'All', 'Artworks', and 'Products'), the wishlist-toolbar updates the wishlist_filter URL parameter and recalculates the visible subset client-side without additional API calls, then updates the wishlist-list display.

On sort change (e.g., 'Recently added', 'Price: low to high', 'Price: high to low'), the wishlist-toolbar updates the wishlist_sort URL parameter, reorders the in-memory items accordingly, and re-renders the wishlist-list.
On clicking a product thumbnail/title or 'View details', the wishlist-item-row navigates to product.html?id={product.id}.
On clicking 'View in gallery' from an artwork-type item row or from the artwork-detail-modal, the wishlist-item-row or modal navigates to gallery.html?category={artwork.category}.

On clicking 'Quick view' for an artwork-type item, the artwork-detail-modal component opens and calls getArtworkDetails; when the modal opens via a URL parameter (focus_artwork_id or focus_wishlist_item), the component reads the param to identify which artwork to request.

On opening the artwork-detail-modal, the component also optionally calls getRelatedArtworks(artworkId) to display a related artworks list; clicking any related artwork’s 'View in gallery' navigates to gallery.html with that artwork’s category parameter.

On clicking 'Remove' for a wishlist item, wishlist-item-row calls removeWishlistItem(wishlistItemId); on success, it removes the item from the local items array, updates total_items using wishlist_count, and may update shared header counters if they subscribe to wishlist changes.

On submitting the quick add-to-cart form for a product-type item, wishlist-quick-add-to-cart-form validates the selected quantity and option labels derived from the product object; if valid, it calls addProductToCart. On success, it shows a non-blocking confirmation state; on failure, it displays an error message without leaving the page.

On clicking the global Cart link in the header (outside this page’s components) after adding an item to cart, the site navigates to cart.html; no extra API calls are triggered by wishlist.html for this navigation.

On back/forward browser navigation, any changes to wishlist_view, wishlist_filter, wishlist_sort, or focus_* URL parameters are read and re-applied by the wishlist-page-loader and wishlist-toolbar, resulting in consistent visible state (e.g., reopened modal or restored filter).
page_functionality.state_logic: The Wishlist page uses URL parameters to govern view mode, filtering, sorting, and optional focused item details, without relying on localStorage. Supported URL parameters include: 1) wishlist_view: controls layout mode (e.g., 'list' or 'grid'). When the user toggles layout via the wishlist-toolbar, the parameter is updated (using history.pushState or similar). On load, wishlist-page-loader reads wishlist_view and configures the wishlist-list accordingly. 2) wishlist_filter: determines which item types are visible ('all', 'artworks', 'products'). Changing the filter updates the URL and triggers client-side filtering of the items obtained from getWishlist; getWishlist itself is not called again solely for this change. 3) wishlist_sort: controls sort order (e.g., 'added_desc', 'added_asc', 'price_asc', 'price_desc'). When the user changes the sort option, the wishlist-toolbar writes the chosen value to the URL and re-sorts the current in-memory items list. On load or when the URL changes, wishlist-page-loader applies the configured sort to the items from getWishlist before rendering. 4) focus_wishlist_item or focus_artwork_id: if present, the page automatically opens the artwork-detail-modal for the referenced artwork after getWishlist completes and the corresponding item is located. For focus_wishlist_item, the page looks up the item in items[] to derive the artwork_id; for focus_artwork_id, the page directly calls getArtworkDetails(artworkId) and opens the modal. Closing the modal clears the focus parameter from the URL. 5) Error and loading states are kept in component-level state only (not in the URL). Any time getWishlist, removeWishlistItem, getArtworkDetails, getRelatedArtworks, or addProductToCart are invoked, the calling component manages transient loading and error flags while leaving URL parameters as the source of navigable/user-shareable state.

components.functionality: Initializes the Wishlist page by calling getWishlist, applies URL-driven filter/sort/view state, and provides the resulting items and total_items to child components.
event_handlers: onInit: Call getWishlist without parameters. Store wishlist_id, items[], and total_items in local state. After data is available, apply wishlist_filter, wishlist_sort, and wishlist_view from URL to derive a visibleItems[] collection.

onUrlChange: When the URL changes (e.g., via back/forward navigation), re-read wishlist_filter, wishlist_sort, wishlist_view, and optional focus_* parameters, recompute visibleItems[], and notify dependent components.

onWishlistUpdated: When a child component removes an item (using removeWishlistItem) or otherwise triggers a re-fetch, optionally call getWishlist again, and propagate the updated items and total_items.

components.functionality: Displays the wishlist title, total number of items, and contextual hints. Provides quick access to continue browsing and reflects current filter/sort state.
event_handlers: onReceiveData: When wishlist-page-loader provides total_items and current filter/sort info, update the summary display.
onClickBrowseGallery: Navigate to gallery.html without additional parameters to allow the user to continue exploring artworks.
onClickResetFilters: Clear wishlist_filter and wishlist_sort URL parameters (retaining wishlist_view), then request wishlist-page-loader to recompute visibleItems[].

components.functionality: Manages filter (all/artworks/products), sort order, and layout view (list/grid) using URL parameters. Does not call APIs directly; instead, it updates URL state and instructs other components to re-render.
event_handlers: onInit: Read wishlist_filter, wishlist_sort, and wishlist_view from the URL. If any are missing, apply default values in memory and optionally update the URL to reflect them.
onFilterChange: When user selects a filter option, update the wishlist_filter URL parameter and notify wishlist-page-loader to recalculate visibleItems[].
onSortChange: When user selects a sort option, update the wishlist_sort URL parameter and notify wishlist-page-loader to re-sort visibleItems[].
onViewToggle: When user toggles between grid and list view, update wishlist_view in the URL and notify wishlist-list to re-render with the new layout.

components.functionality: Renders the list/grid of wishlist items based on the filtered and sorted visibleItems[] from wishlist-page-loader. Delegates per-item actions (remove, view, quick-add) to row-level components.
event_handlers: onReceiveItems: When wishlist-page-loader provides visibleItems[], render each as a wishlist-item-row and handle empty/non-empty states by showing or hiding the wishlist-empty-state component.
onItemRemoved: When a wishlist-item-row signals a successful removal, remove the item from the displayed collection and update any local counts; if no items remain, show wishlist-empty-state.
onLayoutChange: When wishlist-toolbar changes wishlist_view, re-render the list using the requested layout style without altering underlying data.

components.functionality: Represents a single wishlist item (artwork or product). Displays thumbnail, title, price_display, and item_type-specific actions such as viewing details, viewing in gallery, quick view (for artworks), quick add to cart (for products), and removal from wishlist.
event_handlers: onClickTitleOrThumbnailForProduct: If item_type === 'product', navigate to product.html?id={product.id}.
onClickViewInGalleryForArtwork: If item_type === 'artwork', navigate to gallery.html?category={artwork.category}.
onClickQuickViewForArtwork: Open artwork-detail-modal with artworkId = artwork.id or wishlist_item_id, optionally updating focus_* URL parameters.
onClickRemove: Call removeWishlistItem with wishlistItemId = wishlist_item_id. On success, emit an onItemRemoved event with the removed id and updated wishlist_count returned from the API.
onQuickAddToCart: If item_type === 'product' and product.available is true, delegate to wishlist-quick-add-to-cart-form with the current product data.
onProductUnavailable: If product.available is false, disable the quick add-to-cart action and optionally show a non-blocking message (no API call).

components.functionality: Embedded within each product-type wishlist item, this component lets the user configure quantity and optional size/frame labels (using product.size_options and product.frame_options) and add the product directly to the cart via addProductToCart.
event_handlers: onInit: Initialize local form state using product.id, default quantity (e.g., 1 in local state), and product.default_size_label / product.default_frame_label when available. If product.size_options or product.frame_options exist, populate dropdown choices from those arrays.

onChangeQuantity: Update the internal quantity state when the user changes it; validate that it is a positive integer and, if applicable, not greater than product.stock_quantity when that value is provided.
onChangeSizeOption: Update selected_size_label based on user selection; this label is passed to addProductToCart exactly as selected.
onChangeFrameOption: Update selected_frame_label based on user selection; this label is passed to addProductToCart exactly as selected.

onSubmit: Call addProductToCart with productId = product.id, quantity, selected_size_label (or null/empty if not applicable), and selected_frame_label (or null/empty if not applicable). While awaiting the response, show a local loading state. On success, display a confirmation message using message and subtotal_display returned from the API and optionally surface item_count. On failure (success === false), show the error message without leaving the page.

components.functionality: Displays detailed information for a selected artwork-type wishlist item, including labels, pricing, and linked products. Optionally shows related artworks using getRelatedArtworks and provides navigation to the gallery.
event_handlers: onOpenWithWishlistItem: When triggered from a wishlist-item-row, extract artworkId = item.artwork.id and call getArtworkDetails(artworkId). Populate modal fields with artwork, category_label, medium_label, size_label, price_display, is_in_favorites, is_in_wishlist, and linked_products.

onOpenFromUrl: When focus_artwork_id or focus_wishlist_item is present in the URL and getWishlist has already loaded, resolve the artworkId and call getArtworkDetails(artworkId) automatically, then open the modal.
onDetailsLoaded: After getArtworkDetails returns, optionally call getRelatedArtworks(artworkId) to fill a related artworks section within the modal.
onClickRelatedArtworkViewInGallery: For each related artwork, navigate to gallery.html?category={related_artwork.category}.
onClickViewInGalleryMainArtwork: Navigate to gallery.html?category={artwork.category} for the main artwork shown in the modal.
onClose: Close the modal and remove focus_* parameters from the URL (if present) without reloading the page.

components.functionality: Shows when the wishlist has no items. Encourages users to browse the gallery (and, via global navigation, the shop) to add items to their wishlist. Does not display any static sample items or data.
event_handlers: onShow: Render only when total_items === 0 as provided by getWishlist or after removals.
onClickBrowseGallery: Navigate to gallery.html without additional parameters to let the user start exploring artworks.
onNavigateHeaderShop: Rely on global header navigation to reach shop pages; this component does not handle shop navigation URLs directly.

components.functionality: Provides persistent navigational links relevant to the wishlist context, primarily for continuing gallery browsing. Cart navigation is handled by the global header after add-to-cart actions.
event_handlers: onClickBrowseGallery: Navigate to gallery.html, optionally including a category parameter when invoked from an artwork-specific context (e.g., gallery.html?category={artwork.category}).

onClickBackToWishlist: When coming from other pages with a 'Back to wishlist' link, ensure navigation returns to wishlist.html and the existing URL parameters (wishlist_filter, wishlist_sort, wishlist_view) are preserved if present.
onClickCartLinkInHeader: No direct handler; clicking the global Cart link navigates to cart.html and uses the existing cart state updated by addProductToCart.

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered fixed-width layout, likely based on a 12-column grid within a max-width container (~960–1140px), with a two-column split in the main about section (image column ~35–40%, text column ~60–65%).
layout.layout_pattern: All wishlist components sit inside a centered, fixed-width main content column that mirrors the portfolio mockup. The global header (artist name + main navigation) remains at the very top, centered and spacious. The Wishlist page content begins below this, in a clearly separated section. Within the wishlist section, the arrangement is vertically linear: first a subtle top row with contextual links (Wishlist navigation links), then a concise summary header, followed by a low-profile toolbar, and finally the main wishlist grid. Each wishlist item internally uses a two-column structure (image on the left, text and actions on the right) that echoes the about-section layout from the mockup. The artwork detail modal reuses the same two-column, image-left/text-right pattern in an overlay.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All wishlist components sit inside a centered, fixed-width main content column that mirrors the portfolio mockup. The global header (artist name + main navigation) remains at the very top, centered and spacious. The Wishlist page content begins below this, in a clearly separated section. Within the wishlist section, the arrangement is vertically linear: first a subtle top row with contextual links (Wishlist navigation links), then a concise summary header, followed by a low-profile toolbar, and finally the main wishlist grid. Each wishlist item internally uses a two-column structure (image on the left, text and actions on the right) that echoes the about-section layout from the mockup. The artwork detail modal reuses the same two-column, image-left/text-right pattern in an overlay.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The wishlist content sits within a max-width container of about 960–1040px, centered with generous left and right margins on wider screens (roughly 15–20% of viewport as whitespace on each side on large desktops). The upper part of the page (global name and nav) still claims roughly the top 25–30% of the initial viewport with abundant whitespace. The wishlist section starts with a generous vertical gap (48–64px) beneath the navigation. Within the wishlist section, summary header, toolbar, and grid are separated by 24–32px vertical spacing, maintaining an airy feel. Within each wishlist item, the left image column occupies about 35–40% of the card width and the right text/actions column about 60–65%, mirroring the about-section proportions. The artwork detail modal uses a similar 40/60 split and spans roughly 70–80% of the viewport width, centered with large margins around it.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density stays low to moderate, following the mockup’s editorial character. Major blocks (summary header, toolbar, grid) have clear breathing room with 32–48px vertical separations. Text lines for titles and descriptions are set in reasonably wide columns (around 60–70 characters per line) with generous line-height. Wishlist items show enough information to be useful but remain visually calm, with only a few key actions shown per item. The empty state collapses the interface into a single focal message with plenty of whitespace above and below, reinforcing the minimalist feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow starts at the top center with the global artist name and primary navigation, then moves downward into the Wishlist section. Within the wishlist, the eye first encounters the subtle top-right navigation links (analogous to the CV link – slightly offset and colored) as a secondary focal point, then drops to the left-aligned wishlist summary header. From there, the gaze continues down to the toolbar and then into the wishlist grid. Within each item, the eye hits the image thumbnail on the left first (strongest visual element), then moves right to the title and details, and finally down and right to the quick-add-to-cart action. When opened, the artwork detail modal temporarily becomes the main focal area, with the large artwork image on the left pulling attention first, then the text and action area on the right. Overall reading remains predominantly vertical, with left-to-right sweeps over each two-column structure, preserving the F-pattern from the mockup.

layout.overall_description: The Wishlist page lives inside the same clean, centered portfolio frame as the rest of the site. At the very top of the viewport, the artist’s name and main navigation remain centered with generous whitespace, establishing an airy, gallery-like header. Beneath this, after a comfortable vertical gap of about 56px, the wishlist section begins inside a max-width, centered container. At the top-right of this container, a small strip of navigation links lets the user jump back to the gallery or shop—these links sit lightly above the main wishlist title, much like a CV link in an editorial layout. Aligned to the left edge of the content column below, a minimalist summary header shows the word “Wishlist” and a subtle item count, with plenty of breathing room above and below. Directly under that, a slim toolbar spans most of the content width, offering filters and sorting but keeping a light touch visually. The main body of the page is a vertical stack of wishlist items arranged in a soft grid/list hybrid: on larger screens, items align in a single-column vertical list, each item styled as a two-column card with the artwork image on the left and description plus actions on the right, echoing the portrait-and-bio layout from the mockup. The generous side margins and vertical spacing keep every card separate and easy to scan. If an item is clicked, a centered modal overlay appears, dimming the background and presenting the artwork in a larger two-column layout—image on the left, detailed info on the right—maintaining the same proportions and calm typography. When the wishlist is empty, the grid area is replaced by a single, centered empty-state message with lots of whitespace, making the absence of items feel intentional and serene rather than cluttered. The whole page reads as a minimalist editorial spread dedicated to saved works, with a clear top-down, then left-to-right visual flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wishlist page loader appears as a full-viewport, semi-transparent veil that sits on top of all other wishlist content when data is being fetched. It covers from the very top of the browser window to the bottom, including the artist’s name and navigation, aligning with the overall centered layout but dimming it. At the center of the viewport, aligned both horizontally and vertically, a small, unobtrusive loading indicator (such as a thin-line spinner or minimal wordmark) is displayed. The loader respects the site’s minimalism: plenty of empty space around the indicator, no heavy panels or borders, and no shift in the underlying page layout—the content beneath remains fixed in place, simply subdued.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay that covers the entire viewport while active, positioned above all other components with a high z-index; non-scrolling and disappears once content is loaded.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wishlist summary header sits near the top of the wishlist content section, inside the centered main container. Horizontally, it is left-aligned within that container, starting roughly 48–56px below the bottom edge of the global navigation, providing a clear visual separation from the header area. It spans about 70–80% of the container’s width, leaving comfortable whitespace on the right. The main title (e.g., “Wishlist”) is set on a single line with thin, elegant typography; any supporting information such as the item count is placed on the same baseline to the right of the title or just below it in a smaller, lighter style. There is about 16–24px of internal vertical spacing between title and subtitle, and 24–32px of space below the header before the toolbar begins, following the airy rhythm of the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content flow; scrolls away naturally with the page content. On very small screens, the header text may center-align while still retaining generous top and bottom margins.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wishlist toolbar is placed directly beneath the summary header, separated by about 24–32px of vertical space. It runs horizontally across the content area, aligning its left edge with the header title and spanning roughly 80–90% of the container width, leaving a slim margin on the right to reinforce the sense of openness. Controls (filters, sort dropdown, maybe a view toggle) are arranged in a single row with ample spacing (16–24px) between elements, using thin typography and subtle outlines or underlines rather than heavy buttons. On desktop, filter controls are left-aligned, with secondary controls (such as sort) floated or flex-aligned to the right, creating a subtle horizontal tension across the bar. The toolbar height is modest—around 48px of content height—sitting lightly in the layout with a neutral background that matches the page. There is another 24–32px of vertical space below the toolbar before the wishlist grid starts.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with content by default; on larger screens it can become sticky after the user scrolls past the summary header, pinning to the top of the viewport within the main content area to keep filters accessible, while maintaining the same width and left alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wishlist list (grid) occupies the main body of the wishlist section and sits directly below the toolbar with a vertical gap of about 24–32px. It fills the width of the centered container, but individual items are laid out as a vertical stack of full-width cards rather than dense, multi-column tiles, to keep the editorial feel. Each item card spans roughly 100% of the content width on desktop, with internal padding of 16–24px and a light bottom divider or subtle shadow to define separation. Cards are spaced with 24–32px vertical gaps between them, maintaining the low-density rhythm. Inside each card, content is arranged in a two-column layout: the left column (about 35–40% of the card width) is reserved for the artwork thumbnail or product image, filling the column vertically with a fixed aspect ratio and a subtle border or hairline frame reminiscent of the portrait photo in the mockup; the right column (about 60–65% of the card width) holds the item title, brief description, metadata (price, size), and the quick add-to-cart form aligned toward the bottom or right. On tablet-sized screens, the grid still uses this two-column card layout but may reduce horizontal padding; on narrow mobile screens, the list becomes single-column inside each card: image stacked on top, text and actions below, with maintained vertical spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static scrollable content within the main page; the list naturally extends downward and can handle any number of items. Cards reflow responsively, collapsing the two-column internal layout into a vertical stack on small screens while retaining the same paddings and vertical rhythm.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each wishlist item row is designed as a minimalist card that echoes the about section’s image-and-text structure. The card occupies the full width of the wishlist list column, with 16–24px of internal padding on all sides and rounded or crisp corners depending on the site’s system but always with a very subtle visual edge. The card is divided into two primary vertical columns: on the left, taking roughly 35–40% of the card width, a portrait- or landscape-oriented artwork image is positioned near the top, framed by a thin border and comfortable internal whitespace; it aligns visually with the top of the text on the right. On the right, occupying roughly 60–65% of the width, text is arranged in a top-to-bottom flow: title at the top, then optional subtitle or artist/series name, then a short description or key attributes, followed by secondary metadata like price or availability. The quick add-to-cart form is placed toward the lower right of this column, slightly offset from the main text so it feels like a secondary focal point, similar to the CV link in the bio layout. Vertical spacing between text lines is generous (8–16px), and there is sufficient space below the last text/action line and the bottom of the card (16–24px) so nothing feels cramped. On hover (desktop), the entire card may show a very subtle elevation or border darkening, reinforcing its clickability without breaking the minimalist aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Child of the wishlist list; participates in the list’s responsive behavior. On mobile, the item row collapses to a vertical structure: image on top, then title and text, with the quick add-to-cart form at the bottom, all centered or left-aligned with preserved spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quick add-to-cart form is anchored within the right column of each wishlist item row. Horizontally, it is aligned to the right edge of the text column, creating a clear focal point at the lower right of the card. Vertically, it sits slightly below the main title and key metadata, but above any less important notes, so it is easy to find without overpowering the descriptive text—mirroring the way the CV download link is a secondary but noticeable element in the mockup. The form is compact: typically one quantity control and a minimalist button. Elements are arranged inline or in a short horizontal cluster with 8–16px spacing between controls. The add-to-cart button is styled with a subtle emphasis (e.g., a thin bordered button or a filled button in a muted accent color) but kept small and tidy. There is 8–12px of space between the last line of descriptive text and the form, and another 16–24px between the form and the bottom edge of the card. On narrow screens, the form stretches to nearly full width of the card and sits directly below the text, left-aligned or center-aligned depending on the overall mobile typographic alignment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within each wishlist item row; aligns with the right side in wider layouts and reflows beneath text in a vertical stack on smaller screens. It does not stick or float independently of its parent card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The artwork detail modal appears as a centered overlay that dims the entire page behind it with a soft, semi-transparent backdrop, while maintaining the minimalist aesthetic. The modal content window spans approximately 70–80% of the viewport width on desktop and is vertically centered, leaving generous margins (at least 64px) at the top and bottom. Inside the modal, content is arranged in a clean two-column layout, very similar to the about section: the left column occupies about 40% of the modal width and is dedicated to a larger version of the artwork image, framed by a thin border and sufficient padding (16–24px) from the modal edges; the right column, about 60% of the width, contains the artwork title near the top, followed by detailed information (description, dimensions, medium, price) in neatly spaced paragraphs, and any primary actions (such as Add to Cart, View in Shop, or Close) aligned towards the lower right of the column. The top-right corner of the modal includes a minimalist close icon, separated by a small padding (12–16px) from the edges. Vertical spacing between text blocks follows the same 8–16px rhythm, with substantial whitespace around the entire content area. On tablets and small laptops, the two-column proportion remains but may narrow slightly; on mobile, the modal content stacks into a vertical flow with the image on top and text below, still centered within a narrower width.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed to the viewport with a high z-index, centered both horizontally and vertically. The background page is non-scrollable while the modal is open. If content height exceeds viewport height on smaller screens, the modal becomes vertically scrollable within its frame, while remaining centered horizontally.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wishlist empty state takes over the space normally occupied by the wishlist list when there are no items. It is centered within the main content container, starting roughly where the first list item would normally appear—about 24–32px below the toolbar. The main message (e.g., “Your wishlist is empty”) is center-aligned text placed near the vertical center of the visible content area (not necessarily the full viewport), with ample whitespace above and below (at least 48–64px). A brief explanatory subtext and a primary link or button (such as “Browse Gallery” or “Shop Artworks”) sit below the main headline, each separated by 8–16px of vertical space. The overall block occupies no more than 40–50% of the container’s height, leaving generous blank space on all sides, maintaining the gallery-like calm. The navigation links bar and summary header remain visible above, so the user context is clear, but the empty state becomes the principal focal point of the content area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block that replaces the wishlist list region when no items are present. It scrolls with the page if the viewport is short, but typically appears wholly in view on most screens due to its modest height.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wishlist navigation links bar is positioned at the very top of the wishlist content section, inside the same centered container as the summary header. It sits horizontally aligned to the right edge of the container, approximately 16–24px above the baseline of the wishlist summary header title, acting as a subtle secondary element similar to the CV link in the mockup. The bar itself is visually light—no heavy background—just a horizontal row of 2–3 text links (e.g., “Back to Gallery”, “Shop”) with thin typography and a muted accent color (such as blue), spaced about 16–24px apart. The clickable region is modest but comfortable (at least 32px high), and there is clear whitespace above and below the links, preserving the airy character. On small screens, these links may wrap to a second line or align left above the header, but they retain their visual subtlety and do not form a heavy top bar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and part of the natural vertical flow; it scrolls with the page. On narrow mobile viewports, the links may reflow to a stacked or left-aligned layout above the summary header while keeping similar vertical spacing.

name: Commissions
title: Commissions
description: Page for visitors to understand commission options and submit a commission request form that is processed and acknowledged inline.

core_features: Load and display commission information (intro, pricing tiers, process steps) using the commissions page content interface.
Display a prominent primary action that moves focus to the commission request form and optionally updates the URL state to reflect that the form section is active.
Render a commission request form whose selectable options (artwork type, medium, and budget guidance) are dynamically loaded via the commission form options interface.
Collect commission request details including artwork type, medium, budget, preferred completion date, description, and client contact information, bound to the CommissionRequest data model fields.
Validate commission request input client-side before submission (required fields, data types) and pass valid data to the commission submission interface.
Submit the commission request via the commission submission interface and handle the response, including success state, returned commission_request_id, and status.
Display an inline confirmation or error message, powered by the submission response and/or URL parameters, without redirecting to a different page.

Provide navigation from the Commissions page to the home page (via site logo click) and to the portfolio/gallery page (via a dedicated link), using standard anchor navigation and optional URL parameters.
user_workflows:
Workflow A: Read commission information and then open the request form
1. User navigates to commissions.html (e.g., via header or footer link).
2. On initial load, the page calls getCommissionsPageContent with no parameters and waits for intro_markdown, pricing_tiers_markdown, and process_steps.
3. The page concurrently calls getCommissionFormOptions to retrieve artwork_types, mediums, and budget_hint used for the form.
4. The commission information section renders the returned intro_markdown, pricing_tiers_markdown, and process_steps as read-only content.
5. The user reads the information and then clicks the primary action button that is configured to focus/scroll to the commission form.
6. The click handler scrolls or jumps to the form component and updates the URL (e.g., by adding or updating a section-related parameter) so that the active section is reflected in the URL without a full page reload.

Workflow B: Directly access and submit the commission form
1. User opens commissions.html with an URL parameter indicating that the form section should be active (for example, a section parameter set by a previous click or an external deep link).
2. On load, the page parses URL parameters to determine that the form should be scrolled into view or focused after initial content is rendered.
3. getCommissionsPageContent and getCommissionFormOptions are called as in Workflow A.
4. Once form options are available, the form fields are populated with the dynamic artwork_types, mediums, and budget_hint from getCommissionFormOptions. Any URL parameters that indicate pre-selected values (such as artwork type or medium) are used to initialize corresponding form fields.
5. The user fills in the required fields: artwork_type, medium, budget, preferred_completion_date, description, client_name, and client_email, following the structure of the CommissionRequest data model.
6. The user submits the form. The form component performs client-side validation (e.g., required fields not empty, numeric budget, valid date, and syntactically valid email).
7. If validation fails, inline validation messages are displayed and submission is blocked until issues are corrected. No API call is made in this case.
8. If validation passes, the form invokes submitCommissionRequest with the current field values.
9. When the response returns, if success is true, the component:
   - Stores the returned commission_request_id and status in its internal state.
   - Updates the URL parameters (e.g., adding or updating a parameter representing the submission status and the commission_request_id) using pushState/replaceState without reloading the page.
   - Notifies the inline confirmation component to display a success message based on the response message and/or status.
10. If success is false, the component does not change existing URL status parameters or sets a dedicated error-related parameter and triggers an inline error message component.

Workflow C: View submission confirmation after a page reload or from a shared link
1. User has previously submitted a commission request successfully; the page updated the URL to include the submission status and commission_request_id.
2. The user either refreshes the page or opens the same URL in a new browser instance.
3. On load, the page parses URL parameters and detects the presence of a submission status flag and possibly a commission_request_id.
4. getCommissionsPageContent and getCommissionFormOptions are called as usual to render the information and form.
5. After initial render, the inline confirmation component reads the parsed URL state and, if it finds a successful submission state, displays an acknowledgement banner/inline message referencing the existing submission (e.g., using the commission_request_id if present). No additional API calls are made because submitCommissionRequest is only invoked from a user-initiated form submission.
6. The user can stay on the page, navigate back to the form (which can be reset/cleared), or click the logo to return to the home page, or the portfolio link to view artworks.

Workflow D: Navigate from Commissions page to other site sections
1. While on commissions.html, the user decides to review existing artworks.
2. The user clicks the "View portfolio" link within the commission information or near the form.
3. The click triggers navigation to gallery.html, optionally including URL parameters if needed to reflect context (for example, a parameter indicating interest in commissions-related categories). No commission-related API calls are made as a result of this click.
4. Alternatively, the user clicks the site logo in the header while on commissions.html.
5. This click navigates to index.html without modifying or reusing commission form state, as no localStorage is used.
6. If the user later returns to commissions.html using navigation, the page re-initializes by parsing URL parameters and re-calling the content and form option interfaces.
interactions: Page load interaction: On initial load of commissions.html, the page initializes by parsing URL parameters (for example, parameters for active section, pre-selected artwork type or medium, or submission status) before rendering. It then triggers two asynchronous calls: one to getCommissionsPageContent and one to getCommissionFormOptions. When responses arrive, bound components update accordingly.

Primary action button interaction: Clicking the primary action button (for requesting a custom artwork) prevents default link behavior if using an anchor, updates the URL to reflect that the form section is active (e.g., by adding a section-related parameter), and programmatically scrolls or jumps to the commission form component. No API call is triggered by this click alone.

Form field interaction: As the user interacts with each form field (artwork_type, medium, budget, preferred_completion_date, description, client_name, client_email), the component maintains an internal state representation aligned with the CommissionRequest entity fields. Selecting an artwork type or medium uses options loaded dynamically from getCommissionFormOptions. Budget field interactions may display budget_hint from getCommissionFormOptions as helper text.

Form submission interaction: When the user submits the form, the submit event handler prevents default form submission, runs client-side validation logic, and if valid, calls submitCommissionRequest with the bound field values. The UI reflects a pending state (e.g., disabling the submit button) until the request resolves.

Submission response interaction (success): When submitCommissionRequest returns with success true, the form component updates its internal state with the returned commission_request_id, status, and message. It then updates the browser URL using pushState or replaceState to include parameters representing the successful submission state and commission_request_id. The inline confirmation component listens for this state change and renders a success acknowledgement within the page.

Submission response interaction (failure): If submitCommissionRequest returns with success false, the form component surfaces an inline error message and may optionally update a URL parameter representing an error state. The form remains populated so the user can adjust inputs and retry submission. No automatic retry occurs without user action.

Inline confirmation interaction: When the inline confirmation component detects a submission success or error state in the URL or receives notification from the form component, it displays the appropriate confirmation or error message. The user can dismiss this message (e.g., via a close control), which can clear the related status parameter from the URL via replaceState.

Navigation interactions: Clicking the site logo navigates to index.html. Clicking the "View portfolio" link navigates to gallery.html. These are standard anchor navigations that may include query parameters (such as a context hint) but do not invoke any commissions-related interfaces.

URL state change interaction: When the application updates state via URL parameters (for example, when activating the form section, recording a successful submission, or clearing status after dismissing a message), components that read URL state (form section controller, confirmation banner) react without requiring a full reload, ensuring that the URL remains a source of truth for the page state.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page uses URL query parameters as the primary mechanism to represent and restore view state. No localStorage or similar client-side persistence is used. Example URL parameters and their effects include:

- section: Represents which part of the Commissions page should be considered active. For example, when set to a value representing the form section, the page, after initial content rendering, scrolls or focuses the commission form component. The primary action button updates this parameter when clicked (e.g., using history.pushState or history.replaceState) without reloading the page.

- artwork_type and medium: If present, these parameters pre-select the corresponding form fields once getCommissionFormOptions has populated the available options. When the page loads, it parses these parameters and, after the options are available, matches them to returned artwork_types and mediums. If no matching option is found, the field remains unselected. These parameters can be used by external links to deep-link into the form with suggested selections.

- submission_status: Indicates whether a commission request has just been submitted successfully or encountered an error. After a successful call to submitCommissionRequest, the form component sets this parameter to a success-related value. If a failure occurs and the application chooses to reflect this in the URL, it can set the parameter to a failure-related value. On page reload, the inline confirmation component reads this parameter to decide whether to render a confirmation or error message.

- commission_request_id: When submitCommissionRequest returns success with a commission_request_id, the page updates this parameter to store the identifier in the URL. The inline confirmation component can display this value or use it to reference the submitted request in a generic way. On subsequent visits via the same URL, the presence of commission_request_id alongside submission_status allows the page to show that a specific request was submitted.

- error_code (optional): If the application wants to distinguish between different error types without storing static messages, it can use an error_code parameter set by the form component in response to a failed submitCommissionRequest call. The inline confirmation component can use this code to select an appropriate message mapping defined outside the static template.

State updates are always performed via the History API so that the current state is reflected in the URL bar without forcing a reload. On each initial page load, the commissions-page-level controller parses all relevant URL parameters, constructs an initial state object, and passes it as props to child components such as the form section controller and the confirmation banner. Child components read and react to this state but do not independently call any interfaces except the form component, which is the only one allowed to invoke submitCommissionRequest. All other dynamic data (intro content, pricing tiers, process steps, form options) is obtained exclusively through getCommissionsPageContent and getCommissionFormOptions.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading and state for the Commissions page. On load, parses URL parameters into a structured state object (including section, artwork_type, medium, submission_status, commission_request_id, and any error_code) and triggers calls to getCommissionsPageContent and getCommissionFormOptions. When the interface calls resolve, it passes the resulting data and derived state down to the information, form, and confirmation components.

Displays commission-related informational content including introductory text, pricing tier explanations, and a step-by-step process description. Renders intro_markdown and pricing_tiers_markdown as rich text and iterates over process_steps to show individual steps. Provides a primary action control that, when activated, scrolls/jumps to the commission form section and informs the page controller to update the URL section parameter. Includes a link to navigate to the gallery page.

Implements the primary action for starting a commission request. When clicked, prevents default navigation if necessary, signals the page controller to set the section parameter in the URL to represent the form section, and triggers a smooth scroll or direct jump to the form component's anchor.

Renders the list of commission process steps provided by getCommissionsPageContent.process_steps. Each step displays a title and description. This component is purely informational and does not perform navigation or API calls.

Provides the interactive form for users to submit a CommissionRequest. It renders select inputs for artwork_type and medium using artwork_types and mediums from getCommissionFormOptions, a numeric or text input for budget that displays budget_hint, a date input for preferred_completion_date, a multi-line text area for description, and text inputs for client_name and client_email. It initializes default selections based on URL parameters (such as artwork_type and medium) when those values match available options. On submit, it validates all required fields and, if valid, calls submitCommissionRequest with the current field values. It then handles the response by updating local form state (including pending, success, or error), notifying the confirmation component, and letting the page controller adjust URL parameters to reflect submission_status and commission_request_id.

Controls visibility and focus behavior of the commission form section based on URL state. It listens to URL parameter changes (particularly the section parameter) and instructs the view to scroll to or highlight the commission-request-form component when the form section is active. It also passes pre-selected field values derived from URL parameters (artwork_type, medium) down to the form as initial values.

Displays an inline confirmation or error message related to commission request submissions. It observes the combination of local submission events from commission-request-form and URL parameters such as submission_status, commission_request_id, and error_code. When a successful submission is detected, it renders a success acknowledgement referencing the submission. When an error state is detected, it renders an error message. It provides a dismiss control that, when activated, informs the page controller to remove or update the submission-related URL parameters via replaceState so that the feedback is no longer shown on subsequent reloads.

Encapsulates navigation elements specific to the Commissions page: a link wired to the site logo that navigates to index.html, and a "View portfolio" link that navigates to gallery.html. It can append context-related URL parameters if required by the broader site (for example, indicating that the user came from the Commissions page), but does not initiate any commissions-related API calls.

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered fixed-width layout, likely based on a 12-column grid within a max-width container (~960–1140px), with a two-column split in the main about section (image column ~35–40%, text column ~60–65%).
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a vertical, centered layout. At the top, within the page controller, the artist’s name and site-level navigation (commissions-navigation-links) are centered and given a generous vertical envelope. Below this header area, the main content begins with a two-column band that mirrors the mockup’s about section: on the left, the commissions-process-list occupies the role of the portrait (a tall, vertically structured element), and on the right, the commissions-info-section holds explanatory copy. Within the right column, the commissions-primary-action-link is placed near the top of the text column, slightly offset from the main paragraph flow to echo the CV link in the mockup. Beneath this two-column band, after a clear vertical gap, a full-width but centered single-column section contains the commission-request-form and commission-submission-feedback, controlled by the commission-form-section-controller.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a vertical, centered layout. At the top, within the page controller, the artist’s name and site-level navigation (commissions-navigation-links) are centered and given a generous vertical envelope. Below this header area, the main content begins with a two-column band that mirrors the mockup’s about section: on the left, the commissions-process-list occupies the role of the portrait (a tall, vertically structured element), and on the right, the commissions-info-section holds explanatory copy. Within the right column, the commissions-primary-action-link is placed near the top of the text column, slightly offset from the main paragraph flow to echo the CV link in the mockup. Beneath this two-column band, after a clear vertical gap, a full-width but centered single-column section contains the commission-request-form and commission-submission-feedback, controlled by the commission-form-section-controller.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The page controller defines a centered max-width content column (~960–1140px) with wide left and right margins, preserving a gallery-like feel. The top 25–30% of the first viewport is allocated to the header (artist name + navigation) with ample whitespace above and below the nav. The two-column commission info band fills most of the remaining viewport height: the left process list column occupies roughly 35–40% of the content width; the right info column takes about 60–65%. Vertical spacing between header and the two-column band, and between that band and the form section, is generous (approximately 48–64px), ensuring clear separation between conceptual sections. The form section uses a single column that spans about 65–75% of the max-width container, centered, with comfortable padding around form fields.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density remains low to moderate throughout. The navigation links are spaced with ample breathing room. The two-column band presents a structured but airy layout: the process list items have generous vertical spacing and internal padding, and the informational copy uses an editorial-like line length and line height. The primary action link sits alone or with minimal surrounding content so it can stand out without clutter.
