The form fields are stacked with clear vertical separation, avoiding a compressed appearance; the inline feedback area is given enough room to be readable without crowding the form controls.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow starts at the top center with the artist’s name, then drops directly to the centered navigation links. From there, the eye descends to the two-column commission section. Within this band, the left column process list acts as the first focal object (analogous to the portrait image), drawing attention due to its vertical card-like structure. The eye then moves rightward to the commissions-info-section to read details. The blue commissions-primary-action-link near the top of the right column becomes a secondary focal point, attracting attention after the initial scan of the heading and first lines of text. After absorbing this area, the user continues downward to the single-column form section where the commission-request-form is the primary focus and the inline feedback appears immediately beneath it, keeping the user’s attention within the request/confirmation area. The overall reading path is primarily top-to-bottom, with a left-to-right scan within the two-column band, echoing an F-pattern reading rhythm.

layout.overall_description: The Commissions page presents as a calm, centered canvas with a strong editorial sensibility. At the top, within a broad field of whitespace, the artist’s name and navigation links sit in the middle of the screen, aligned on a narrow vertical axis and framed by wide side margins, creating a gallery-like entry into the content. Scrolling slightly, the layout opens into a balanced two-column section: on the left, a tall, card-like list of commission steps guides the eye vertically; on the right, a wider text column explains commission options, with a subtly highlighted primary action link near the top that echoes the feel of a CV download link. The two columns sit comfortably within a fixed-width content container, with generous gaps above, below, and between elements, so nothing feels cramped. Below this, separated by a clear vertical pause, a centered form section spans a comfortable portion of the width, presenting the commission request form as a focused, single-column experience. Inline submission feedback appears directly underneath, within the same visual block, so acknowledgment feels immediate and contained. The whole page reads as a carefully paced sequence—from oriented understanding at the top, through process explanation, to a calm, pared-back form—guided by whitespace and a restrained visual hierarchy.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller functions as the overarching canvas for the Commissions page. It centers all content within a max-width container of roughly 960–1140px, aligned to the middle of the viewport horizontally, with wide left and right margins that remain mostly empty on larger screens. Vertically, it establishes a generous rhythm: a tall header area at the top (occupying about the top quarter of the initial viewport) for the artist name and the commissions-navigation-links, followed by a clear gap of around 48–64px before the main two-column commission section, and another similar gap before the form section governed by the commission-form-section-controller. All child components (navigation, two-column band with info and process list, form section with feedback) are stacked in a linear top-to-bottom flow with consistent section margins and internal padding based on the 8px spacing scale. On smaller screens, the controller allows sections to collapse into a single-column layout while maintaining centered alignment and comfortable vertical spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a static, scrollable container that centers content, enforces the fixed-width grid, and manages consistent vertical spacing between sections; it does not have sticky or fixed behavior itself.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The commissions-info-section occupies the right column of the main two-column band, mirroring the biography text area in the mockup. It begins approximately 48–64px below the navigation, aligned so that its top edge is level with the top of the commissions-process-list on the left. Within the overall content width, this section spans about 60–65% of the available horizontal space, starting from the centerline and extending to the right, leaving a comfortable inner gutter (roughly 32–40px) between it and the left column. The text inside is left-aligned, with an editorial column feel: comfortable line length (around 60–70 characters), generous line height, and 16–24px internal padding on larger screens. The section’s heading (e.g., 'Commissions') sits at the top, followed by explanatory paragraphs arranged in a vertical stack with clear spacing between paragraphs. The commissions-primary-action-link is positioned near the top of this area—either just below the heading or aligned to the upper-right portion of the text block—slightly offset from the main paragraph flow so it reads as a secondary focal point rather than part of the primary text stream. On smaller screens, this section expands to full width and appears above or below the process list, while retaining ample vertical spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the document flow, participating in a two-column layout on larger screens and gracefully stacking to a single-column layout on smaller viewports while remaining right-aligned in the large-screen grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The primary action link (e.g., 'Start a Commission Request' or 'View Commission Details') is embedded within the commissions-info-section but visually treated like the CV download link in the mockup. It is positioned near the top of the right text column, above or just before the main body paragraphs. Horizontally, it aligns to the left edge of the text column but is given a small amount of top and bottom spacing (around 16–24px) so it floats slightly apart from the surrounding body text. If the info section includes a heading, the link sits directly beneath the heading with a modest gap, making it easy to catch in a brief scan. The link text is concise and visually distinct (e.g., blue text with an inline icon) yet remains lightweight to preserve the minimalist aesthetic. Its width is naturally defined by its text; it does not span the full column, which keeps it visually compact and clearly tappable. On mobile, it remains near the top of the content, still immediately following the section heading with consistent vertical spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline-block element within the right column; it respects text flow but is given intentional vertical margins to stand apart as a focal action without changing the surrounding layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The commissions-process-list forms the left column of the main two-column band, taking the place of the portrait image in the mockup as a tall, visually stable anchor. It occupies roughly 35–40% of the available content width, starting from the left edge of the centered content container and extending toward the center, with a consistent horizontal gutter (around 32–40px) separating it from the right column. Vertically, its top edge is aligned with the top of the commissions-info-section so the two columns feel balanced. The process list is arranged in a vertical card-like container with a subtle border or background tint and 16–24px internal padding on all sides, echoing the framed feel of the portrait. Each step in the process is stacked top-to-bottom with ample spacing (16–24px) between steps, possibly with small minimalist icons or step numbers aligned along the left edge inside the card, reinforcing a calm, ordered reading. The list likely extends through a substantial portion of the right column’s height, contributing a columnar visual mass that draws initial attention. On smaller screens, this card stacks above or below the info section, occupying nearly full width but still centered with generous side padding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the two-column layout; on larger screens it locks to the left column position, and on smaller viewports it reflows into a full-width block stacking with the info section while maintaining its tall, card-like presence.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The commission-request-form sits in its own dedicated section below the two-column information band. It begins after a generous vertical gap of approximately 48–64px, creating a clear conceptual transition from 'understanding commissions' to 'taking action.' Within the centered max-width container, the form itself is constrained to about 65–75% of the content width and horizontally centered, leaving wide whitespace on both sides to maintain the airy, gallery-like feel. The form fields are arranged in a single column, stacked vertically from top to bottom, with consistent spacing (12–24px) between fields. Labels and inputs align to the same left edge, producing a clean, invisible column grid. The submit button is placed at the bottom of the form, aligned with the left edge of the fields or centered within the form’s width depending on the overall aesthetic, but always within the same central grouping. There is comfortable padding (16–24px) between the outer boundaries of the form’s container and its contents, and a clear gap (16–24px) below the submit button where the commission-submission-feedback will appear. On mobile, the form expands to nearly full width but remains centered with symmetric side padding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the commission-form-section-controller. It scrolls naturally with the page and does not use sticky or floating positioning; it simply occupies a dedicated, clearly separated section of the vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The commission-form-section-controller defines the entire lower form area as a distinct, cohesive section. It starts approximately 48–64px below the bottom edges of the commissions-info-section and commissions-process-list, spanning the full width of the centered content container. Within this section, content is arranged as a single vertical column: any optional section heading (e.g., 'Request a Commission') at the top, followed by a small vertical gap, then the commission-request-form, and directly beneath that, the commission-submission-feedback. The section controller maintains generous top and bottom padding (32–48px), ensuring the form block feels like a calm, focused area separate from the informational content above. Horizontally, it keeps the form and feedback centered and constrained in width (around 65–75% of the container) while leaving the remaining width as clean whitespace. This section reads as the final primary zone of the page before any footer content, giving the request action clear visual emphasis.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section-level container that organizes and spaces the form and its feedback. It governs vertical spacing and width constraints but does not introduce sticky or overlay behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The commission-submission-feedback component appears inline within the commission-form-section-controller, directly beneath the commission-request-form’s submit button. It occupies the same horizontal width as the form (about 65–75% of the content container, centered) so it feels like an extension of the form area. When visible, it sits within a narrow vertical band, starting roughly 12–16px below the submit button, with enough space around the text to be legible without crowding the controls. If styled as a subtle message block, it may include light padding and a thin border, maintaining the minimalist aesthetic. The feedback does not push content far away; instead, it gently expands the form section downward, keeping acknowledgment within the user’s immediate eye line. When not active, this area either collapses or maintains a minimal reserved space so the layout remains stable. On mobile, its behavior is identical, following the same centered column as the form fields.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, dynamically appearing element within the form section. It occupies standard document flow beneath the form, expanding or contracting the section height when messages appear or disappear, without overlaying other content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The commissions-navigation-links component is placed near the top of the page, within the header area controlled by the commissions-page-controller. It sits centered horizontally, directly beneath the artist’s name or site title, with a modest vertical gap of around 16–24px between the title and the nav. The nav itself is a horizontal string of minimalist text links (e.g., 'Work', 'About', 'Commissions'), evenly spaced with 24–32px gaps between items to preserve the clean, airy feel. The entire navigation row spans no more than 50–60% of the content width, remaining tightly centered and surrounded by ample whitespace above and below, so it feels light and unobtrusive. The nav sits roughly in the middle of the initial viewport height when combined with the title and top padding, occupying the top 25–30% of the page before the main content begins. On smaller screens, the links may wrap or stack while still centered with generous vertical spacing between rows, preserving the minimal, gallery-like presentation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the top of the document within the header area; it scrolls away with the page and does not stick or float. It maintains centered alignment and consistent spacing regardless of viewport width.

name: About
title: About
description: Displays the artist’s biography, statement, and studio information, and provides an Exhibitions & Events section with filterable, sortable exhibitions that can be added to a Visit Plan.

core_features: Load and display the artist’s biography, statement, and studio information using getAboutPageContent.
Provide section-level navigation (e.g., Overview vs. Exhibitions & Events) that can be controlled by the section URL parameter.
List exhibitions and events in an Exhibitions & Events section using getExhibitions, with date, status, and sort controls.
Allow date-based filtering for exhibitions via From (and optional To) fields plus an Apply action, wired to getExhibitions.from_date and getExhibitions.to_date.
Support filtering by exhibition status (e.g., upcoming, ongoing, past) via getExhibitions.status.
Allow sorting exhibitions by date and other supported sort keys via getExhibitions.sort.
Link each exhibition entry to its exhibition_detail.html page, passing exhibitionId in the URL.
Allow adding an exhibition to the Visit Plan via addExhibitionToVisitPlan, reflecting the result in the UI.
Provide a Visit Plan link from the Exhibitions & Events section, navigating to visit_plan.html.
Provide a link from the About page to gallery.html to view the full portfolio.
Sync filter, sort, and section state with URL parameters so state is preserved on reload and shareable via URL.
user_workflows:
1) Viewing the artist overview:
   - User navigates to about.html (directly or via header/footer navigation).
   - Page initializes and calls getAboutPageContent with no parameters.
   - The Biography, Artist Statement, and Studio Information sections render with the returned markdown content.
   - The default section/tab (e.g., Overview) is active unless a section URL parameter specifies otherwise.
   - User can click an internal control to navigate to Exhibitions & Events, which updates the URL section parameter and switches the visible section.

2) Opening the page focused on Exhibitions & Events from another page:
   - User clicks an "About" link on index.html that includes a section query parameter such as about.html?section=exhibitions.
   - On load, the page-url-state-manager reads section=exhibitions and activates the Exhibitions & Events tab/section instead of the overview.
   - The exhibitions-filter-form reads any from_date, to_date, status, and sort parameters in the URL (if present).
   - Exhibitions-list triggers getExhibitions with these parameter values.
   - The user sees the filtered, sorted list in the Exhibitions & Events section.

3) Filtering and sorting exhibitions by date and status:
   - User has the Exhibitions & Events section active.
   - User sets or changes the From date field (and optionally To date), selects an exhibition status, and chooses a sort option (e.g., by date).
   - User clicks the Apply control on the exhibitions-filter-form.
   - The form handler updates the URL query parameters (from_date, to_date, status, sort) accordingly using URL-state updates.
   - The exhibitions-list component listens for the updated filter state, calls getExhibitions with the new parameters, and re-renders the list based on the response.
   - The browser back/forward buttons move between previous filter states because each change updates the URL.

4) Viewing exhibition details:
   - User scans the exhibitions list in the Exhibitions & Events section.
   - User clicks an exhibition title or a dedicated "Details" action on an exhibition-card.
   - The page navigates to exhibition_detail.html with a URL parameter such as exhibition_detail.html?exhibitionId=<selected-exhibition.id>.
   - No additional data is fetched on the About page; the detail page is responsible for loading the exhibition details.

5) Adding an exhibition to the Visit Plan:
   - Within the Exhibitions & Events section, the user identifies an exhibition of interest.
   - User clicks a "Save to Visit Plan" (or equivalent) control on that exhibition-card.
   - The visit-plan-add-control calls addExhibitionToVisitPlan with exhibitionId set to the selected exhibition.id.
   - On success, the component receives success, visit_plan_id, visit_plan_item_id, visit_plan_count, and message.
   - The exhibitions-list updates the UI for that exhibition (e.g., marking it as in the Visit Plan, using the exhibition.is_in_visit_plan flag or local state) and may display a transient confirmation using the returned message.
   - The Visit Plan summary/link component may update any locally stored count display using visit_plan_count, without persisting it outside the page.

6) Navigating to the Visit Plan from About:
   - User is in the Exhibitions & Events section.
   - User clicks the Visit Plan link provided in this section (e.g., a simple text or button navigation).
   - Browser navigates to visit_plan.html with no required parameters, as defined in outgoing navigation.
   - State on About (filters, selections) is preserved in the URL, so the user can return via browser back and see the same view.

7) Navigating to the full portfolio from About:
   - While reading the biography or studio information, the user sees a control like "View full portfolio".
   - User clicks this control.
   - Browser navigates to gallery.html (no parameters required by this specification).
   - When the user returns via browser back, the About page reloads, restoring the section and filters from the URL if they were present.
interactions:
Loading About content:
      - Trigger: Page load.
      - Action: about-content-loader calls getAboutPageContent.
      - Result: Biography, Statement, and Studio sections render markdown content; any loading or error states are handled by the corresponding content sections.

Switching between Overview and Exhibitions & Events:
      - Trigger: User clicks on a section/tab control (e.g., "Overview" or "Exhibitions & Events").
      - Action: about-section-tabs updates internal activeSection state and writes section=<target-section> into the URL query parameters.
      - Result: Only the active section is visible; the other is hidden. A reload of the page with the same URL re-opens the same section.

Changing exhibition filters (From/To date, status, sort):
      - Trigger: User modifies one or more filter fields and clicks the Apply button.
      - Action: exhibitions-filter-form validates the input (e.g., date formats), then updates URL parameters from_date, to_date, status, and sort, and emits a "filtersChanged" event.
      - Result: exhibitions-list observes new filter values, calls getExhibitions with the current from_date, to_date, status, and sort, and re-renders the list.

Clicking an exhibition for more details:
      - Trigger: User clicks an exhibition title or a dedicated "Details" link on a card.
      - Action: exhibition-card executes a navigation to exhibition_detail.html?exhibitionId=<exhibition.id>.
      - Result: Browser loads the exhibition detail page, which uses the provided exhibitionId.

Adding an exhibition to Visit Plan:
      - Trigger: User clicks the "Add to Visit Plan" control on an exhibition-card.
      - Action: visit-plan-add-control invokes addExhibitionToVisitPlan({ exhibitionId }).
      - Result: On success, the control updates UI state to reflect that this exhibition is now in the Visit Plan (e.g., toggling to a non-add state) and optionally shows a confirmation using the returned message; if the API returns an error (success=false), an error state is displayed without changing is_in_visit_plan.

Navigating to Visit Plan:
      - Trigger: User clicks the Visit Plan link in the Exhibitions & Events section.
      - Action: Simple navigation to visit_plan.html.
      - Result: Visit Plan page opens, showing the saved exhibitions/events.

Navigating to full portfolio:
      - Trigger: User clicks a "View full portfolio" link from the About page.
      - Action: Simple navigation to gallery.html.
      - Result: Gallery page opens; About state is preserved in URL and can be restored via browser navigation.

Handling URL parameter changes directly (e.g., manual edits or shared links):
      - Trigger: URL query string changes (page load with parameters, manual edits followed by reload, or browser back/forward navigation).
      - Action: page-url-state-manager parses section, from_date, to_date, status, and sort from the URL, broadcasts them to about-section-tabs and exhibitions-filter-form.
      - Result: The visible section and filter controls reflect the URL; exhibitions-list calls getExhibitions with the current parameters when the Exhibitions & Events section is active.
page_functionality.state_logic:
State is managed using URL query parameters and in-memory component state, without localStorage or other persistent client storage:

- section:
  - Represents the active main section of the About page (e.g., 'overview', 'exhibitions').
  - On page load, page-url-state-manager reads section; if it equals 'exhibitions', about-section-tabs activates the Exhibitions & Events section; otherwise the default section is active.
  - When the user switches sections, about-section-tabs updates the URL's section parameter via history.pushState or history.replaceState without reloading the page.

- from_date, to_date, status, sort:
  - Represent the current exhibition filters and sorting.
  - On load, exhibitions-filter-form reads these values from the URL, initializes the corresponding form fields, and passes them to exhibitions-list.
  - exhibitions-list uses these values directly as arguments to getExhibitions from_date, to_date, status, and sort.
  - When the user clicks Apply in the filter form, exhibitions-filter-form updates the URL query parameters and emits a filtersChanged event. The page does not need to reload; exhibitions-list re-fetches data using getExhibitions.
  - Using history.pushState/replaceState ensures that each filter change is reflected in the URL so users can bookmark or share their current view.

- is_in_visit_plan (per exhibition):
  - Comes from the getExhibitions response for each exhibition item (boolean is_in_visit_plan) and determines the initial state of the Visit Plan add control.
  - When addExhibitionToVisitPlan succeeds for a given exhibition, exhibitions-list or exhibition-card updates its in-memory state to treat that exhibition as in the Visit Plan, even before any subsequent getExhibitions call.
  - The page does not store this state in URL parameters; it relies on API responses and current page state.

- Visit Plan counts:
  - visit_plan_count returned by addExhibitionToVisitPlan can be used to update an in-memory count displayed near the Visit Plan link, but it is not written to URL parameters.

- Navigation to other pages:
  - Exhibition detail navigation uses exhibition_detail.html?exhibitionId=<id>.
  - Visit Plan navigation uses visit_plan.html (no parameters defined here).
  - Gallery navigation uses gallery.html.
  - About page URL, including section and filter parameters, can be bookmarked or shared; when opened, the same section and exhibition filter state are reconstructed solely from query parameters and subsequent interface calls.

components.functionality: On page initialization, fetches the artist’s biography, statement, and studio information by calling getAboutPageContent and exposes the resulting markdown fields to the relevant content sections.
event_handlers: onInit: call getAboutPageContent(); distribute returned content to biography-section, artist-statement-section, and studio-info-section.
onReloadRequested: re-call getAboutPageContent(), e.g., if a manual refresh is triggered.
onError: notify content sections to display an error state or placeholder while failing gracefully.

components.functionality: Controls which main section of the About page is visible (e.g., overview vs. Exhibitions & Events) and synchronizes this state with the section URL parameter.
event_handlers: onInit: read section from URL via page-url-state-manager and set activeSection accordingly.
onTabChange: when the user selects a different section, update activeSection, write section=<newSection> to the URL query parameters, and emit a sectionChanged event.
onUrlStateChange: when the URL changes due to back/forward navigation, update activeSection to match URL.section.

components.functionality: Displays the artist’s biography text within the About page, using markdown content provided by about-content-loader.
event_handlers: onContentLoaded: render bio_markdown as formatted text.
onSectionActivated: when Overview is active, ensure this section is visible; otherwise hide.
onError: show a non-intrusive fallback message if biography content cannot be loaded.

components.functionality: Displays the artist’s statement, leveraging markdown content and remaining visible only when the Overview section is active.
event_handlers: onContentLoaded: render statement_markdown as formatted text.
onSectionActivated: show or hide based on active section from about-section-tabs.
onError: display a fallback or short notification if the statement cannot be loaded.

components.functionality: Shows studio information (e.g., location, visit information) using markdown content and provides functional links to the Visit Plan and the full portfolio.
event_handlers: onContentLoaded: render studio_info_markdown as formatted text.
onClickGoToExhibitions: instruct about-section-tabs to activate the Exhibitions & Events section (which updates URL.section).
onSectionActivated: show or hide with the rest of the Overview content.

components.functionality: Provides control inputs for Exhibitions & Events filtering: From date, optional To date, status, and sort, and exposes an Apply control that updates URL parameters and triggers data reload.
event_handlers: onInit: read from_date, to_date, status, and sort from URL via page-url-state-manager, initialize form fields with these values, and emit filtersReady to exhibitions-list.
onFieldChange: update internal form state as the user edits fields but do not call getExhibitions yet.

onApplyClick: validate form values (e.g., date formats and ranges); if valid, update URL query parameters for from_date, to_date, status, and sort, and emit filtersChanged event so exhibitions-list re-fetches data.
onUrlStateChange: when URL filter parameters change for any reason (including back/forward navigation), update displayed form values accordingly.

components.functionality: Displays a list of exhibitions and events based on filters and sorting. It calls getExhibitions with current URL-based parameters and renders a collection of exhibition cards, each with navigation and Visit Plan controls.
event_handlers: onSectionActivated: when Exhibitions & Events becomes the active section, call getExhibitions using current URL.from_date, URL.to_date, URL.status, and URL.sort.
onFiltersReady: after exhibitions-filter-form initializes filters from URL, call getExhibitions with those parameters if the Exhibitions & Events section is active.
onFiltersChanged: re-call getExhibitions with updated from_date, to_date, status, and sort values and re-render the list.
onExhibitionClick: when an exhibition-card signals a click on title or Details, navigate to exhibition_detail.html?exhibitionId=<exhibition.id>.
onVisitPlanAddRequested: delegate to visit-plan-add-control for the selected exhibition; upon success, update that item’s is_in_visit_plan state in the rendered list.
onReloadRequested: allow manual re-fetch of exhibitions using the current filters (e.g., via a refresh button within the section).

components.functionality: Represents a single exhibition in the Exhibitions & Events list, showing title, venue, date range, location label, status-derived indicators, and controls for viewing details and adding to the Visit Plan.
event_handlers: onClickTitle: emit exhibitionSelected with the bound exhibition.id so exhibitions-list can navigate to exhibition_detail.html.
onClickDetails: behave the same as onClickTitle, emitting exhibitionSelected.

onClickAddToVisitPlan: if is_in_visit_plan is false, emit visitPlanAddRequested with exhibition.id to visit-plan-add-control; if true, prevent duplicate calls and optionally show that it is already saved.
onVisitPlanStatusUpdated: when visit-plan-add-control reports a successful addition, update local state to mark is_in_visit_plan as true.

components.functionality: Handles the logic for adding a selected exhibition to the Visit Plan using addExhibitionToVisitPlan and updates both the clicked exhibition card and an optional Visit Plan summary state.
event_handlers: onVisitPlanAddRequested: receive exhibitionId from an exhibition-card, call addExhibitionToVisitPlan({ exhibitionId }), and handle success or failure.

onAddSuccess: when addExhibitionToVisitPlan returns success=true, emit visitPlanStatusUpdated to the originating exhibition-card and update any in-memory Visit Plan count display (e.g., in the Visit Plan link).
onAddFailure: show a contextual error message associated with the exhibition-card or a global notification using the returned message, if provided.

components.functionality: Provides a navigational entry point to visit_plan.html from the Exhibitions & Events section and may display an in-memory count of items in the Visit Plan after add operations.
event_handlers: onVisitPlanCountUpdated: when visit-plan-add-control receives a new visit_plan_count, update a displayed count indicator if present.

components.functionality: Offers navigation from the About page to the main portfolio/gallery page.
Centralizes reading and writing of URL query parameters relevant to this page (section, from_date, to_date, status, sort) and broadcasts changes to other components.
event_handlers: onInit: parse current window.location.search into structured state and provide it to about-section-tabs and exhibitions-filter-form.
onUpdateSection: when about-section-tabs requests a section change, update URL.section using history API without reloading and notify subscribers.
onUpdateFilters: when exhibitions-filter-form applies new filters, update URL.from_date, URL.to_date, URL.status, and URL.sort and notify subscribers such as exhibitions-list.
onPopState: when the browser back/forward events occur, re-parse the URL and broadcast updated section and filter states to all dependent components.

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered fixed-width layout, likely based on a 12-column grid within a max-width container (~960–1140px), with a two-column split in the main about section (image column ~35–40%, text column ~60–65%).
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a centered, fixed-width layout that mirrors the mockup: the global header (artist name + site navigation) sits in the shell above. The About page content starts with a spacious two-column 'about' band: a tall portrait on the left and a flexible content column on the right. Within the right column, a slim horizontal tab navigation controls which textual about subsection (Biography, Artist Statement, Studio Info) appears. Below this about band, a clearly separated Exhibitions & Events section spans the same centered content width, starting with a light header row (title + Visit Plan summary link) followed by a horizontally arranged filter form and then a vertically stacked list of exhibition cards. A subtle 'View Portfolio' link acts as a transition between the about band and the exhibitions section.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a centered, fixed-width layout that mirrors the mockup: the global header (artist name + site navigation) sits in the shell above. The About page content starts with a spacious two-column 'about' band: a tall portrait on the left and a flexible content column on the right. Within the right column, a slim horizontal tab navigation controls which textual about subsection (Biography, Artist Statement, Studio Info) appears. Below this about band, a clearly separated Exhibitions & Events section spans the same centered content width, starting with a light header row (title + Visit Plan summary link) followed by a horizontally arranged filter form and then a vertically stacked list of exhibition cards. A subtle 'View Portfolio' link acts as a transition between the about band and the exhibitions section.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The content is constrained within a max-width container (~960–1140px) centered on the page, with generous left and right margins on large screens. The upper about band (portrait + tabs + text) occupies roughly the top half of the scrollable area for an average viewport, with the portrait taking about 35–40% of the content width and the right text column taking 60–65%. Vertical spacing between major sections (tabs area vs. portrait band vs. Exhibitions section) uses large gaps of around 48–64px to echo the gallery-like openness of the mockup. Individual cards and text blocks use internal padding in the 16–24px range. On smaller screens, the columns collapse to a single centered column while preserving generous vertical spacing.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page maintains a low to moderate density: generous line spacing, comfortable line length, and substantial whitespace between blocks. The about text is laid out in a single editorial-style column with paragraphs breathing in vertical margins of around 16–24px. The Exhibitions list has a bit higher density (multiple items) but each card is airy, with subtle borders and clear separation (24–32px vertical gaps). Controls (tabs, filters, buttons) are visually lightweight, avoiding heavy fills or large icons, so they support but never overpower the content.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow inherits the mockup's vertical sequence with a secondary left-to-right scan. After the global header, the eye lands on the left portrait (strongest visual block), then moves rightward to the tab row and the active about text. A prominent but slim CV download link sits near the top of the right column, creating a localized focal point before the main paragraphs. As the user scrolls, a center-aligned 'View Portfolio' link provides a clear pivot to artworks, followed by the Exhibitions & Events header. Within that section, the eye moves from the section title and Visit Plan summary link on the right, down to the filter form and then along the vertical column of exhibition cards, scanning each card left-to-right (title and details) and finally to the Add to Visit Plan control on the right of each card. The overall reading pattern is top-to-bottom with repeated F-like sweeps across each major band.

layout.overall_description: The About page presents as a calm, centered column of content sitting within a wide field of whitespace, much like a printed artist monograph. After the global header, the main content opens with a broad horizontal band where a tall, narrow portrait on the left and a clean text column on the right share the width in a roughly 40/60 ratio. The portrait is framed by a thin, light border and closely aligned at the top with the right column, so they read as a single unit. At the top of the right column, a slim horizontal tab bar and a discreet CV download link introduce the about content, which flows in generous paragraphs below. After this about band, a notable stretch of white space leads to a center-aligned 'View Portfolio' link, acting as a minimalist call-to-action before the page transitions into a second major band: the Exhibitions & Events section. Here, the same centered width is used, with a lean header row that places the section title on the left and a subtle Visit Plan summary link on the right. Immediately below, a lightweight filter bar sits on one or two lines, followed by a vertical stack of exhibition cards that each stretch nearly the full content width but remain airy through ample padding and subtle borders. Within each card, information is left-aligned with a simple right-aligned Add to Visit Plan control. The overall layout feels spare and editorial, guiding the eye in a smooth downward flow with occasional horizontal scans, always prioritizing the artwork-related content over interface chrome.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This data-loader component does not render any visible UI. Conceptually, it sits at the very top of the About page’s content area, wrapping the entire main content region (from the two-column about band through the Exhibitions section). It occupies no visual space and does not affect layout; instead, it ensures that the underlying data is loaded before the visible components (tabs, about sections, exhibitions list) appear. When loading states are needed, they are expressed through skeletons or placeholders provided by the visible sections, not by this component itself.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual infrastructure component; it does not occupy measurable width or height and behaves as an invisible wrapper around the main page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tab navigation is positioned near the top of the right-hand column of the main about band, aligned horizontally and left-aligned within that column. Vertically, it sits roughly 16–24px below the upper edge where the portrait aligns on the left, so the top of the tabs line up visually with the upper third of the portrait. The tab bar occupies about 60–70% of the right column’s width, appearing as a series of slim, text-only tab labels with ample spacing (16–24px) between them, and a thin underline or subtle weight change to indicate the active tab. There is 16–24px of whitespace below the tab row before the body text of the active section begins. On mobile (when the layout collapses to a single column), the tabs stretch closer to full width but retain the same airy spacing and remain positioned immediately above the active content section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline with the content flow inside the right column; it does not stick to the viewport on scroll but remains anchored above the active about content sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The biography section occupies the main text area of the right-hand column in the two-column about band and is the default visible tab content. Horizontally, it spans roughly 60–65% of the centered content container, starting right at the column edge defined by the implicit grid. The text flows in a single vertical column with a comfortable line length (~60–70 characters) and generous line height. A subtle 'Download CV' link with a small red PDF icon sits at the very top of this section, either flush left with the text or subtly aligned to the right within the same column, and is separated from the first paragraph by about 8–16px of whitespace. Paragraphs are stacked with 16–24px vertical spacing between them to echo an editorial layout. The top of the biography content aligns near the top of the portrait, creating a balanced visual band across the page. On narrow screens, the biography section drops below the portrait (which becomes full-width), maintaining the same internal spacing and centered alignment within the page width.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Visible only when the corresponding tab is active; otherwise hidden without occupying space. It is fully contained within the right column and scrolls with the rest of the page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The artist statement section shares the exact spatial footprint as the biography section: it lives in the right-hand column of the two-column about band and occupies the same 60–65% width of the centered container. When its tab is active, it replaces the biography content in place, starting just below the tabs with 16–24px of top padding to separate the tabs from the first line of the statement. Text is arranged in an editorial style with one primary column, a similar line length, and 16–24px vertical spacing between paragraphs or subheadings. There is no heavy box or background; the content floats cleanly on the white page. Vertically, its top aligns with the same baseline used by the biography content, preserving a stable layout when switching tabs. On mobile, it appears below the portrait image in a single centered column, maintaining the same spacing rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mutually exclusive with other about sections—only visible when its tab is active, otherwise not rendered or occupying space. It scrolls normally within the right column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The studio info section also inhabits the right-hand column of the main about band, sharing the identical width and horizontal alignment as the biography and artist statement sections. When active, it appears beneath the tab bar with 16–24px of vertical separation. The content is likely a mix of short paragraphs, address lines, and possibly small sublabels (e.g., 'Studio Location', 'Hours'), laid out vertically with 8–16px between related lines and 16–24px between distinct blocks. The overall block maintains the same top alignment relative to the portrait so that switching tabs does not cause major vertical shifts in the layout. On smaller screens, the studio info appears as a single, centered text block below the portrait, consistent with other tab content and using the same generous whitespace.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Exclusive tab content within the right column; displayed only when its corresponding tab is selected. It remains part of the regular scroll flow and does not have fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The exhibitions filter form appears at the top of the Exhibitions & Events section, below the about band. There is a generous vertical gap (approximately 48–64px) between the bottom of the about content (regardless of which tab is active) and the Exhibitions header area. The filter form is positioned beneath the section heading row: the heading ('Exhibitions & Events') sits left-aligned within the centered container, with the Visit Plan summary link aligned to the far right of the same row; the filter form then appears 16–24px below this header row. Horizontally, the filter form aligns with the same content edges as the about columns, occupying about 70–80% of the container width, starting from the left margin. Its controls (dropdowns, checkboxes, sort options) are arranged in a single slim horizontal band on large screens, evenly spaced with 16px gaps and plenty of breathing room above and below (16–24px). When the viewport narrows, the form’s controls gracefully wrap or stack into two or more rows, but still remain left-aligned within the centered container, preserving the minimalist, light feel without heavy field borders.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and fully integrated into the Exhibitions section’s vertical flow; it scrolls with the page and does not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The exhibitions list occupies the main body of the Exhibitions & Events section, directly beneath the filter form with a modest gap of about 24px to clearly separate controls from content. It spans nearly the full width of the centered content container, leaving only the outer page margins as whitespace, and consists of a single vertical column of exhibition cards. Each card is separated from the next by a vertical gap of approximately 24–32px, allowing the list to remain readable and airy even with many items. The left edge of the list aligns with the left edge of the filter form and the text columns above, maintaining a straight visual column from the top of the page. On small screens, the list remains a single column that fills the available width within the content container, with spacing preserved so no card feels cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrollable list within the Exhibitions section; it does not use infinite scrolling or sticky behavior by default, and its height grows naturally with the number of exhibition cards.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each exhibition card is a horizontally oriented, full-width list item within the Exhibitions list. It spans roughly 100% of the list’s width (minus the container margins) and is framed by a thin, light border or hairline rule to subtly differentiate it from the background without adding visual weight. Inside, there is a padding of about 16–24px on all sides, creating an airy, gallery-card feel. The card’s content is arranged with the exhibition title at the top left, followed by dates and location details stacked below with 8–12px vertical spacing. On the right side of the same card, aligned either with the title row or slightly below it, sits the Add to Visit Plan control, giving a gentle right-edge anchor. This creates a mild left-to-right scan: title and details on the left, action on the right. Cards are vertically separated from each other by 24–32px of whitespace. On narrower screens, the Add to Visit Plan control wraps below the textual content but remains right-aligned within the card, with adequate spacing above it so the card still feels uncluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline list items that expand vertically based on content; they scroll with the page and do not have fixed positioning or overlapping behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The visit plan add control is embedded within each exhibition card, positioned toward the right edge of the card to act as a subtle call-to-action without overpowering the text. On larger screens, it sits in the same horizontal row as the exhibition title or the primary metadata, right-aligned with about 16px of inset from the card’s right padding, and 8–12px from the top padding. The control itself is a compact, minimally styled element—such as a light outline button or text link with a small plus icon—so it reads as a tertiary action rather than a bold button. There is sufficient space (at least 8–12px) around it so it does not feel crowded by the surrounding text. On smaller screens where horizontal space is limited, this control drops to a new line below the main text block but remains aligned to the right side of the card, with 8–16px of vertical separation from the nearest text line.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Anchored inside each card, moving with the card content; it has no sticky or floating behavior and does not alter the card’s basic rectangular footprint.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The visit plan summary link is placed in the header row of the Exhibitions & Events section, sharing a horizontal line with the section title. The section title is left-aligned at the start of the content container, while the summary link is right-aligned at the far edge of the same container, creating a clean, balanced header band. There is approximately 8–16px of vertical padding above and below this row, giving it breathing space without making it feel like a separate bar. The summary link itself appears as a slim text link or understated pill (e.g., 'View Visit Plan (3)'), using a slightly stronger accent color but maintaining thin typography to match the overall minimalist aesthetic. On smaller screens, if horizontal space is limited, the summary link can drop below the section title, right-aligned on its own line with about 4–8px of vertical separation, still within the same overall header block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within the Exhibitions header; it scrolls normally and is not persistent or sticky at the viewport edge.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The view portfolio link acts as a transitional navigation element between the about band and the Exhibitions section. It is positioned below the two-column about area (portrait + text), after a comfortable vertical gap of around 32–40px from the bottom of the active about content. The link is centered horizontally within the main content container, creating a focal point in the empty space between the about content and the Exhibitions header. It is styled as a minimal text link or a very light text-button with a subtle accent color and maybe a small directional arrow, but without a heavy filled button shape, so it fits the understated editorial feel. Additional whitespace (about 24–32px) is placed below the link before the Exhibitions header row begins, allowing it to read as a distinct, optional pivot rather than part of the next section’s heading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline element in the vertical flow; it remains centered and scrolls with the page, with no sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page URL state manager is an invisible utility component that does not appear on screen. Logically, it sits alongside the data loader at the top level of the About page content, coordinating the active tab state and exhibitions filters with URL parameters. It occupies no physical space and does not affect alignment, spacing, or the grid; it simply ensures that changes in tabs or filters can be reflected in and restored from the URL.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-spatial; it has no width, height, or position and functions purely as a behavioral layer tied to the page’s state and URL.

name: Exhibition Detail
title: Exhibition Detail
description: Displays full details for a single exhibition, lets visitors save it to their Visit Plan, and provides navigation to related artworks, the exhibitions list, and the Visit Plan.

core_features: Load a specific exhibition using the exhibitionId URL parameter and the getExhibitionDetails interface, displaying title, date range, location, status, venue details, and descriptions.
Display any featured artworks returned by getExhibitionDetails.featured_artworks, allowing visitors to click through to the Gallery page for deeper exploration.

Show an 'Add to Visit Plan' action that calls addExhibitionToVisitPlan with the current exhibitionId, then updates page state based on the response and reflects whether the exhibition is already in the Visit Plan using is_in_visit_plan.
Provide a 'View Visit Plan' navigation option after successfully adding the exhibition, taking the user to visit_plan.html.
Offer a 'Back to Exhibitions' navigation link that returns the user to about.html with section=exhibitions to see the full exhibitions list.

Optionally let the user request related artworks for a selected featured artwork by calling getRelatedArtworks with that artwork's id and displaying the returned artworks as cross-navigation suggestions.
user_workflows:
View exhibition details:
1. User navigates to exhibition_detail.html with a valid exhibitionId URL parameter.
2. On page initialization, the page controller reads exhibitionId from the URL.
3. The page calls getExhibitionDetails(exhibitionId).
4. When the response returns, the page populates the header with exhibition.title, date_range_label, location_label, and exhibition.status.
5. The page fills the information section with venue_name, venue_address, venue_city, venue_country, website_url, and any other available fields.
6. The description section displays description_long if available, otherwise description_short.
7. The featured artworks section lists all featured_artworks, if any are returned.

Add an exhibition to the Visit Plan and go to Visit Plan:
1. User clicks the 'Add to Visit Plan' button.
2. The button’s event handler calls addExhibitionToVisitPlan(exhibitionId) and shows a loading state while awaiting the response.
3. If success is true, the page updates its local exhibition visit-plan state (e.g., marks is_in_visit_plan as true) and shows a success notification using the returned message and visit_plan_count.
4. The 'Add to Visit Plan' button is disabled or visually updated to indicate the exhibition is already in the Visit Plan.
5. A 'View Visit Plan' button or link becomes available.
6. When the user clicks 'View Visit Plan', the browser navigates to visit_plan.html (no additional parameters are set by this page).

Return to the exhibitions list:
1. User clicks the 'Back to Exhibitions' link.
2. The page navigates to about.html with the URL parameter section=exhibitions so the About page can scroll or focus on the exhibitions section.
3. No interfaces are called as part of this navigation from the Exhibition Detail page.

Explore featured artworks and related artworks:
1. The featured artworks list is populated from getExhibitionDetails.featured_artworks.
2. When the user clicks on a featured artwork’s thumbnail or title, the page navigates to gallery.html (optionally including artwork-related URL parameters as defined on that page) so the user can see the artwork in the broader gallery context.
3. Optionally, for a given featured artwork, the user can click a 'Show related artworks' action.
4. The page calls getRelatedArtworks(artworkId) for the selected artwork.
5. The returned array of related artworks is rendered in a related-artworks panel.
6. From each related artwork, the user can navigate to gallery.html for deeper exploration.

Handle missing or invalid exhibitionId:
1. If the page loads without an exhibitionId or with an invalid value, the page controller does not call getExhibitionDetails.
2. Instead, it renders an error state instructing the user that the exhibition could not be found and offers a link to about.html?section=exhibitions.
3. If getExhibitionDetails fails or returns no exhibition, the same fallback error state is displayed.
interactions: Page initialization: On load, the page reads the exhibitionId query parameter from the URL. If present, it triggers getExhibitionDetails(exhibitionId). If absent or invalid, it renders an error message and a link back to the exhibitions list.

Add to Visit Plan button click: When the user clicks the primary 'Add to Visit Plan' button, the page calls addExhibitionToVisitPlan(exhibitionId). While awaiting the response, the button shows a loading state and is temporarily disabled to prevent duplicate submissions.

Visit Plan addition response handling: If addExhibitionToVisitPlan returns success=true, the page:
- Updates internal state so the exhibition is treated as in the Visit Plan.
- Updates the button label/state (for example, disabling or changing it to reflect success).
- Displays a notification using the returned message and visit_plan_count.
If success=false, it displays an error notification with the returned message and leaves the button enabled for retry.

View Visit Plan navigation: After a successful add, when the user clicks 'View Visit Plan', the browser navigates to visit_plan.html using a standard link, with no new interfaces called from this page.
Back to Exhibitions navigation: When the user clicks 'Back to Exhibitions', the browser navigates to about.html?section=exhibitions. No interfaces are called as part of this interaction.

Featured artwork click: When the user clicks on any featured artwork card, the page navigates to gallery.html (and may append artwork-specific URL parameters defined elsewhere in the system). No Exhibition Detail interfaces are called by this interaction.

Show related artworks for a featured artwork: When available, if the user clicks a 'Show related artworks' control on a featured artwork, the page calls getRelatedArtworks(artwork.id). The response is rendered in a related-artworks panel. Subsequent clicks on artworks in that panel navigate the user to gallery.html.

Status-dependent UI behavior: If getExhibitionDetails returns is_in_visit_plan=true, the 'Add to Visit Plan' button is shown in a completed/disabled state from initial render, and the 'View Visit Plan' shortcut is available without requiring a new addExhibitionToVisitPlan call.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary state driver for this page is the exhibitionId URL query parameter.

1) URL parameter handling:
- The page expects a query parameter named exhibitionId (e.g., exhibition_detail.html?exhibitionId={id}).
- On each page load or URL change, the page controller reads exhibitionId.
- If exhibitionId is present and non-empty, the page calls getExhibitionDetails(exhibitionId).
- If exhibitionId is missing or empty, the page does not call any interfaces and instead renders an error state with a navigation option back to about.html?section=exhibitions.

2) Data-driven UI state from getExhibitionDetails:
- The exhibition object from getExhibitionDetails drives the main content sections (header, venue information, descriptions).
- date_range_label and location_label are used directly for display, and they determine what appears alongside the title and venue.
- featured_artworks, if non-empty, causes the featured artworks component to render; if empty or undefined, that component is hidden.
- is_in_visit_plan determines the initial state of the Visit Plan controls:
  - If true, the 'Add to Visit Plan' button is disabled or shown as already saved, and a 'View Visit Plan' link is available immediately.
  - If false, the 'Add to Visit Plan' button is enabled and 'View Visit Plan' may be hidden until a successful add.

3) State updates after addExhibitionToVisitPlan:
- When addExhibitionToVisitPlan(exhibitionId) succeeds, the page updates its internal state to treat the current exhibition as in the Visit Plan, equivalent to is_in_visit_plan=true.
- This update occurs without a page reload and without changing the URL parameters.
- The visit_plan_count from the response can be displayed in the success notification and optionally reflected in any Visit Plan badge in the UI, but it does not alter URL state.

4) Related artworks state with getRelatedArtworks:
- Related artworks are not loaded on initial page render.
- When a user requests related artworks for a specific featured artwork, the selected artwork's id is passed to getRelatedArtworks.
- The returned list is stored in the related-artworks component state and displayed until the user navigates away or dismisses the panel.
- This state is ephemeral and not encoded in the URL; reloading the page will clear it and require the user to re-trigger 'Show related artworks'.

5) Navigation-related state:
- Navigating to about.html?section=exhibitions and visit_plan.html leaves exhibition_detail.html entirely; those pages manage their own state using their respective URL parameters and interfaces.
- Clicking on any artwork (featured or related) navigates to gallery.html, where artwork-specific behavior is handled on that page. Exhibition Detail does not modify or depend on gallery-specific URL parameters.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by reading the exhibitionId URL parameter, calling getExhibitionDetails, handling loading and error states, and distributing the fetched data and flags (such as is_in_visit_plan) to child components.
Displays the exhibition title, date range label, location label, and status, using data loaded by the page controller.
Shows venue information (venue_name, venue_address, venue_city, venue_country) and an optional external website link from exhibition.website_url.

Displays the long description of the exhibition when available, falling back to the short description. Handles cases where both are missing by hiding itself or showing a generic 'No description available' message.

Manages the 'Add to Visit Plan' and 'View Visit Plan' controls for the current exhibition. Uses is_in_visit_plan to determine initial states and calls addExhibitionToVisitPlan when the user chooses to save the exhibition.

Displays a list/grid of featured artworks associated with the exhibition using featured_artworks from getExhibitionDetails. Each artwork tile can be clicked to navigate to the gallery page or to request related artworks.
When triggered for a selected featured artwork, calls getRelatedArtworks(artworkId) and displays the related artworks returned. Provides navigation from each related artwork to the gallery page.
Provides navigation shortcuts for 'Back to Exhibitions' (about.html?section=exhibitions) and 'View Visit Plan' (visit_plan.html), based on the current exhibition state.
Displays transient success or error messages for operations like addExhibitionToVisitPlan and getRelatedArtworks, using the message field returned by addExhibitionToVisitPlan when available.

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered fixed-width layout, likely based on a 12-column grid within a max-width container (~960–1140px), with a two-column split in the main about section (image column ~35–40%, text column ~60–65%).
layout.layout_pattern: Use a vertically linear, centered layout: a prominent exhibition header sits at the top center, followed directly by a centered navigation links bar. After a generous vertical gap, the main exhibition content forms a two-column band: the left column contains the exhibition info panel (analogous to the portrait image in the mockup), and the right column contains the exhibition description text with visit plan actions positioned near the top of that column (analogous to the CV link). Below this two-column block, the layout returns to a single-column flow for the featured artworks grid, followed by the related artworks panel. A slim feedback notification banner appears just below the navigation when needed, without breaking the overall vertical flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a vertically linear, centered layout: a prominent exhibition header sits at the top center, followed directly by a centered navigation links bar. After a generous vertical gap, the main exhibition content forms a two-column band: the left column contains the exhibition info panel (analogous to the portrait image in the mockup), and the right column contains the exhibition description text with visit plan actions positioned near the top of that column (analogous to the CV link). Below this two-column block, the layout returns to a single-column flow for the featured artworks grid, followed by the related artworks panel. A slim feedback notification banner appears just below the navigation when needed, without breaking the overall vertical flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Constrain content within a centered max-width container (~960–1140px), with wide left and right gutters on larger screens to create a gallery-like feeling. Allocate roughly the upper 25–30% of the initial viewport to the exhibition header and navigation area, surrounded by generous whitespace. The main two-column band occupies the central vertical portion of the page, with the left info panel taking about 35–40% of the available content width and the right description column taking about 60–65%. Below, featured and related artworks sections each span nearly the full container width but maintain large vertical spacing between them (~48–64px) to preserve the airy rhythm.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a low-to-moderate density, with substantial vertical gaps between major sections, and comfortable inner padding (16–24px) inside panels and around text. Text columns should have generous line spacing and not extend edge-to-edge; instead, they should sit within the central max-width with clean margins. The info panel and artwork grids should feel uncluttered, with clear separation between items and minimal decorative elements, keeping the focus on content rather than UI chrome.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye from the top center exhibition title down to the centered navigation links, then gently into the main two-column content area. Within that band, the eye first encounters the left-side info panel due to its structured, framed appearance, then moves right to read the description text. The visit plan actions, styled with a subtle accent color, sit near the top of the right column, acting as a secondary focal point similar to the CV link in the mockup. From there, the flow continues downward to the featured artworks grid and then to the related artworks panel. The feedback notification banner, when present, briefly captures attention just below navigation but remains visually light so it does not overpower the core content.

layout.overall_description: The exhibition detail page sits within a calm, centered white canvas. At the top of the viewport, a large, softly spaced exhibition title and key summary information are centered, occupying the upper quarter-to-third of the screen with ample whitespace above and below. Just beneath this header, a thin, elegant navigation links bar runs horizontally across the center, offering access to the exhibitions list, the visit plan, and related sections, while still feeling light and unobtrusive. When active, a narrow notification banner appears just below these links, stretching across the same max-width container and gently announcing feedback (such as a successful save) without disrupting the minimal aesthetic. After a generous vertical gap, the layout transitions into a balanced two-column band: on the left, a compact yet prominent info panel with dates, venue, and key metadata framed by subtle borders takes about one-third of the content width; on the right, occupying the remaining width, a clean column of description text begins near the top of the info panel. At the top edge of this text column, a visually accented but minimalist visit plan action block offers to save or manage the exhibition in the visitor's plan, echoing the position and emphasis of the CV link in the original mockup. Below this main band, the page opens back into full-width sections within the same centered container: first a featured artworks grid that displays key works in an airy multi-column layout, followed by a slightly more subdued related artworks panel. Generous vertical spacing between these sections and the absence of heavy dividers create a smooth, gallery-like reading experience that emphasizes the exhibition content over the interface itself.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller acts as the structural frame for the entire view. It spans the full browser width with a plain white (or very light) background, and centers all content within a fixed-width container approximately 960–1140px wide. This container sits horizontally centered with wide left and right gutters on large screens, reinforcing a gallery-like feel. Vertically, the controller applies generous top and bottom padding (around 64px each) and manages the vertical rhythm: a spacious region at the top for the exhibition header and navigation, a clearly separated two-column main section in the middle, and distinct full-width sections for featured and related artworks below. All components sit in normal document flow within this controller, with section-to-section vertical gaps mostly in the 48–64px range.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a standard scrolling container; not fixed or sticky. Establishes a centered max-width layout and consistent spacing but does not itself draw visual focus.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The exhibition summary header is positioned at the very top of the content area inside the centered container, starting roughly 64px below the top edge of the viewport. It is horizontally centered and spans about 70–80% of the container width, leaving comfortable left and right margins within the container itself. The exhibition title appears as a large, thin-weight heading centered on the page, with one or two lines of supporting details (such as dates and venue) in smaller, lighter text directly beneath it. There is substantial whitespace above the title (at least 48px of padding from the top of the container) and a clear gap of around 24–32px below the summary header before the navigation links bar begins. The layout and spacing mirror the mockup’s centered artist name section, creating a calm focal point when the page first loads.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block in the normal flow, always at the top of the page content. Its height expands with the amount of summary text but maintains generous top and bottom margins.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation links bar sits directly below the exhibition summary header, aligned to the center of the same max-width container. It occupies roughly 60–70% of the container width, leaving symmetrical whitespace on both sides to keep it visually light. Navigation items are arranged horizontally in a single row, with thin typography, generous letter-spacing, and equal spacing between items (around 24–32px). The bar itself is visually subtle, with either no background or a slightly warmer white, and minimal or no borders. There is about 12–16px of vertical padding inside the bar, and a gap of roughly 24–32px below it before any feedback notification banner or the main content begins. This mirrors the mockup’s centered navigation row beneath the artist’s name.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard non-sticky navigation within the page content. It scrolls with the page rather than remaining fixed, reinforcing the minimal, content-first aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The feedback notification banner appears conditionally and is positioned immediately below the navigation links bar within the same centered container. When present, it spans nearly the full width of the container (90–100%), preserving a small horizontal margin if desired to stay in line with the understated style. Its height is compact—roughly 40–56px—with 8–12px vertical padding above and below the text. The banner uses a very light tinted background and thin text, centered horizontally, to convey messages such as 'Added to your Visit Plan' without feeling heavy. There is a clear vertical gap of about 24–32px below the banner before the main two-column exhibition content begins. When no feedback is being displayed, this banner is absent and the two-column layout moves up to sit directly below the navigation links bar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears within the normal document flow, pushing content down when visible. It is not fixed or sticky; it scrolls away with the rest of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The exhibition info panel forms the left column of the main content band. This band begins after a generous vertical gap (around 48–64px) below the navigation or notification banner. Within the centered container, the content area is split into two columns: the left column, occupied by the info panel, takes approximately 35–40% of the container width, while the right column takes the remaining 60–65%. The info panel is aligned flush to the left edge of the content band and top-aligned with the right column's description text. It is vertically oriented, similar in overall footprint to the portrait image in the mockup: a tall, rectangular card with subtle borders or a thin outline and perhaps a very light background to suggest a framed panel. Inside, exhibition metadata (dates, venue, opening hours, etc.) is laid out in stacked rows or short blocks, with internal padding of about 16–24px on all sides and vertical spacing between items of 8–16px. The panel’s height grows with content but remains visually balanced against the right column, creating a stable left-hand anchor for the eye.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Participates in a two-column layout using a grid or flex system. It remains top-aligned with the description column and scrolls normally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The exhibition description section forms the right column of the main content band, starting at the same vertical level as the top of the info panel. It occupies about 60–65% of the container width and is separated from the left column by a comfortable horizontal gap of roughly 32–40px, preserving the airy feel. The text column begins near the top edge of this right-side area, visually aligning with the top of the info panel on the left. Paragraphs are set in a single, clear column with generous line-height and paragraph spacing (12–16px between paragraphs), allowing the description to read like an editorial article. The visit plan actions component sits at the top of this section, slightly offset from the main paragraph flow: it appears above the first paragraph, aligned either to the top-right edge of the text column or just inside the top-left edge, with about 8–16px of vertical separation from the beginning of the body text. Below this action area, the description flows uninterrupted down the right column, with the entire block ending before a substantial vertical gap (48–64px) that precedes the featured artworks list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Resides in the right half of a two-column layout, with flexible height depending on text length. It scrolls naturally and does not employ sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The visit plan actions component is positioned within the right column, near the top of the exhibition description section, acting as a localized focal point similar to the CV download link in the mockup. It is horizontally aligned with the right description column and occupies no more than 30–40% of that column’s width, ensuring it feels like a compact accent rather than a full-width block. The component sits just above the first paragraph of the description, with a vertical gap of approximately 8–12px between its lower edge and the beginning of the text. Its content—such as a primary 'Save to Visit Plan' control, status text, or a subtle icon—is arranged in a single row or small cluster, using a restrained accent color (e.g., blue) to draw attention without overpowering the minimalist aesthetic. The component could be aligned to the top-right of the text column to echo the slightly offset CV link positioning, creating a visual pivot where the user’s eye moves from the info panel on the left into the action and then into the descriptive text.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline with the description column’s layout at its top, in normal flow. It is not sticky or floating; it scrolls away as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured artworks list sits directly below the main two-column band, separated from it by a substantial vertical gap of approximately 48–64px to signal a new section while maintaining an open, breathable layout. It returns to a single-column section within the same centered container, spanning approximately 90–100% of the container width. At the top of this section, a modest, left-aligned heading (e.g., 'Featured Artworks') sits with 8–16px of spacing below it before the artworks grid begins. The grid itself is laid out in multiple columns—typically three equal-width columns on larger screens—with 24–32px of horizontal and vertical spacing between artwork cards to preserve the minimal, gallery-like feeling. Each artwork tile remains visually simple, relying on the artwork image and minimal text; there are no heavy borders or backgrounds. The section’s bottom is marked by another generous vertical gap (48–64px) before the related artworks panel begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard section in the vertical flow. The grid reflows responsively (e.g., from three columns to two or one on narrower screens) but remains centered within the max-width container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related artworks panel is placed below the featured artworks list, with a clear vertical separation of 48–64px to differentiate it as a secondary browsing area. It also spans the full width of the centered container (around 90–100% of that width), maintaining consistent side margins with other sections. A subtle heading or label at the top (e.g., 'You may also like' or 'Related works') is left-aligned and smaller in visual weight than the main section headings, reflecting its secondary importance. Beneath the heading, related artworks can be displayed in a narrower grid or a horizontal strip of cards, with slightly tighter spacing than the featured grid (e.g., 16–24px between items) to reflect its supporting role. The overall height of this panel is moderate; it should feel like an extension of the main exhibition rather than a completely new page section. Ample whitespace below the last row of related items (around 64px) creates a soft visual endpoint to the content or space for any footer elements outside the scope of this layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard, scrollable section. It does not stick or overlay; it follows the featured artworks list in normal document flow.

name: Visit Plan
title: Visit Plan
description: Manage exhibitions and events you have saved to visit: review details, open each exhibition, remove items, and navigate to discover more exhibitions.

core_features: Fetch and display the current visit plan using getVisitPlan, including each saved exhibition with title, date_range_label, location_label, and any available notes.
Show the total number of saved exhibitions/events and keep this count in sync with the visit_plan_count returned by removeVisitPlanItem.
Allow users to open an exhibition’s detail page by clicking its title, navigating to exhibition_detail.html with the appropriate exhibitionId URL parameter.
Enable removal of individual exhibitions from the visit plan via removeVisitPlanItem, updating the list and total count without requiring a full page reload.
Provide an empty-state view when there are no items in the visit plan, with a navigational link to about.html pre-filtered to the exhibitions section.
Offer client-side sorting and filtering controls (e.g., by date or exhibition status) that operate on the data returned by getVisitPlan and are encoded in URL query parameters.
Support client-side pagination over the visit plan items, driven by URL parameters (e.g., page and per_page), without additional API calls beyond getVisitPlan.
Provide user feedback for removal actions (success or failure) using the message and visit_plan_count returned from removeVisitPlanItem.
user_workflows:
View and review visit plan:
