User interaction: clicking "Return Home" triggers navigation to index.html without additional parameters.
User interaction: clicking the site logo in the header triggers navigation to index.html.
Optional interaction: clicking a "Cancel" or "Back to projects" control before submission navigates away from the page to projects.html without submitting the form.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven primarily by URL parameters and API responses. On load, the page reads project_reference_id and project_reference_title from the query string. If project_reference_id is present and non-empty, the project-context-loader component calls getProjectDetails with projectId=project_reference_id. While getProjectDetails is pending, a 'loading project context' state is set. Upon successful response, the project context state stores the returned project object and associated labels. The Project reference input field's initial value is determined as follows: (1) If project_reference_title exists in the URL, use that; (2) else if getProjectDetails returns a project with a title, use that; (3) else leave the field empty. If getProjectDetails fails, the project context state is marked as 'error', causing an error notice in the context panel while leaving the form usable; no prefill from the API is applied. The form maintains its own internal state (name, email, project_reference_title input value, message, validation errors, submission status). When the user submits, if required fields are missing or invalid, the form sets a 'validation error' state and prevents the API call. If validation passes, the form sets a 'submitting' state and calls submitCollaborationInquiry with the current field values and project_reference_id (which may be null). On a successful API response (success=true), the form sets a 'submitted' state, stores the returned inquiry_id and status for potential use (e.g., in the confirmation message), and renders the submission-feedback-panel while disabling further editing of the form fields or hiding the form. On an error response (success=false or network error), the form sets an 'error' state with the returned message (if any) and re-enables the Submit button for retries. URL parameters remain unchanged throughout; state transitions are all in-memory for this page and do not use localStorage or other persistent client storage.

components.functionality: Provides a consistent page header with a clickable logo that allows users to return to the home page (index.html). It does not depend on project or inquiry data but supports navigation as defined in the page architecture.

Top-level container that initializes the page by reading URL parameters, orchestrates loading of project context when needed, and coordinates rendering of the project context panel, the collaboration inquiry form, and the submission feedback panel.

Responsible for fetching project details when a project_reference_id is present in the URL. It calls getProjectDetails and exposes loading, success, and error states along with the retrieved project data to child components. It also determines the effective default value for the Project reference field based on URL and API data.

Displays contextual information about the referenced project when available. If project data is loaded, it shows the project title and optional metadata from getProjectDetails (e.g., client_type_label, project_category_label, wall_size_label). If project_reference_id is provided but loading fails, it shows an error message. If no project_reference_id is provided, it is hidden or displays a neutral state indicating that no specific project has been selected. It also communicates the resolved default Project reference title to the form component.

Core form component that collects the user's Name, Email, Project reference (as free text, optionally prefilled), and Message. It requires Name, Email, and Message for submission, performs client-side validation before calling submitCollaborationInquiry, and manages the form's internal states (idle, validation error, submitting, submitted, submission error). It uses the project_reference_id from page state and the current Project reference input value when calling the API.

Displays an inline confirmation or error summary after the collaboration-inquiry-form attempts to submit. On success, it shows a thank-you/confirmation message along with optional details such as the returned inquiry_id or status. It also provides navigation actions for 'Back to projects' (to projects.html) and 'Return Home' (to index.html). On error, it presents the error message returned by submitCollaborationInquiry and offers a way back to the form for correction and resubmission.

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered fixed-width layout, likely based on a 12-column grid within a max-width container (~960–1140px), with a two-column split in the main about section (image column ~35–40%, text column ~60–65%).
layout.layout_pattern: Reuse the mockup’s structure: a centered, airy header at the very top (artist name and horizontal navigation in a single-column stack), followed by a clear vertical break, then a two-column main section. In the main section, the left column is reserved for project context (mirroring the portrait column) and the right column is dedicated to the collaboration inquiry form (mirroring the biography column). The submission feedback panel sits within the right column, above the form, so it is noticed immediately after submission without disrupting the overall linear flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Reuse the mockup’s structure: a centered, airy header at the very top (artist name and horizontal navigation in a single-column stack), followed by a clear vertical break, then a two-column main section. In the main section, the left column is reserved for project context (mirroring the portrait column) and the right column is dedicated to the collaboration inquiry form (mirroring the biography column). The submission feedback panel sits within the right column, above the form, so it is noticed immediately after submission without disrupting the overall linear flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Maintain a max-width central container of roughly 960–1140px with wide left/right margins. The header (site-header) occupies roughly the top 25–30% of the initial viewport through generous vertical padding and whitespace. Below it, the page-shell hosts the main section in a two-column layout: the project-context side uses about 35–40% of the content width; the form side uses about 60–65%. Vertical spacing between major sections (header → main) is in the 48–64px range, and internal padding inside panels/cards follows a 16–24px pattern.
layout_strategies.content_density: Overall density is low to moderate. Large vertical gaps separate header, section label (if used), and the main two-column content. Within the columns, text and form fields are spaced with comfortable 16–24px gaps, avoiding crowding. The project context panel reads as a light, bordered card with generous padding and minimal decoration, while the form has a clean editorial column feel rather than a compact, utilitarian form layout.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow begins at the top center with the artist’s name, moves down to the centered navigation, then drops further into the main content where the eye first hits the left project context panel (the analog to the portrait photograph). From there, the eye naturally travels rightward to the collaboration inquiry form, which is the primary interactive area. After the user submits the form, the feedback panel appears just above the form fields, becoming a local focal point without overpowering the rest of the layout. The overall reading pattern is vertical with a secondary left-to-right scan, similar to an editorial F-pattern.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a quiet, gallery-like canvas: a wide, white background with a centered content column. At the top, the artist’s name is set in thin, understated type, centered with ample whitespace above and below. Immediately beneath, a minimalist horizontal navigation is also centered, using slim typography and generous letterspacing. A large vertical gap then introduces the main area, where a two-column layout sits within a max-width container. On the left, a tall, lightly bordered project context panel occupies roughly one-third of the width, aligning its top edge with the main content on the right. On the right, a wide, airy form column extends to about two-thirds of the width, with a brief introductory line and the collaboration inquiry fields arranged in a single, well-spaced vertical stack. When active, a subtle feedback panel appears above the form, occupying the same width. The overall impression is calm and editorial: plenty of white space, soft separations between sections, and a clear visual flow from header to context to form, letting the content and communication intent take center stage without heavy UI framing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The site-header spans the full width of the browser window at the very top of the page, but its contents are constrained to the centered max-width container (about 960–1140px). Visually, it forms a tall, airy band occupying roughly the top quarter of the initial viewport. At the center of this band, the artist’s name sits on its own line, horizontally centered, with generous vertical padding (roughly 48–64px above and below), and no visible borders or background blocks, just text on white. Directly below the name, separated by about 16–24px, is the horizontal navigation: a thin-typed, centered row of links with ample spacing between items. The navigation remains within the same central column as the name. There are large white margins to the left and right of this content, reinforcing the feeling of a centered, fixed-width layout. As the user scrolls, the header scrolls away with the page (no sticky behavior).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Full-width horizontal band at the top of the document, with content centered inside a fixed max-width container; participates in normal document flow (non-fixed, non-sticky).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-shell-collaboration-inquiry component begins immediately below the site-header and defines the main content container. It centers all inner content within a max-width column (approximately 960–1140px) and applies horizontal padding of about 24–32px on smaller viewports to ensure the content never touches the screen edges. A substantial vertical gap (around 48–64px) separates the header from the top of this shell, visually marking a new section. At the top of the shell, aligned left within the main column, a subtle page title or label such as “Collaboration Inquiry” can appear, set in light, editorial text with about 16–24px of whitespace below it. Below this label, the shell establishes a two-column layout that spans the full internal width of the container on desktop: a left column occupying roughly 35–40% of the width and a right column occupying roughly 60–65%, with a clear gutter of about 32–40px between them. The left column is reserved for the project-context-loader/project-context-panel, while the right column houses the submission-feedback-panel and the collaboration-inquiry-form. The shell ensures generous vertical padding at the bottom (48–64px) before the page footer or next section. On narrow screens, these columns stack into a single column, with the project context content appearing above the form, maintaining the same generous vertical spacing between them.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a structural, centered container for the main content; standard scrolling behavior with responsive adjustment from two-column to single-column on smaller viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The project-context-loader occupies the left column area defined by the page shell whenever project data is being fetched. It is aligned with the top of the main content region, so its top edge lines up horizontally with the top of the right-column content (feedback panel or form). The loader sits within the left 35–40% of the container width, leaving a consistent gutter of about 32–40px between it and the right column. Visually, it should read as a light, unobtrusive placeholder—such as a vertically oriented rectangle with a subtle gray tint or skeleton blocks—roughly matching the anticipated size of the final project-context-panel (a tall card reaching from near the top of the main section to roughly mid-page). It has internal padding comparable to the finished panel (16–24px all around) so that when it transitions to actual content, the layout doesn’t jump. On mobile, the loader appears as a full-width block directly above the form area, separated by about 24–32px of white space. Once loading completes, this area smoothly transitions into or is replaced by the project-context-panel without altering the column proportions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies the left column within the main two-column layout during data loading; participates in normal flow and is replaced/hidden once data is loaded.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The project-context-panel lives in the left-hand column of the main two-column layout and is visually analogous to the portrait photograph card in the original mockup. It starts near the top of the main content area so that its top edge aligns horizontally with the top of the right column’s primary content. The panel itself occupies approximately 35–40% of the available content width and is offset from the right column by a clear gutter of around 32–40px. It is styled as a tall, vertically oriented card with a very thin outline or hairline border and a small drop of internal padding (16–24px on all sides). Inside, project information (such as project title, a small thumbnail image, and brief metadata) is stacked vertically with generous spacing, leaving plenty of white space around each element so that the panel remains visually light. The height of the panel will typically extend from near the top of the main section to roughly mid-page, depending on content, but it should not visually overwhelm the form. On smaller screens, the project-context-panel reflows to sit above the form, spanning 100% of the content width, with about 24–32px of vertical spacing separating it from the form column below. In all cases, it acts as the first focal point in the main section, providing visual and informational context before the user’s eye moves to the form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card within the left column of the main two-column layout; scrolls normally with the page. On narrow viewports, it stacks above the form as a full-width block.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The collaboration-inquiry-form occupies the right column of the main section and serves as the primary interactive area. It starts near the top of the right column, horizontally aligned with the top of the project-context-panel. This column spans roughly 60–65% of the content width inside the page shell, set to the right of the project-context-panel with a 32–40px gutter between them. Directly above the first form field, there may be a short piece of introductory text or a compact heading explaining the purpose of the form; below that, the form fields are stacked in a single vertical column. Each field (e.g., name, email, project reference, message) stretches most of the column width, leaving slender margins on the left and right for breathing room. Vertical spacing between form fields is generous (16–24px) and groups of related fields can have slightly larger separation (24–32px) to maintain a calm, editorial feel. The submit button is aligned with the left edge of the input fields, sitting in its own row with a comfortable buffer of whitespace above and below. The entire right column maintains ample top and bottom padding relative to the page-shell edges so that the form never feels cramped. On smaller screens, this form reflows into a full-width single column beneath the project-context-panel, with consistent spacing (24–32px) between the panel and the first form field. The form is visually dominant within the main content, but remains light and uncluttered, in keeping with the minimalist portfolio aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies the right column of the two-column layout; standard scrolling behavior. On narrow viewports, becomes a full-width vertical form stacked below the project context content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submission-feedback-panel is positioned within the right-hand column, directly associated with the collaboration-inquiry-form. When visible (e.g., after a submission attempt), it appears at the very top of the right column’s content area, immediately above any form heading or introductory text. It spans the full width of the form column (roughly 60–65% of the main container on desktop), aligning its left and right edges with the form fields below, giving the impression of a unified block. Visually, it takes the form of a low-profile, horizontally oriented panel or card with a subtle border or background tint to distinguish it from the white page background, but still maintaining the minimalist aesthetic. Internal padding of around 12–16px on all sides ensures that feedback text has breathing room and is easily scannable. When active, it introduces a modest vertical offset (about 16–24px) before the first form field, pushing the form content downward slightly but not interfering with the left column. On mobile, the feedback panel remains directly above the form in the stacked layout, occupying 100% of the available content width, with consistent vertical spacing above and below. When hidden, its space collapses entirely so that the form moves up seamlessly.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, collapsible panel within the right column that appears and disappears above the form content; participates in normal document flow and does not overlay other elements.

name: Newsletter Signup
title: Newsletter Signup
description: Page for visitors to subscribe to the artist’s newsletter by providing an email address, selecting interests, and choosing preferred email frequency.

core_features: Load newsletter configuration (interest options, frequency choices, privacy message) using getNewsletterSignupConfig and bind it to the signup form.
Display a newsletter signup form with an email field, dynamic interest checkboxes based on config.interests, and dynamic frequency radio buttons based on config.frequencies.
Allow selection of preferred email frequency, including any option that corresponds to a monthly-style summary provided by the configuration API.
Handle optional source tracking via the URL parameter source and pass it to subscribeToNewsletter.source, with a fallback value when the parameter is missing or invalid.
Validate form input (email format, at least one interest, a selected frequency) before calling subscribeToNewsletter.
Submit the subscription using subscribeToNewsletter and, on success, redirect the user to newsletter_thank_you.html.
Display the privacy_message returned from getNewsletterSignupConfig to reassure visitors about privacy and email frequency.
Provide navigation back to index.html when the site logo is clicked.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Initial page load
1. User navigates to newsletter.html, optionally with a source query parameter (e.g., newsletter.html?source=...).
2. The page-init controller parses the source URL parameter and stores it in page state if present.
3. The page calls getNewsletterSignupConfig.
4. While waiting, the signup form shows a loading state and disables submission.
5. On success, the form populates the interest checkboxes from config.interests and frequency radio buttons from config.frequencies, and displays config.privacy_message.
6. If getNewsletterSignupConfig fails, an error message is shown and the form remains disabled until the user retries.

Workflow: Submitting the newsletter form
1. User enters an email address into the email input.
2. User selects one or more interests via the dynamically rendered checkboxes.
3. User selects one frequency via the dynamically rendered radio buttons.
4. User clicks the form submit button.
5. The form validator runs client-side validation for required fields and email format.
6. If validation fails, inline error messages are displayed and the API call is not made.
7. If validation passes, the submission handler derives the source value: if a valid source URL parameter was found on load, it uses that; otherwise it uses a predefined value representing the newsletter page.
8. The page calls subscribeToNewsletter with { email, interests: [selected interest values], frequency: selected frequency value, source }.
9. While awaiting the response, the submit button is disabled and a submitting state is shown.
10. If subscribeToNewsletter returns success: true, the page redirects the user to newsletter_thank_you.html.
11. If subscribeToNewsletter returns success: false or the request fails, an error banner is shown with the returned message if available, and the user can correct inputs and retry.

Workflow: Navigating back to the home page
1. User clicks the site logo in the header.
2. A navigation handler triggers a redirect to index.html.
3. No newsletter-related state is preserved across this navigation, except what might be represented in the URL if the user manually navigates back.

Workflow: Handling URL source parameter
1. User arrives from another page (e.g., via a footer or inline link) that appends a source query parameter to newsletter.html.
2. On initialization, the controller reads the source parameter from the URL.
3. If the source parameter matches a supported source value for newsletter subscriptions, it is stored in page state.
4. If the source parameter is missing or does not match supported values, a default value representing the newsletter page is stored instead.
5. On form submission, this stored source value is passed unchanged into subscribeToNewsletter.source.
interactions:
Interaction: Page initialization
- Trigger: newsletter.html is loaded.
- System actions: Parse URL query parameter source; call getNewsletterSignupConfig; update page state with config.interests, config.frequencies, and config.privacy_message; handle loading and error states.

Interaction: Email input change
- Trigger: User types or edits the email field.
- System actions: Update local form state for email; clear any email-related validation error; keep other fields unchanged.

Interaction: Interest checkbox toggle
- Trigger: User checks or unchecks an interest option.
- System actions: Update the array of selected interests in form state based on the checkbox value from config.interests; clear interest-related validation error if at least one interest is now selected.

Interaction: Frequency radio selection
- Trigger: User selects a frequency option.
- System actions: Set the selected frequency in form state based on the chosen option’s value from config.frequencies; clear frequency-related validation errors.

Interaction: Form submission
- Trigger: User clicks the submit button or presses Enter while focused in the form.
- System actions: Run client-side validation for email, interests, and frequency; if validation fails, display inline errors and prevent API call; if validation passes, construct an API payload using current form state and stored source; call subscribeToNewsletter; while pending, disable the form submit button and optionally show a submitting indicator; on response, either redirect to newsletter_thank_you.html if success is true, or show an error banner if success is false or the call fails.

Interaction: Retry after configuration load failure
- Trigger: User clicks a 'Retry' control in the configuration error banner.
- System actions: Reinvoke getNewsletterSignupConfig; while pending, show loading state again; on success, re-render form fields; on failure, keep error visible.

Interaction: Logo click navigation
- Trigger: User clicks the site logo in the page header.
- System actions: Immediately navigate to index.html without attempting to submit or preserve form state.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary external state is derived from the URL parameter source. On page load, the initialization logic reads window.location.search and attempts to extract source. If source is present and matches a supported value for NewsletterSubscription.source (for example values such as those used to distinguish footer forms, inline home buttons, or direct newsletter page visits), it is stored in an internal variable (e.g., pageState.source). If source is missing or does not match any supported value, pageState.source is set to a default value that represents the newsletter signup page itself. This stored source value is not displayed to the user but is passed directly into subscribeToNewsletter.source during submission, ensuring consistent tracking. No other cross-page state is stored in the URL; the email, interests, frequency, submission status, and configuration data are all kept in in-memory component state and are reset on a fresh page load. The URL is not modified during normal interactions. Navigation to newsletter_thank_you.html on a successful subscription does not add any additional query parameters as defined by the outgoing connection specification.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial page behavior: reads URL parameters, loads newsletter signup configuration via getNewsletterSignupConfig, and exposes the resulting data and loading/error states to child components.
Provides top-level navigation for the page, including a clickable logo that navigates back to index.html.

Renders the newsletter signup form fields (email input, interest checkboxes, frequency radio buttons) using configuration from getNewsletterSignupConfig, manages form state, and delegates submission to the submission handler.

Validates the newsletter signup form fields before submission, including checking that the email is non-empty and matches a basic email pattern, at least one interest is selected, and a frequency is chosen. Returns a validation result object with field-level error messages.

Handles calling subscribeToNewsletter with the email, selected interests, selected frequency, and derived source. Manages submission state (pending, success, error) and triggers navigation to newsletter_thank_you.html when the API indicates success.
Displays the privacy_message text returned by getNewsletterSignupConfig to reassure the user about how their email will be used and how often emails are typically sent.
Shows an error state if getNewsletterSignupConfig fails, preventing use of the form until configuration is available and offering a retry control.

Displays submission-level errors (such as failures returned by subscribeToNewsletter or network issues) that are not tied to individual fields, and allows the user to attempt the submission again after adjusting the form if needed.
Triggers form validation and submission when clicked, and reflects the submission state (enabled, disabled while submitting).

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered fixed-width layout, likely based on a 12-column grid within a max-width container (~960–1140px), with a two-column split in the main about section (image column ~35–40%, text column ~60–65%).
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a vertical, top-to-bottom structure. At the top, the existing site header remains centered and minimal. Below it, any global configuration error banner appears as a slim horizontal strip. After a generous vertical gap, the main newsletter section is presented inside a centered max-width container using a two-column layout: the left column houses the newsletter-privacy-message (and can carry a brief contextual description), while the right column houses the newsletter-signup-form, including the inline form error banner and the submit button at the bottom of the form.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a vertical, top-to-bottom structure. At the top, the existing site header remains centered and minimal. Below it, any global configuration error banner appears as a slim horizontal strip. After a generous vertical gap, the main newsletter section is presented inside a centered max-width container using a two-column layout: the left column houses the newsletter-privacy-message (and can carry a brief contextual description), while the right column houses the newsletter-signup-form, including the inline form error banner and the submit button at the bottom of the form.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The site header occupies roughly the top quarter of the initial viewport, dominated by whitespace around the artist name and navigation. The main content container is constrained to ~960–1140px max-width and centered with wide side margins, reinforcing the gallery feel. Within the main content, the left column (privacy/context) uses about 35–40% of the container width, and the right column (form) uses about 60–65%. Vertical spacing between the header (and any config banner) and the main newsletter section is generous, around 48–64px, and internal spacing between form elements follows the 8px base scale (16–24px gaps).
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density remains low to moderate. The header and banners are slim but surrounded by comfortable whitespace. The two-column main section is spacious: the privacy text is in a relatively short, airy column with ample line height, and the form fields are vertically separated so that the form reads like a clean editorial column rather than a crowded UI. No component is edge-to-edge; all text and controls sit within padded areas, leaving a light, uncluttered impression.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first lands on the centered site header at the top, then moves down to any visible configuration error banner. After that, the gaze drops into the main newsletter section, encountering the left-column privacy/context text first, similar to the portrait column in the mockup. The attention then shifts right to the more interactive form column. Within the form, users read from top to bottom (email, interests, frequency), with the submit button acting as a strong focal point at the bottom. Any form-specific error banner appears at the top of the form column and briefly pulls focus before the user continues down through the fields.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a clean, centered composition with plenty of breathing room. At the top, the existing portfolio header—with the artist’s name and slim navigation—sits in the center of the page, surrounded by generous whitespace, occupying about the top quarter of the first viewport. Just below, a single-line configuration error banner, when present, spans the width of the content area as a soft, tinted strip, then gives way to a large vertical gap before the main newsletter area begins. The main section is a balanced two-column layout inside a max-width, gallery-like container. On the left, a narrow column holds a brief explanatory note and the privacy message, set in thin, readable type with ample line spacing. On the right, a wider column holds the newsletter form, with fields stacked vertically and separated by comfortable gaps. If there are form errors, a subtle form error banner appears at the top of this column. At the bottom-right of the form, a clearly accented submit button anchors the interaction and provides a visual destination to the vertical reading path. The overall effect is calm, editorial, and minimal, letting the content and invitation to subscribe feel deliberate and unforced.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This controller component is non-visual and does not occupy any physical space on the page. It operates behind the scenes, initializing the newsletter signup page before any visible elements are rendered. It has no width, height, or position in the layout; it simply ensures that, by the time the site-header and main content are displayed, all required configuration and state are ready.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual background logic; no spatial footprint in the rendered layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The site header stretches horizontally across the top of the viewport but is visually centered within a max-width container of about 960–1140px. The artist’s name appears at the top center of this container, with the navigation links directly below it, also centered. There is generous whitespace above the name (around 48–64px) and below the navigation (another 32–48px) to maintain the airy, gallery-like feel. Overall, the header area (from the top of the page down to the bottom edge of the navigation) occupies roughly the top 25–30% of the initial viewport height, with wide left and right margins framing the content. All elements are aligned in a single vertical column, reinforcing the minimalist, editorial style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block at the top of the page within normal document flow; not sticky or fixed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When present, the configuration error banner appears directly beneath the site-header, still within the same centered max-width container. It spans the full width of that container, forming a slim horizontal strip of text that runs from the left content edge to the right content edge. There is a modest gap of about 16–24px between the bottom of the navigation in the site-header and the top of this banner, so it feels connected to but distinct from the header. The banner itself has 12–16px of vertical padding and a subtle tinted background to differentiate it from the white page, while maintaining the light aesthetic. Below the banner, a larger vertical gap of around 48px separates it from the start of the main newsletter two-column section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width bar within the content container, pushed down by the header and pushing the main content down; not fixed or floating.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter signup form occupies the right column of the main content section. This main section sits below the header (and any config banner) with a generous gap of approximately 48–64px, and is wrapped in a centered max-width container (around 960–1140px wide). Within this container, the form column spans roughly 60–65% of the content width and is aligned to the right side of the container, leaving the remaining 35–40% for the left column. The top of the form aligns roughly with the top of the privacy text in the left column, creating a clean, horizontal visual line. Inside the form column, text labels and inputs are arranged in a single vertical stack, left-aligned, with each field group separated by 16–24px of vertical spacing. The content width of the form elements is slightly inset from the column edges (for example, by 16–24px of internal padding), so inputs do not touch the column boundaries, preserving the light, editorial feeling. At the very top of the form column, room is reserved for the newsletter-form-error-banner when it appears; otherwise, the first field or form heading sits close to that top edge. At the bottom of this column, the newsletter-submit-button is positioned, visually anchoring the form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block element within the right column of the main two-column layout, flowing vertically with the rest of the page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The form validator operates entirely behind the scenes and has no direct visual representation. It attaches logically to the newsletter-signup-form, validating inputs as the user interacts with the fields or attempts to submit. It does not occupy any width or height and does not alter layout spacing directly; instead, it triggers state changes that can show or hide the newsletter-form-error-banner and field-level validation states.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual background logic associated with the newsletter-signup-form; no spatial behavior of its own.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submission handler is a non-visual component that processes the form data when the user activates the newsletter-submit-button. It lives conceptually behind the form and does not appear on the screen, so it creates no visible shape, margin, or padding in the layout. Its presence is only perceived indirectly through loading states or success/error feedback presented by other UI elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual API/action layer; no physical space in the layout and no direct spatial behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter privacy message sits in the left column of the main content section, acting as a textual counterpart to the portrait image in the original mockup. This left column begins at the same vertical position as the top of the newsletter-signup-form on the right, creating a balanced two-column header line. The privacy message column occupies about 35–40% of the content container width and is aligned to the left of the centered container. The text is set in a narrow, vertically oriented block, with ample line height and a comfortable margin of 16–24px between the text and the column edges. There is a modest gap (around 16–24px) between any brief introductory heading/paragraph and the privacy details themselves, so the block reads as a small editorial sidebar clarifying what subscribers can expect. The bottom of the privacy block may end higher or lower than the bottom of the form, depending on text length, but the primary alignment is at the top, echoing how the portrait aligned with the bio text in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block element occupying the left column of the two-column layout, aligned with the top of the form and flowing vertically with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The form error banner sits inside the right form column, directly above the form fields. When visible, it spans nearly the full width of the form column, maintaining small insets (around 16px) from the column edges to match the internal padding of the form. It is a relatively slim horizontal block with 8–12px vertical padding and a subtle tinted background to distinguish it from the white page while staying visually light. The banner is separated from the elements above (such as the column’s top or any form heading) by about 8–16px, and from the first form field below by roughly 16–24px, so it is clearly connected to the form but does not feel cramped. Without errors, this space collapses and the fields move up, preserving the clean vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, context-local bar anchored to the top of the form column; it appears and disappears without affecting the placement of other major sections, only shifting the internal vertical spacing of the form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submit button is placed at the bottom of the newsletter-signup-form in the right column, acting as the main interactive focal point for the page. It is horizontally aligned with the left edge of the form fields but slightly emphasized within its local space—either right-aligned within the form field width or placed on its own line with ample whitespace around it. There is a vertical gap of about 24–32px between the last input field (e.g., frequency selection) and the button, allowing the button to stand out as a separate call to action. Below the button, additional whitespace of 32–48px separates it from any subsequent content or page footer, giving it a strong, anchored presence at the end of the user’s reading path. The button itself does not span the entire column; instead, it occupies a compact width (for example, 25–35% of the column width) to maintain a refined, minimalist look, with sufficient padding inside the button to keep it legible and tactile.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline-block element within the form’s vertical flow, anchored at the lower end of the form’s content stack.

name: Newsletter Thank You
title: Newsletter Subscription Confirmation
description: Page that confirms a successful newsletter subscription, exposes the lookbook download action, and offers navigation back into the portfolio site.

core_features: Load and display dynamic thank-you content (headline and body) using the getNewsletterThankYouContent interface.
Conditionally show a lookbook download action when lookbook_available is true, using label text from lookbook_label.
Trigger the downloadLookbook interface with a year value derived from URL parameters, then initiate the file download from the returned DocumentAsset.file_url.
Reflect downloadLookbook success or failure to the user via success/error states, without storing data in localStorage.
Provide navigation links back to the gallery, shop (with categoryId URL parameter), and home pages using standard anchor navigation.
user_workflows: Successful subscription → confirmation page load: 1) User submits the newsletter form on newsletter.html. 2) Backend validates and stores a NewsletterSubscription record and redirects the user to newsletter_thank_you.html with URL parameters such as status=success and year=<lookbook-year>. 3) On page load, the page-init logic reads URL parameters and calls getNewsletterThankYouContent. 4) When the response arrives, the page renders the headline and body from the response alongside any contextual text controlled by the interface.

Download lookbook via button click: 1) After the content is loaded, the user sees a download action if lookbook_available is true. 2) The user clicks the download control. 3) The event handler reads the year value from the URL (e.g., ?year=<lookbook-year>). 4) The handler calls downloadLookbook with that year. 5) If success is true and document is present, the page initiates a file download or navigates the browser to document.file_url. 6) Any success message in the response is reflected in a notification state. 7) The backend can update the associated NewsletterSubscription.lookbook_2024_downloaded field via the interface side-effect.

Automatic lookbook download on arrival (optional): 1) The subscription completion redirect may include an autoDownload parameter (e.g., autoDownload=1) alongside year. 2) After getNewsletterThankYouContent resolves successfully and if lookbook_available is true, the page-init logic checks autoDownload. 3) If enabled, it automatically triggers the same downloadLookbook flow as the manual button: sending year from the URL, handling success/failure, and starting the download from document.file_url.

Navigate to gallery after subscribing: 1) From the confirmation page, the user selects the link to explore artwork. 2) Clicking the gallery link navigates directly to gallery.html (no additional parameters required for this page). 3) The gallery page independently handles its own state based on its URL parameters, unrelated to this page.

Navigate to shop (prints) after subscribing: 1) From the confirmation page, the user chooses the link to view prints. 2) Clicking this link navigates to shop.html with the URL parameter categoryId=prints. 3) The shop page reads categoryId from the URL and initializes with the prints filter active.

Return home from confirmation: 1) The user clicks either the dedicated home link or the site logo (both configured as anchors). 2) The browser navigates to index.html with no additional parameters required by this page. 3) Any further personalization is handled by the home page, not this confirmation page.
interactions: Page load (no user action): On initial load, a page-init component reads URLSearchParams (e.g., status, year, autoDownload). It immediately calls getNewsletterThankYouContent. While loading, a loading state is shown. If the call fails, an error state is set and displayed by an error/notification component.

Display thank-you content: Once getNewsletterThankYouContent resolves successfully, a content component binds to the returned headline and body properties and renders them. The component only displays text provided by the interface, without hardcoded fallback copy.

Conditional rendering of lookbook action: A lookbook-download section component reads lookbook_available and lookbook_label from the content state. If lookbook_available is true, it renders an actionable control using lookbook_label as its label. If false, the download control is hidden or disabled and no download action is possible.

Click lookbook download control: When the user clicks the download control, an onClick handler: 1) Reads the year value from the URL (e.g., from ?year=<value>). 2) Sets a local downloading state (e.g., to disable the button). 3) Calls downloadLookbook with the derived year. 4) On response, if success is true and a document is returned, the handler directs the browser to document.file_url to start the download (e.g., via window.location or opening in a new tab). 5) If success is false, it sets an error state that is displayed via a notification component.

Automatic download if requested: After the initial content is loaded, if a URL parameter (e.g., autoDownload=1) is present, the page automatically invokes the same downloadLookbook handler flow as described above, without waiting for user interaction. The downloading state prevents double-triggering if the user also clicks.
Navigation to gallery: The user clicks a link element configured with href="gallery.html". No interface is called. Browser navigation takes the user away from this page.
Navigation to shop (prints): The user clicks a link with href="shop.html?categoryId=prints". No interface is called from this page; the next page uses the categoryId URL parameter for its own logic.
Navigation to home: The user clicks a link or the logo with href="index.html". No interface is called; a full navigation occurs.

Error handling interaction: If getNewsletterThankYouContent or downloadLookbook returns an error (e.g., success false or network failure), an error state message is displayed via a notification component. The user may dismiss the notification (e.g., via a close icon), which clears the error state in memory but does not affect any backend data.
page_functionality.state_logic: URL parameters and page state are used as follows: 1) status: A query parameter such as status=success is read on load. If status indicates a successful subscription, the page behaves normally. If status is missing or indicates an error, the page-init component can switch to an error-focused state (e.g., hiding the download section and showing a generic problem message sourced from state). This status only affects local rendering; no interface behavior changes. 2) year: A query parameter year=<number> provides the year value used when calling downloadLookbook. The lookbook-download component must not hardcode the year; instead, it reads it from URLSearchParams. If no year is present, the component can treat the lookbook as unavailable for download (e.g., disable or hide the download control) and may set an internal error state. 3) autoDownload: A query parameter autoDownload=1 (or any truthy value convention chosen by the implementation) indicates that the page should automatically initiate the lookbook download after getNewsletterThankYouContent has finished loading and if lookbook_available is true. If autoDownload is absent or false, the user must manually click the download control. 4) source: A query parameter source can indicate where the user came from (e.g., newsletter_page, footer_form), but this page does not directly call any interface with it. It can be used only for conditional display of contextual text by the page, as long as that text is not hardcoded to conflict with API-provided content. 5) All page state (loading, error, content, download state) is kept in in-memory variables or framework state and is not persisted in localStorage, sessionStorage, or cookies. 6) Outgoing navigation passes state via URL: the link to the shop page appends categoryId=prints, while gallery and home links use their respective filenames without additional parameters from this page.

components.functionality: Initializes the Newsletter Thank You page by reading URL parameters, triggering the getNewsletterThankYouContent interface, and coordinating optional auto-download of the lookbook.
Displays the confirmation headline and descriptive body text returned by getNewsletterThankYouContent, only after content has been successfully loaded.
Shows the lookbook download control when lookbook_available is true, uses lookbook_label for the control text, and coordinates downloadLookbook calls using the year value derived from URL parameters.
Provides links back to key areas of the site after subscription: gallery.html, shop.html with categoryId=prints, and index.html for home.
Displays transient success or error messages related to loading page content or downloading the lookbook based on state set by the page-init-loader and lookbook-download-section components.
Shows or hides a loading indicator while getNewsletterThankYouContent or downloadLookbook are in progress, based on shared loading state flags.

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered fixed-width layout, likely based on a 12-column grid within a max-width container (~960–1140px), with a two-column split in the main about section (image column ~35–40%, text column ~60–65%).
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same vertical, centered, gallery-like arrangement as the mockup. Immediately under the existing site header (artist name + main navigation), a slim status notification can appear, then the main content forms a two-column layout: on the left, the lookbook download section acts as the strong visual anchor (equivalent to the portrait image); on the right, the thank-you content block provides the confirmation text and expectations (equivalent to the biography). Below this main block, a compact, centered navigation group offers paths back into the portfolio. The page-init loader and loading indicator sit above this structure as transient states rather than permanent layout elements.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same vertical, centered, gallery-like arrangement as the mockup. Immediately under the existing site header (artist name + main navigation), a slim status notification can appear, then the main content forms a two-column layout: on the left, the lookbook download section acts as the strong visual anchor (equivalent to the portrait image); on the right, the thank-you content block provides the confirmation text and expectations (equivalent to the biography). Below this main block, a compact, centered navigation group offers paths back into the portfolio. The page-init loader and loading indicator sit above this structure as transient states rather than permanent layout elements.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: All content sits inside a centered max-width container (~960–1140px), with generous left/right margins on large screens. Within the main content area, the two columns follow the observed ratio: the lookbook download section takes about 35–40% of the content width on the left, while the thank-you text occupies about 60–65% on the right. The upper header area (artist name + primary navigation, outside this spec) still visually occupies the top ~25–30% of the initial viewport; the notification banner, if present, appears just beneath it. The main two-column section begins after a generous vertical gap (48–64px) from the header area, and there is another 48–64px gap before the lower navigation links. Internal padding of 16–24px is used inside the card-like elements to maintain the light, editorial feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content remains low to moderately dense, mirroring the mockup. The thank-you text is set in a comfortably wide right column with ample line-height and paragraph spacing so it reads like an editorial note rather than marketing copy. The lookbook download section is concise: a single visual preview, a short description, and one primary call-to-action, all surrounded by whitespace and a subtle border. The navigation links after signup are minimal, with only a handful of links spaced out horizontally or in a loose cluster, ensuring that no area feels crowded.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first moves to the top center (site header, then any status banner), then drops into the main content zone. Within the two-column layout, the left-hand lookbook card is the initial focal point due to its image-like presence and bordered container, analogous to the portrait in the mockup. From there, the user’s gaze shifts rightward into the thank-you text for context, then naturally downwards through the copy toward the lower navigation links. When the lookbook download is in progress, the localized loading indicator near the download control creates a subtle secondary focal point without disrupting the vertical reading flow. The page-init loader temporarily replaces this flow with a simple, centered loading state before revealing the main composition.

layout.overall_description: Inside a centered, max-width canvas framed by generous side margins, the page opens with the existing portfolio header above and a slim, optional status banner just below it. After a tall band of whitespace, the main content appears as a calm two-column composition floating in the middle of the viewport: on the left, a vertically oriented card presents the newsletter lookbook download, framed by a light border and ample interior padding; on the right, aligned to the top of that card, a clean block of thank-you text explains the successful subscription and what to expect next. Both columns are spacious, with thin typography and comfortable line spacing, creating an editorial, gallery-like feel. Beneath this block, separated by another generous vertical gap, a small, centered row of navigation links offers quiet pathways back into the portfolio sections. Transient elements like the full-page init loader and the small inline loading indicator briefly overlay or sit within this structure but do not introduce any heavy chrome, preserving the minimalist, understated aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-init loader appears as a full-viewport overlay that temporarily sits above all other content when the page first loads. It covers the entire browser window edge-to-edge with a plain, light background matching the site’s canvas color, maintaining the minimalist aesthetic. At the exact center of the viewport, a small, thin-lined loading indicator or subtle wordmark is displayed, horizontally and vertically centered, with ample empty space around it to reinforce the calm, gallery-like feel. Once initialization is complete, this overlay fades out to reveal the underlying header, status banner, and the main two-column layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay covering 100% of viewport width and height; positioned above all components with a high z-index; appears only during initial page load and then is removed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The status notification banner sits at the very top of the main content area, directly beneath the existing site header (artist name and primary navigation). It spans the full width of the central content container (roughly 80–90% of the viewport width on large screens, constrained to ~960–1140px and centered), with modest vertical padding (8–12px) and thin typography. The banner is visually lightweight: a subtle background tint and a fine bottom border separate it from the whitespace below without feeling heavy. A vertical gap of about 24–32px follows the banner before the main two-column thank-you section begins, so the banner feels like a slim, discrete strip rather than fused with the main content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the normal document flow, anchored to the top of the main content region; when shown, it pushes the rest of the content downward. On dismissal, the layout closes the gap so the main content moves up smoothly.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The thank-you content block forms the right column of the primary two-column layout. It occupies approximately 60–65% of the central content width and is aligned to the right side of the max-width container, starting on the same vertical line as the top of the lookbook download card on the left. The block itself does not use a heavy card outline; instead, it is a clean text area with generous internal breathing room defined by margins rather than borders, giving it the feel of a well-set editorial column. At its top, a prominent but thin-weight heading (e.g., 'Thank you for subscribing') is left-aligned relative to this column, followed by one or two short paragraphs explaining the successful subscription and pointing out that the lookbook is available. Paragraphs are spaced with about 16–24px vertical separation, and the line length is tuned to roughly 60–70 characters to maintain a comfortable reading rhythm. On narrower screens, this block drops below the lookbook download section, becoming a full-width text column with balanced margins and the same airy vertical spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, participating in a responsive two-column layout on medium and large screens; stacks below the lookbook-download-section as a full-width block on smaller screens (e.g., below ~768px).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The lookbook download section occupies the left column of the main content area and serves as the visual anchor of the page, echoing the portrait image position in the mockup. It spans roughly 35–40% of the content width and is vertically aligned so its top edge matches the top of the thank-you content block on the right, creating a clean horizontal alignment. The section is presented as a tall, vertically oriented card centered within its column, with 16–24px of internal padding and a subtle, hairline border or shadow to lightly frame it against the white background. Inside, a lookbook cover image or abstract placeholder sits near the top, followed by a concise title, a brief one- or two-line description, and a primary download control (button or prominent link) styled as the key interactive element. The primary download control is positioned in the upper half of the card so that, when the user’s eye first lands on the card, the availability of the download is immediately clear. On small screens, this card becomes a full-width block and appears above the thank-you text, maintaining its framed appearance and vertical spacing (32–48px) from adjacent sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main flow, forming the left column of a responsive two-column layout on medium and large screens; expands to full width and stacks above the thank-you-content-block on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation links after signup sit below the main two-column block, separated from it by a generous vertical gap of about 48–64px to create a distinct lower section, similar in rhythm to the gap between header and about section in the mockup. The links are centered horizontally within the same max-width container, either as a single row of text-based links or a tight cluster of 2–4 items with ample horizontal spacing (24–32px) between them. They use the same thin, understated typography as the main navigation, with minimal or no icons, reinforcing the lightweight, editorial feel. This group occupies only a small vertical slice—roughly the bottom 15–20% of the initial viewport—so it feels like a quiet set of paths back into the portfolio rather than a heavy footer. Ample whitespace below (32–48px) ensures the page ends with a light, open edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, centered within the main content width; remains at the bottom of the scrollable content and does not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading indicator is a localized, minimal element that appears within the lookbook download section when a download or related async action is in progress. It is positioned directly adjacent to or beneath the primary download control—typically to its right or just below the button label—so users can clearly associate the state with the action they initiated. The indicator is small and unobtrusive, such as a thin spinner or a subtle 'Downloading…' label, and it does not alter the overall layout structure or spacing of the card; instead, it occupies a pre-reserved, small horizontal or vertical slot to avoid content shift. It inherits the same centered, airy context of the card, maintaining sufficient whitespace around it and never encroaching on the edges of the section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, appearing within the lookbook-download-section component without affecting the global layout; it does not fix or overlay but simply shows and hides in place near the download control.

name: Info & Policies
title: Info & Policies
description: Provides visitors with dynamically loaded information about shipping, returns, artwork care, privacy, and FAQs, and links them to related actions such as reviewing their cart, exploring commissions, or subscribing to policy updates.

core_features: Load and display shipping, returns, care instructions, and privacy content from the getInfoAndPoliciesContent interface.
Render a structured FAQ list with expandable answers using FAQ data from getInfoAndPoliciesContent.
Allow visitors to navigate between subsections (shipping, returns, care, privacy, FAQ) using URL parameters so that specific sections can be deep-linked.
Provide an FAQ keyword search/filter on the client side based on the FAQ questions and answers returned by getInfoAndPoliciesContent, with the search query stored in URL parameters.

Offer context-specific actions from the info sections: a "Review cart" action in shipping and returns, a link to commission details from relevant content, and a "Stay updated" CTA to the newsletter page with the correct source parameter.
Keep the open/closed state of individual FAQ items in sync with URL parameters so that a specific FAQ can be linked or restored on back/forward navigation.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Read shipping information and review cart
1. User navigates to info.html (or via footer link).
2. Page controller calls getInfoAndPoliciesContent and receives shipping_markdown, returns_markdown, care_instructions_markdown, privacy_markdown, and faqs.
3. Section navigation component reads the URL parameter section. If section=shipping (or missing and shipping is the default), it highlights the shipping tab and scrolls the shipping section into view.
4. Shipping section renders shipping_markdown as markdown content.
5. Within or directly under the shipping section, the user clicks the "Review cart" action.
6. The click navigates to cart.html (no extra parameters), leaving the current info.html URL intact in history for possible back navigation.

Workflow: Check return policy details and then go back to FAQs
1. User lands on info.html with ?section=returns.
2. Page controller parses section=returns and sets the active section before or after content load.
3. Returns section displays returns_markdown.
4. User scrolls down and decides to view FAQs, clicking the FAQ tab.
5. Section navigation updates the URL to info.html?section=faq (preserving any other params, such as search query) via history.pushState and activates the FAQ section.
6. FAQ list renders using faqs from getInfoAndPoliciesContent and shows all items expanded/collapsed according to the FAQ state parameters (if any).

Workflow: Find an answer via FAQ search and open a specific FAQ
1. From any section, user navigates to the FAQ section via the navigation tabs; URL becomes info.html?section=faq.
2. FAQ section is activated; FAQ search input reads any existing q parameter from the URL and pre-fills the search field.
3. User types a keyword into the search input and submits (or triggers live search).
4. FAQ search updates the q parameter in the URL (e.g., info.html?section=faq&q=term) using history.pushState.
5. The FAQ list is filtered client-side using the q value, matching against question and answer_markdown.
6. User clicks a specific FAQ question; the component toggles its open state and updates the URL with faqIndex (or similar) to indicate which FAQ is expanded (e.g., info.html?section=faq&q=term&faqIndex=2).
7. If the user copies the URL or uses back/forward navigation, the same FAQ search and open item are restored based on q and faqIndex parameters.

Workflow: Learn about custom work and jump to the commissions page
1. User browses care or FAQ content that references custom work or commissions.
2. A link or button labeled for custom work is rendered (either as part of markdown content if the backend inserts a link, or as a dedicated CTA block below the relevant section).
3. When clicked, the navigation logic takes the user to commissions.html (no additional parameters from this page).
4. User can then submit a commission request using the dedicated Commissions page.

Workflow: Stay informed about policy or shop updates
1. User is reading any info section (e.g., shipping) and notices a "Stay updated" CTA near or below the policy content.
2. User clicks the CTA.
3. Page navigates to newsletter.html with the URL parameter source set to other, e.g., newsletter.html?source=other.
4. Newsletter page can use this parameter to attribute the signup source to the Info & Policies page.
interactions:
Interaction: Section navigation
- User clicks a navigation tab or in-page link for a section (shipping, returns, care, privacy, FAQ).
- Event handler updates the URL parameter section to the corresponding value via history.pushState without reloading the page.
- Active section state is updated and scrolled into view.
- On browser back/forward, a popstate listener re-reads section from the URL and updates the active section accordingly.

Interaction: FAQ search
- User types in the FAQ search input.
- On submit or debounced change, the search term is written to the q URL parameter.
- The FAQ list reads the current value of q and filters the faqs array client-side, matching against question and answer_markdown.
- Clearing the search field removes q from the URL and restores the full FAQ list.

Interaction: FAQ expand/collapse
- User clicks on an FAQ question header.
- If the item is currently collapsed, component expands its answer_markdown, and updates a URL parameter (e.g., faqIndex) with the index of the opened FAQ or a serialized list of open indices.
- If the item is expanded, it is collapsed and the URL parameter is updated to remove or adjust the index.
- On page load and on popstate, the component reads faqIndex from the URL and sets the corresponding FAQ item(s) open.

Interaction: "Review cart" action
- Inside shipping and returns sections, a dedicated "Review cart" action button is rendered.
- When clicked, it navigates directly to cart.html using a standard link or location change.
- No additional interfaces are called from this page for cart operations.

Interaction: Commissions link
- A link or button related to custom work is shown in or near relevant content (e.g., within FAQs or care instructions as defined by the content).
- Clicking the link navigates to commissions.html using a standard link.
- URL parameters are not required for this navigation from this page.

Interaction: "Stay updated" CTA
- A CTA element related to updates or policy changes is displayed in or below the main information sections.
- Clicking it navigates to newsletter.html with the URL parameter source=other, e.g., newsletter.html?source=other.
- The navigation is done by constructing the URL from the base path and the source parameter.

Interaction: Internal anchor navigation within a section
- If the markdown from getInfoAndPoliciesContent contains heading anchors (e.g., generated by the markdown renderer), clicking those anchors updates the hash portion of the URL (e.g., #international-shipping) without altering the section parameter.
- On hash change, the browser scrolls to the corresponding anchor; this does not trigger new API calls.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page relies solely on URL parameters and internal component state synchronized with those parameters. No localStorage or external state persistence is used.

URL parameters used on this page:
- section: Controls which main subsection is active. Expected values: 'shipping', 'returns', 'care', 'privacy', 'faq'. If missing or invalid, the page controller selects a default section based on available content from getInfoAndPoliciesContent (for example, choosing the first non-empty content block) and may update the URL to include this default. Changing section via UI updates this parameter with history.pushState. On page load and on popstate, the controller reads section to set the active section.

- q: Optional FAQ search query. Only used when the FAQ section is active. The FAQ search component reads q on initialization and populates the search field with it. Any changes to the query by the user rewrite q in the URL (adding, updating, or removing it). The FAQ list filters the faqs array using q. If q is removed, full FAQ list is shown. Back/forward navigation restores previous q values and refilters FAQs.

- faqIndex (or a similar parameter): Represents which FAQ item is expanded by default. The value is interpreted as an index into the faqs array returned by getInfoAndPoliciesContent (or a serialized list of indices, such as a comma-separated string, if multiple open items are desired). On load, FAQ component reads faqIndex, validates it against the available faqs length, and sets matching item(s) as open. When user toggles an FAQ, the component updates faqIndex accordingly using history.pushState. If faqIndex is not present, no item is forced open beyond the component's default behavior.

Hash fragment (optional, not a query parameter): If the markdown content includes anchors, the user may navigate within long sections using hashes (e.g., info.html?section=shipping#delivery-times). The hash is handled natively by the browser and does not cause new API calls or affect section, q, or faqIndex.

All other state, such as whether content is loading or errors from getInfoAndPoliciesContent, is held in volatile component state only and is not persisted beyond the page session.

components.functionality: Initializes the Info & Policies page, calls getInfoAndPoliciesContent, parses URL parameters, manages high-level loading/error state, and passes the loaded content plus normalized URL state to child components.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Invoke getInfoAndPoliciesContent, then compute default section if URLSearchParams.section is missing or invalid, and update browser URL if needed.
onPopState: Re-read URLSearchParams.section, URLSearchParams.q, and URLSearchParams.faqIndex, and propagate to child components.
onSectionChange: Called by section navigation; updates URLSearchParams.section via history.pushState without reloading, then scrolls to the corresponding section.
onFaqSearchChange: Updates URLSearchParams.q via history.pushState and triggers FAQ filtering.
onFaqToggle: Updates URLSearchParams.faqIndex (or equivalent) via history.pushState based on which FAQ item index is open.

components.functionality: Provides a set of navigation controls (e.g., tabs or a simple list) for switching between the main subsections: shipping, returns, care, privacy, and FAQ. Keeps the active tab in sync with the section URL parameter.

Displays the shipping information using shipping_markdown. Shows a context-specific "Review cart" action button that navigates to the cart page. Can host additional inline CTAs such as "Stay updated" if required by the UX.
event_handlers: onSectionVisible: Triggered when URLSearchParams.section === 'shipping'; scrolls this section into view if necessary.
onClickReviewCart: Navigate to 'cart.html' using a standard link or programmatic redirect.

components.functionality: Displays the returns and cancellations information using returns_markdown. Includes a "Review cart" action and may contain or be followed by a link to commissions if relevant reasons for returns refer to custom work.
event_handlers: onSectionVisible: Triggered when URLSearchParams.section === 'returns'; scrolls this section into view if necessary.

components.functionality: Displays artwork care instructions using care_instructions_markdown. May include or be followed by links to commissions.html if care instructions mention custom pieces, and to the newsletter CTA if relevant.
event_handlers: onSectionVisible: Triggered when URLSearchParams.section === 'care'; scrolls this section into view if necessary.

components.functionality: Displays privacy, cookies overview, and terms of use using privacy_markdown. Contains or is followed by a "Stay updated" CTA leading to newsletter.html for updates including privacy or policy changes.
event_handlers: onSectionVisible: Triggered when URLSearchParams.section === 'privacy'; scrolls this section into view if necessary.

components.functionality: Provides a keyword search input specifically for filtering FAQs. Syncs the search term with the q URL parameter so that FAQ filters are shareable and recoverable.
event_handlers: onInit: Read URLSearchParams.q and prefill the search input with the existing query value.
onChangeQuery: Update URLSearchParams.q in the URL using history.pushState; notify FAQ list to re-filter faqs.
onClearQuery: Remove q from URLSearchParams (rebuilding the URL without q) and reset FAQ list to show all items.

components.functionality: Renders the FAQ items from getInfoAndPoliciesContent.faqs, each with a clickable question and an expandable answer rendered as markdown. Filters items based on the q URL parameter and keeps the open item(s) synchronized with faqIndex.
event_handlers: onSectionVisible: When URLSearchParams.section === 'faq', ensure the FAQ list is visible and scroll into view if needed.
onFilterChange: Triggered when URLSearchParams.q changes; re-run client-side filtering of the faqs array based on the query matching question or answer_markdown.
onInitOpenState: On first render, read URLSearchParams.faqIndex and set the corresponding FAQ item(s) to open if the index is valid.
onClickQuestion: Toggle the open state of the clicked FAQ item. After computing the resulting set of open indices, call page-controller.onFaqToggle to update URLSearchParams.faqIndex accordingly.
onClickReviewCartFromFaq: If any FAQ answer includes a dedicated 'Review cart' action, navigate to 'cart.html'.
onClickCommissionsFromFaq: If FAQ content references custom work via a dedicated action element, navigate to 'commissions.html'.
onClickStayUpdatedFromFaq: Navigate to 'newsletter.html?source=other' when the FAQ-related CTA is clicked.

components.functionality: Reusable CTA component that can be embedded at the bottom of any section (shipping, returns, care, privacy, FAQ) to lead visitors to the newsletter signup page with the source parameter set to indicate that the origin was the Info & Policies page.
Reusable "Review cart" CTA component that can appear within the shipping section, returns section, and any FAQ answer where it makes sense for the visitor to jump to their cart.

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered fixed-width layout, likely based on a 12-column grid within a max-width container (~960–1140px), with a two-column split in the main about section (image column ~35–40%, text column ~60–65%).
layout.layout_pattern: The page mirrors the mockup’s vertical progression: a centered page-level controller region at the top (title/context handled by the page controller), followed immediately by a centered horizontal tab navigation. After a generous vertical gap, the main content switches into a two-column layout for desktop: on the left, an interactive FAQ area; on the right, the currently active policy text section. Within the right column, a subtle inline 'Review cart' CTA near the top echoes the CV download link placement from the mockup. Below the two-column region, a full-width, centered 'Stay updated' CTA closes the page. On mobile, this collapses to a single centered column with the same vertical order.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page mirrors the mockup’s vertical progression: a centered page-level controller region at the top (title/context handled by the page controller), followed immediately by a centered horizontal tab navigation. After a generous vertical gap, the main content switches into a two-column layout for desktop: on the left, an interactive FAQ area; on the right, the currently active policy text section. Within the right column, a subtle inline 'Review cart' CTA near the top echoes the CV download link placement from the mockup. Below the two-column region, a full-width, centered 'Stay updated' CTA closes the page. On mobile, this collapses to a single centered column with the same vertical order.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: A max-width container (~960–1140px) is centered on the page, with wide left and right margins to maintain a gallery-like feel. The top controller + tab navigation together occupy roughly the upper quarter of the scrollable viewport, mostly as whitespace punctuated by typography. The two-column body section uses about 90% of the container width: the left FAQ column at ~35–40% and the right policy content column at ~60–65%. Vertical spacing follows the 8px scale: 48–64px gaps between major sections (controller to tabs, tabs to content, content to final CTA), 24–32px between related elements inside sections.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density stays low to moderate. Each block (tabs, policy text, FAQ search, FAQ list, CTAs) is allowed to breathe with generous padding and line-height, similar to an editorial layout rather than a dense dashboard. Policy text is set in a comfortable line length within the right column; FAQ items are well separated for scanability. No component touches the container edges; each has 16–24px internal padding where applicable.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye starts at the center with the page’s top region (handled by the page controller), then moves directly down to the centered tab navigation. From there, the gaze drops into the two-column area, first encountering the left FAQ column as a distinct vertical block, then moving right to the larger policy text column. Within the right column, the inline 'Review cart' CTA near the top becomes a local focal point, echoing the blue download link from the mockup. From there, the reading pattern is primarily vertical down the policy text and FAQs (F-pattern), finishing at the bottom-centered 'Stay updated' CTA.

layout.overall_description: All content sits inside a centered, fixed-width container that feels like an airy editorial spread. At the top of the page, the Info & Policies context (handled by the page controller) is aligned centrally, surrounded by ample whitespace. Directly beneath it, a slim horizontal tab bar spans a comfortable width in the center, allowing users to switch between Shipping, Returns, Care, Privacy, and FAQ-focused content.
